entries.journal_entry:
Had a meeting with Dr. Villanueva about the dissertation chapter on extractive policy in Peru. For once he actually engaged with the argument instead of just redirecting me toward his framework. He pushed back on my case selection, which was fair, but he did it by asking questions rather than just telling me to change it. That felt different. I left his office thinking clearly for the first time in weeks.

Walked to the mercado afterward and sat with a coffee going through my notes. The chapter outline is solid. The theoretical framing is mine -- not his, not borrowed from the lab's usual line. I've been circling this argument for months and it finally has shape. There's a version of this that could become a standalone article, which matters because I need publications that aren't just appendages to his projects.

I forget sometimes that I came here with a decade of professional experience. I wasn't some kid who wandered into a program. I chose this. I left a salary and a title because the work I was doing in Bogota hit a ceiling I couldn't break through without the credential. Days like today remind me the trade-off has a point.

Nudge: "That chapter outline is yours -- what usually pulls you away from it?"

Lab meeting today was three hours of performative agreement. Villanueva presented his new grant proposal and everyone nodded along like it was revelation. Half the methodology section is recycled from a paper we all know didn't replicate well. I sat there biting my tongue because the last time I raised a concern in front of the group he reframed it as me "not understanding the nuance yet." In front of the first-years. That was six months ago and I still feel it.

Marina, the other senior student, caught my eye during his presentation. She sees it too but she's smarter about it than I am -- she just stays quiet, publishes on the side, and keeps her head down. I can't do that. Something in me won't let me just sit there when the reasoning is sloppy. But speaking up costs more than staying silent, at least in this lab.

Came home and opened the Peru chapter again. The contrast is sharp. In Villanueva's lab I'm a student who should listen. In my own work I'm building something that could actually matter for how these countries manage their mining conflicts. The gap between those two versions of me is getting harder to hold.

Nudge: "What does Marina's approach cost her that yours doesn't?"

Got an email from a former colleague in Bogota asking if I'd co-author a policy brief on lithium regulation for a think tank. Good money, my name on it, and the topic overlaps with my dissertation. I said yes immediately. Then spent the rest of the afternoon wondering if I should have checked with Villanueva first. The program technically doesn't require it, but there's an unspoken expectation that outside work gets cleared through him.

The thing that bothers me is the hesitation itself. I spent eight years writing policy documents without asking anyone's permission. Now I'm second-guessing a decision I'm fully qualified to make because someone with a title might disapprove. I don't know when I started treating his opinion as a gate I have to pass through, but I notice it more and more.

Same day, later. Called my mother in Cali to tell her about the policy brief. She was happy, asked how much it paid, then reminded me that my cousin Luis just got promoted to director at the ministry. She doesn't mean it as a comparison but it lands like one. Luis is 33, has a corner office, and never left the country. I'm 38, sharing a desk in a research lab.

But then she said something that settled me: "You always needed to understand why things work, not just that they work." She's right. That's why consulting felt hollow toward the end. I was writing recommendations I didn't fully believe in because the analysis was never deep enough. The PhD is slow and humbling but at least I'm doing the actual thinking now.

Nudge: "Is the thinking deeper now, or just differently constrained?"

Response: Different constraints, sure. But at least I chose these ones.

Villanueva told me today that he wants to be listed as co-author on the Peru chapter if I submit it to a journal. His reasoning is that the "intellectual environment of the lab" contributed to the work. I pushed back. Told him the chapter uses a different framework than anything the lab has produced, that the fieldwork contacts are mine from my Bogota years, that the data collection was funded by my own assistantship hours. He got quiet in that way he does -- not angry, just recalibrating.

He said we'd "revisit it later," which means he expects me to come around. I won't. This is the one piece of work in this entire program that is entirely mine. Every argument, every source, every late night rewriting the methodology section because his suggestions didn't fit. I'm not handing that over.

I know how this looks from outside. Grad student refusing to share credit with advisor. But this isn't about generosity. If I publish this with his name on it, it becomes part of his portfolio, his grant narrative, his legacy. My contribution disappears into his. I've watched it happen to Marina three times already. That's not going to be me.

Nudge: "What happens if he doesn't let it go?"

Response: I'll figure that out when it comes. Right now the chapter is mine.

Can't focus. Keep replaying the conversation with Villanueva. The way he leaned back in his chair. The pause before he said "revisit." I know that move because I used to do it in meetings in Bogota -- the deliberate silence that says I'm not done with this. He's not going to drop it. And the problem is he doesn't need to fight me directly. He just needs to delay things. Slow down feedback on my other chapters. Forget to sign off on conference funding. The machinery of a program has a hundred small levers.

Started the policy brief for the Bogota think tank. The writing came fast, like muscle memory. Two thousand words in three hours. It reminded me of what it feels like to work without someone standing over the output, deciding if it's good enough to carry their name.

Had lunch with Marina. She told me Villanueva asked her opinion about my "collaboration style." That's how he phrases it -- not that I'm difficult, just that my style needs adjustment. She told him I'm thorough. Diplomatic of her. We both know what he's doing. At least someone in that lab sees it clearly.

Nudge: "What would it change if you and Marina pushed back together?"

Later in the evening. Finished the first draft of the policy brief. Sent it to the think tank contact. Fourteen pages, clean argument, clear recommendations. Took me less than a week of evening work. The dissertation chapter, by contrast, has been in revision for four months because every piece of feedback from Villanueva sends me in a different direction.

Thinking about what Marina said at lunch, about his use of "collaboration style." It's a framing that puts the problem on me, on how I work, instead of on the fact that he's claiming credit for something he didn't build. Language like that is how people with institutional standing rewrite the narrative. I've seen it in government -- the person with the title gets to define the terms.

I need to decide soon whether to submit the Peru chapter independently or wait for whatever "revisit" means. Waiting gives him time to build his case or create friction. Submitting means the relationship might not recover. My mother would say "no pelees batallas que no puedes ganar." But I'm not sure this is a battle I can afford to lose either. The chapter is the best thing I've written. If it goes out with his name, the work is still good. But it won't be mine anymore.

Nudge: "Which loss would sit with you longer?"

entries.journal_entry:
Got an email from my cousin Faris today, forwarding photos from his daughter's first birthday party back in Amman. The whole family was there — uncles, aunts, my mother holding the baby with that particular look she gets. I was not invited to skip it; I just was not there. No one said anything pointed but the group chat went quiet for a while after the photos dropped and I could feel the space where my name should have been.

Nothing happened, really. I ate leftover lentil soup, re-read a paper on morphological parsing, tried to focus. My flatmate Callum asked if I wanted to go to a pub quiz and I said no, then sat there wishing I had said yes.

Nudge: "What was the quiet in the group chat about?"

Response: It is not that they are angry. It is that they have stopped expecting me to show up. That is worse, I think. Like I have been reclassified from "absent" to "gone."

Supervisor meeting. Dr. Keane asked me to commit to one research question by next Friday and I heard myself say yes, absolutely, no problem. Walked home and could not stop thinking about the three other directions I had sketched out last month.

Nudge: "What stopped you from pushing back in the meeting?"

Response: Because pushing back feels like making a scene, and I do not make scenes. My father never raised his voice in a meeting in his life. But I keep wondering if the question I pick will be the one that bores me by October.

Library until six. Drafted the introduction for the narrowed thesis proposal, which took longer than it should have because I kept second-guessing paragraph transitions. Callum and I walked to the chippie on Clerk Street after.

Friday call with my mother went well for once. She asked about the thesis and I described the morphological analysis angle in simple terms — how computers learn to break Arabic words into their root pieces. She said it sounded useful, which from her is high praise.

I felt like myself on that call. Not performing, not deflecting her questions about when I am coming home. Just explaining something I actually care about to someone who wanted to listen. She even asked a follow-up about how the root system works differently in dialects. Small thing, but it settled something in me.

Second entry today because my brain will not shut off. After the call with my mother I went for a walk up Arthur's Seat. Halfway up I got this rush of clarity — or maybe it was just adrenaline — where I started thinking about what it would look like to pivot my thesis entirely. Not the careful narrowing Dr. Keane wants. Something bigger. Cross-linguistic transfer models for under-resourced dialects. Levantine, Gulf, Maghrebi. My heart was actually racing on the hill and it was not from the climb.

Then I sat on the bench at the top and the wind hit me and I thought about what pivoting would mean. Another meeting with Keane where I contradict what I said three days ago. My mother asking again when I will finish. My uncle Hisham, who helped fund the first year, shaking his head at another delay. Everyone back home already thinks this PhD was an impulsive detour, and changing direction again would prove them right.

So I walked back down, made tea, opened the narrowed proposal draft. Still sitting here with it. The cursor is blinking at the end of paragraph three and I have not typed anything in forty minutes.

Nudge: "What would happen if you brought the bigger idea to Keane?"

Department social last night at The Pear Tree. I went because Dr. Keane mentioned it would be good to show face, and I know what that means in academic circles — it means attendance is optional in the way that prayer is optional at a family gathering. So I went.

Ended up in a long conversation with Priya from the NLP group. She is working on code-switching detection in Hindi-English tweets and the way she talked about it — with this specific, focused energy — made me jealous. Not of the topic. Of the certainty. She has her question, her method, her timeline. She did not seem to need a detour.

I told her about the dialect transfer idea. She said it sounded fascinating and asked why I was not doing it. I said timing, funding, the usual. She shrugged and said "those are real constraints" in a way that felt like she was politely letting me off the hook. I appreciated it and hated it at the same time.

Nudge: "What did you want her to say instead?"

Response: Something like "do it anyway." Or "those are excuses." Someone outside the family orbit giving me permission to be reckless. Which is ridiculous because I am thirty-eight years old and should not need permission from a colleague I barely know.

Submitted the narrowed thesis proposal to Dr. Keane this morning. Forty-two pages, bibliography cleaned up, research questions scoped to morphological segmentation for Modern Standard Arabic. Felt like handing in a homework assignment I had done properly rather than one that kept me up at night, but there is something to be said for finishing.

Callum and I cooked mansaf tonight — or my version of it, which involves Greek yogurt because Edinburgh does not have decent jameed. He kept asking questions about the spices and I caught myself explaining things the way my mother does, with small corrections and zero patience. Made me laugh.

After dinner I sat with my laptop and, almost without thinking about it, opened a blank document and started outlining the dialect transfer idea. Not as a thesis. Just notes. No one has to see it. I wrote for an hour and it was the most alive I have felt in front of a screen in weeks.

My uncle Hisham called today. Not the usual Friday family call — a separate, pointed call. He asked how the PhD was going and I told him I submitted the proposal. He said good, asked about the timeline, and then said he hoped I was not "chasing new rabbits" — his phrase, not mine. Apparently my mother mentioned something about dialect research and he connected dots.

I told him everything was on track, the proposal was submitted, Dr. Keane was happy. All true. I did not mention the outline document from last night. It is not lying. It is managing information. People share what is relevant and I shared what was relevant.

Went to the Edinburgh Farmers' Market at Castle Terrace this morning, mostly for the za'atar bread from the Lebanese stall. Ran into Priya and her partner. We talked about the summer conference circuit and she mentioned a workshop on low-resource NLP in Copenhagen in September. She looked at me when she said it, like she knew.

I came home and emailed Dr. Keane asking for a meeting next week. Just to discuss, I wrote. I did not say what about. But I printed out the dialect transfer outline and put it in my bag. Maybe I will bring it, maybe I will not. It is in the bag though. That feels like enough for today.

Nudge: "Why print it out if you might not show it?"

Response: Because I need to know it is there. Like a door I have not walked through yet but I can see. If I leave it at home it is just a file on my laptop. In the bag it is something I chose to carry. I think I am going to show it. I think I have been working up to this for weeks and I just did not want to admit it because admitting it means it matters, and if it matters then Keane saying no will actually hurt.

entries.journal_entry:
Started doing the math again tonight. If I keep the delivery app running five days a week and pick up at least two handyman jobs on the weekends, I can cover everything with about twelve thousand pesos left over. That leftover is supposed to be for the motorcycle insurance renewal in May. I wrote it on a sticky note and put it on the fridge next to the old one that says "DO NOT touch savings."

Funny thing is, I spent Saturday at the parrilla with Cacho and Pocho like always, and the bill came out heavier than usual because we ordered that tira de asado. I didn't blink. Six hours of good food and good talk, what am I going to do, skip it? Some weeks the numbers work, some weeks they don't. But I'm not going to stop living just because the spreadsheet says I should.

My sister Graciela keeps calling to tell me I should go back to a regular job. She doesn't get it. I'm not going to sit in some warehouse again answering to a guy ten years younger than me who treats people like furniture.

Nudge: "What is it about the idea of going back that feels impossible?"

Response: It's not impossible, I just don't want to. There's a difference. Look, I know what I'm doing. I've been managing fine for five years this way. Graciela worries because she's Graciela. She married an accountant, everything in her life has a line item. Not everyone lives like that.

Bad week. The delivery app lowered the per-trip rate again, no warning, no explanation. Just opened the app Monday morning and the numbers were different. I did the same twelve deliveries I always do on a weekday and came home with almost twenty percent less. Twenty percent. That's not nothing. That's a week of groceries, that's Negrita's vet visit I've been putting off.

The handyman side is slower too because people are tightening up. I had three jobs lined up this week and two of them canceled last minute — one lady said she'd "wait until next month" to fix her bathroom faucet and the other one just stopped responding. So now I'm sitting here Thursday night with the calculator app open, moving numbers around like I'm playing some kind of sad puzzle game, trying to figure out where the motorcycle payment comes from.

The thing that really gets to me is the feeling. It's not just about the money, it's the not knowing. Every month is a guess. Every week I wake up and I don't know what I'm going to make. I used to think that was the trade-off for not having a boss breathing down my neck. Lately I'm not so sure the trade-off is worth it. But what am I supposed to do, crawl back and prove Graciela right? No. I'll figure it out. I always figure it out.

Nudge: "You said you're not sure the trade-off is worth it anymore. What would it look like if you really sat with that thought?"

Got a call from Don Alfredo across the street — he needs his whole kitchen faucet replaced plus some tile work. That's a good-sized job, probably two days of work. Felt like I could breathe again for the first time all week.

Motorcycle started making a noise yesterday. A grinding thing when I brake. I took it to Raul's shop and he looked at it and said the brake pads are gone and the disc is warped, it needs replacing. He quoted me a number that wiped out everything I'd saved from the Alfredo job. Every peso.

I stood there in Raul's shop and felt my stomach just drop. Because it's not optional — the motorcycle is how I make deliveries. Without it I'm on foot or taking the subte, which means fewer trips, which means less money, which means next month's rent gets tight. It's like this chain where one thing breaks and everything behind it starts wobbling. I told Raul to do it, because what choice do I have. But I walked home and I couldn't even eat dinner.

Nudge: "When you walked home, what was going through your head?"

Response: Honestly, I was thinking about my old man. He worked at the same factory for thirty-two years. Hated it, but every month the check came in. He never had to wonder if the floor was going to fall out from under him. I used to think that was a sad way to live. Now I'm not so sure. He always had a little envelope in the dresser drawer with cash for emergencies. I don't have that envelope. I don't have anything between me and the next bad surprise. And that thought — that I'm one broken motorcycle or one bad month away from not being able to pay rent — that sat on my chest the whole walk home.

Couldn't sleep. Opened the app at 1am to see if there were any late-night delivery runs. There were two. I took them both, rode the bicycle I borrowed from my neighbor. Came home at 3am with barely enough to cover tomorrow's gas.

Picked up the motorcycle from Raul this morning. It rides clean now, brakes feel solid. That's something. But I'm running on maybe four hours of sleep from the last few days and I can feel it in my eyes, in the way I'm slow to react at intersections. Almost clipped a bus mirror on Corrientes.

Saturday is coming and Cacho already texted about the parrilla. Part of me wants to skip it this week, save the money. But another part of me — the louder part — says that's the one thing that keeps me sane. You take away the asado with friends and what do I have left? A cat, a calculator, and a leaking faucet in my own bathroom that I haven't even bothered to fix.

Nudge: "When you say the parrilla is the one thing that keeps you sane — what does it give you that nothing else does?"

Response: It's the only time during the week where I'm not counting anything. Not counting deliveries, not counting pesos, not counting hours. I sit down, the meat comes, somebody pours the wine, and for a few hours I'm just Roberto having a good time with his friends. Nobody's asking me to fix something or deliver something or be somewhere in fifteen minutes. It's the closest thing I have to feeling like my life is actually mine.

Don Alfredo recommended me to his daughter for some painting work at her new apartment. Three rooms, good rate, she even offered to pay half upfront. I bought Negrita the fancy wet food she likes to celebrate.

Graciela came over unannounced today while I was between deliveries. She brought empanadas, which was nice, except then she sat at my kitchen table and started doing that thing she does where she looks around the apartment cataloguing everything that's wrong. The peeling paint above the window, the chair with the broken leg, the fact that I still don't have a proper desk and eat on the couch.

Then she said — and I quote — "Roberto, you're almost fifty, you can't live like a college student." And I just stood there holding an empanada feeling like I was fourteen again getting lectured. She doesn't see that I actually like my life most of the time. I like picking my own hours. I like riding through Palermo on a Tuesday morning with the sun out and nobody telling me where to be. But she looks at this apartment and she sees failure. And the worst part is that sometimes, when she leaves, I start seeing it through her eyes too.

Nudge: "You said sometimes you see it through her eyes. What do you see when that happens?"

Response: A guy who's been treading water for five years and calling it swimming. Someone who left a stable situation because of pride and never figured out the next step. I see the broken chair and I think, why haven't I fixed that? I'm literally a handyman. And the answer is because when I come home I don't want to fix anything else, I just want to sit down and not move. And that scares me a little because my old man never left things broken. He always had the house perfect. I can't even keep up with my own apartment and I live alone with a cat.

Painting job at Don Alfredo's daughter's place is done. She was happy with it, paid the balance, even tipped me a little extra. So the money situation is stable for the moment. Rent is covered, motorcycle payment is covered, Raul is paid. There's a small cushion again, not much, but it's there.

Went to the parrilla Saturday like always. Pocho brought his nephew, a kid of about twenty-five who does the same delivery app I do but also drives for a rideshare service on the side. He was telling me about his "strategy" — how he works the surge hours, how he tracks which neighborhoods tip best, how he's saving for a down payment on his own place. The whole table was impressed. I just sat there eating my chorizo and feeling old.

It's not jealousy exactly. The kid has energy and optimism and a plan, and good for him. But I was that kid once. I had the energy to work sixteen-hour days and still go dancing with my ex-wife on Saturday night. Now I do eight hours of deliveries and my back locks up. I eat the asado and drink the wine and it's still good, it's always good. But there's this thin layer underneath the enjoyment now, like a film on old coffee. Like I'm enjoying it because I know this might not last. Like I should be saving what I'm spending here but I can't bring myself to stop because this is the part of my life that doesn't feel like survival.

Nudge: "You described a thin layer underneath the enjoyment. When did you first start noticing it?"

Fixed the chair today. The one Graciela noticed. It took me twenty minutes. I don't know why I left it broken for so long. I also patched the peeling paint above the window. Not because she said anything but because once I started with the chair I just kept going. Negrita watched me from the couch like she was supervising.

After I finished I sat in the chair and it felt solid. Stupid thing to feel good about but I did. I texted Cacho and told him I'd bring a bottle of the good Malbec tomorrow — the Catena, not the box wine we usually get. Sometimes you just want to do something a little extra for no reason. I've got the painting money still in my pocket. Next week is uncertain, it always is. But right now the chair is fixed, the wall looks clean, and tomorrow I'll eat well with people I like. That's not nothing.

entries.journal_entry:
Told Nadia she could take the summer to think about London but that we would discuss it seriously in June. She looked relieved but I felt nothing. I keep wondering if I am just delaying because I do not know how to say no to her face.

Nudge: "What would saying no actually cost you?"

Response: More than the argument itself. I think it would mean admitting the business is not enough to hold the family together the way it held us when Baba was running it.

My cousin Faisal called to say his son is taking over his construction firm this year. He did not say it to make a point, but it landed like one. His kid is twenty-three and already stepping in. Meanwhile I cannot even get Nadia to visit the warehouse.

People keep acting like I should just let her go do whatever she wants. Rania says I am being stubborn. But nobody asks what happens to this company in fifteen years. Nobody asks who carries it forward. I did not choose this either, but I showed up.

Nudge: "Did you want someone to ask you that back then?"

Response: That was a different time. There was no question to ask.

The new Italian distributor wants us to rebrand. Sleeker labels, English-only packaging, drop the family crest. He said the current look feels "regional." I almost hung up on him. Regional. My father designed that label with a calligrapher from Salt in 1982. It has been on every bottle we have ever shipped.

I brought it up with Samir, my operations manager, and he shrugged and said maybe the Italian has a point. That the European market responds to minimalism. I asked him if he would strip his grandmother's embroidery off the wall because a decorator said it clashed with the furniture. He laughed. I was not joking.

Rania told me later I am letting sentiment run the business. Maybe. But if I strip away everything that connects this company to where it came from, what exactly am I running? A logistics operation with my name on it. That is not what Baba built and it is not what I agreed to carry.

Could not sleep last night. Kept turning over the rebranding conversation. Went down to the kitchen at two in the morning and found Baba's old notebooks in the drawer where Mama keeps the good tablecloths. He used to write down every buyer's name, where they were from, what they ordered. Pages of it in his careful handwriting.

I sat there reading for an hour. He wrote about a French buyer in 1991 who wanted to change the oil blend and he refused. Lost the account. Got it back three years later when the buyer admitted the original was better. I do not know if that story is supposed to comfort me or scare me. The market is different now.

The thing that unsettles me is not the Italian distributor. It is that part of me can see his point. The rational part. And I do not know what it means that I can see it and still feel like acting on it would be a kind of betrayal. Like I would be the one who finally broke the thread.

Nudge: "What does that thread hold together for you?"

Response: Everything. The way we do business, yes, but also who I am in relation to my father. If I change the way he built things, I am saying his way was not good enough. And I am not ready to say that, even if the numbers eventually force me to.

Nadia brought home a prospectus from the London film school. Left it on the kitchen table like it was a magazine. Rania pretended not to notice. I noticed.

She has been building her case quietly. Showing us short films she made on her phone, mentioning alumni who work at Al Jazeera and BBC Arabic. She is smart about it. She knows a direct argument will not work with me so she is laying evidence like a lawyer. I would admire it if it were not aimed at dismantling the one plan I had for this family's future.

My brother Kamal called and said I should let her go, that holding her back will only push her further away. Easy for him to say. He moved to Dubai fifteen years ago and calls on holidays. He has no idea what it takes to keep things together here. To sit at Mama's table every Friday and hold the center while everyone else drifts toward their own orbits. Someone has to be the anchor and apparently that someone is always me.

Nudge: "Is being the anchor something you chose or something that happened to you?"

Response: Both, I think. It happened because I was the eldest and stayed. But I also chose it every single time I could have left. The problem is I chose it so many times it stopped feeling like a choice and started feeling like who I am. I do not know how to separate the two anymore.

Long week. Two shipments delayed because of port congestion in Aqaba, had to personally call four buyers to explain. Spent most of Thursday driving to Irbid because Mama's water heater broke and my brother the Dubai expert could not exactly fix it from there.

Came home to Rania and the kids eating mansaf without me. They saved me a plate. Ate it cold at ten o'clock watching the news. Just one of those weeks where everything takes twice as long and nothing feels finished.

Actually, the mansaf was good even cold. Rania uses Mama's recipe, the one with the dried yogurt from the women's co-op in Ajloun. Small thing but it reminded me that some things do carry forward without anyone forcing them.

Quarterly numbers came in and they are flat. Not terrible, but flat in a market that is growing means we are losing ground. Samir put together a presentation about expanding into the Gulf retail market. New packaging, smaller bottles, gift sets for Ramadan. I told him to go ahead with the proposal.

I did not fight it this time. Maybe because I am tired, maybe because the port delays last week reminded me how fragile our European pipeline really is. Diversifying makes sense. I told myself the Gulf packaging can look different from the European line without touching the original label. A compromise. Baba would have called it something else.

Nadia asked me at dinner if she could shadow a documentary crew in Amman next week. Some university project. I said fine without really thinking about it. Rania gave me a look, like she was surprised I did not put up a fight. I did not have one in me tonight.

Nudge: "Are these concessions or decisions?"

Took the kids to Friday prayers at the old mosque near Jabal Amman instead of our usual one. The imam there knew my father. Afterward he told my youngest, Omar, a story about how Baba once donated an entire shipment to a refugee camp in the eighties. Omar's eyes went wide. He had never heard that one.

Drove home with all three kids in the back seat arguing about whether Jiddo was a hero or just generous. Nadia said both. I did not correct anyone. It was a good morning.

Nudge: "What would you have corrected?"

Response: That he was not being generous. He was being obligated. In our family, in our community, you give because that is what you do. It is not heroism, it is just the way things work. But watching Omar hear it as something extraordinary made me realize maybe that way of doing things is not as obvious to them as it was to us. And that worries me.

Nadia came back from the documentary shoot glowing. She talked through dinner about camera angles and interview techniques. I listened and felt two things at once: pride and something heavier I cannot name.

The Italian distributor sent a revised proposal. They will keep the family crest but want to modernize the typography and add English and Italian text below the Arabic. Samir thinks it is a fair middle ground. I stared at the mockup for twenty minutes.

It looks fine. Objectively, it looks fine. The crest is there, the Arabic is there, just smaller. And I keep telling myself that Baba adapted too, that the company looked different in 1995 than it did in 1978. But he was the one making those changes. He earned the right to decide what stayed and what evolved. I inherited the decision and it does not sit the same way.

Rania asked me tonight if I have thought more about Nadia and London. I told her not now. She said there will never be a right time and she is probably correct. The June deadline I set is almost here and I do not have an answer. I do not even have a framework for the answer. Just this weight in my chest every time I think about the company ten years from now with no family in it.

Nudge: "What if the company survives but looks nothing like what your father built?"

Told Nadia she can apply to London. She cried. I cried. Rania stood in the doorway pretending she was not crying. The house felt lighter after, like we had been holding our breath for weeks.

Nudge: "What changed between last week and now?"

Response: Nothing changed, really. I just stopped waiting for the feeling to go away and made the decision with it still there. Maybe that is what my father did too. Carried the weight and moved anyway.

entries.journal_entry:
Got an email from the procurement office in Mafraq saying they want to switch to a cheaper Chinese filter unit. Half the cost, sure, but the failure rate data I have seen from field tests in East Africa is not good. I started drafting a reply with the test results attached, then stopped. Because what if they just need to save money this quarter and I am the one being difficult.

Rania said at dinner that Noor needs new textbooks and the car registration is due. She did not say it as a complaint, just facts, and that somehow made it worse. I kept thinking about those filters in Mafraq and whether I am fighting the right fight or just being stubborn about something that is not mine to decide.

Nudge: "What makes it feel like stubbornness vs. the right call?"

Response: When it is the right call I feel steady about it. This time I keep second-guessing, which probably means I know the cheaper option might actually work fine and I just hate the idea of cutting corners on something that goes to people who cannot complain if it breaks.

Cannot sleep. Went back to my laptop after everyone was in bed and found a message from Youssef, my old university friend who still works at the consultancy I left. He forwarded a job listing -- senior engineering director, Dubai office, salary that would cover both girls' tuition in one year. He wrote "just in case" with a smiley face. I know he means well.

The thing that got me was not the money. It was the simplicity. Show up, solve technical problems, go home. Nobody asking me to justify why clean water matters more than a lower unit cost. Nobody expecting me to write another grant narrative about "impact metrics" while families in Azraq are drinking from storage tanks that have not been serviced in months. I sat there staring at the listing for maybe twenty minutes.

Then I closed the tab. But I did not delete the email, and I am not sure what that means. Rania would not pressure me either way, but she would be relieved, and I can picture exactly how her shoulders would drop. That image keeps coming back. I feel like I chose this path and I keep choosing it, but tonight the choosing feels heavier than usual.

Nudge: "Why didn't you delete it?"

Response: Because part of me wanted to keep the option visible. Not to take it, probably. But to know it exists. Like proof that I could still walk away if this gets too hard. That sounds pathetic written down.

Drove out to the factory in Sahab this morning. Watched the team assemble thirty units in two hours, tested every seal myself. Loaded them into the truck for the Irbid delivery and the driver, Abu Faris, said his cousin's family has one of our filters and the kids stopped getting sick last winter.

Board call this afternoon, mostly routine. Layla from the Beirut office mentioned a school in Bekaa that wants to pilot our units in their water system. My mind wandered to whether we could scale the housing design to fit institutional pipes.

Nudge: "Did you bring up the school idea on the call?"

Response: No. I have learned not to pitch things until I have run the numbers. Last time I jumped ahead on a project in front of the board, the finance guy asked about margins and I had nothing. Embarrassing. Now I keep ideas to myself until they are bulletproof, which means some of them die quietly in my notebook.

A report came out from an NGO I respect showing that three water projects in southern Jordan were suspended due to funding cuts. Three. Just gone. Families who were relying on those systems now have to go back to buying trucked water at five times the cost. I read it on my phone during Leila's basketball game and could not focus on the match after that.

It makes me furious in a way I cannot really explain to anyone at the game. The other parents are talking about weekend plans and I am sitting there doing math in my head about how many households that affects. Rania noticed and squeezed my hand but did not ask. She knows the look by now.

Nudge: "Does Rania ever say anything about the look?"

Met with a potential corporate sponsor today, a petrochemical company that wants to fund fifty filter units for a camp near Zarqa as part of their CSR portfolio. Good meeting on paper. They want their logo on the units, photos for their annual report, a "brand ambassador" visit where executives tour the camp.

I said yes to all of it because we need the money. Fifty units is fifty families with clean water, and that is not nothing. But I spent the drive back feeling uneasy. The camp is not a backdrop. The people there are not props for someone's sustainability page. I know how it works -- I have done these partnerships before and the water still gets delivered -- but every time I shake hands on one of these deals I lose a small piece of something.

Told Rania about it over tea and she said, "You always do this. You take the deal and then you punish yourself for taking it." She is probably right. I just wish there was a way to fund this work that did not require performing gratitude for people who are writing it off on their taxes.

Noor asked me at dinner why we do not live in a bigger house like her friend Dina's family. She is fifteen and it was not a complaint, more like genuine curiosity. I told her we chose to put our money into the business. She asked what the business does exactly, and I realized I have never really sat down and explained it to her properly.

So I did. I told her about the filters, about the camps, about how water-borne illness is the thing that keeps kids out of school more than anything else in those communities. She listened, then asked, "But does it actually change anything or does it just help for a while?" I did not have a quick answer for that. It is the question I carry around constantly and hearing it from my daughter in her school uniform at our kitchen table hit differently than when it comes from a grant reviewer.

I said something about how helping for a while still matters. She shrugged and went to do homework. I sat there for a long time after. The honest answer is I do not know if what I am building will outlast the next funding cycle, and some nights that uncertainty feels like it is eating through the floor under me. But I also cannot imagine doing anything else, which maybe is its own kind of trap.

Long day at the factory fixing a seal press that jammed. Hands smell like silicone and my back is done. But we got the Zarqa order out on time and Abu Faris brought his wife's knafeh to share with the crew, which made the overtime feel less grim.

Nudge: "The Zarqa batch -- that the corporate sponsor one?"

Response: Yeah, that one. Fifty units, all sealed and loaded. Anyway the knafeh was really good, she puts extra cheese.

My brother Faisal called to tell me our cousin Samir just got promoted to VP at his bank in Riyadh. The way Faisal said it -- "Samir is doing very well" -- had that particular family tone that means "and what about you." I said congratulations and changed the subject but it sat with me the rest of the day.

I do not need validation from Faisal. He has never understood why I walked away from the consultancy and he is never going to. What I do know is that last week a mother in Irbid told our field coordinator that her son's eczema cleared up two months after we installed the filter. That is real. Samir's title is a line on a business card. I should not have to keep defending this to myself but apparently I still do.

Nudge: "If you don't need it, why's it still sitting with you?"

Response: Because somewhere in the back of my head there is still a version of me that measures success the way my family does. Cars, titles, where your kids go to school. I thought I outgrew that but it surfaces when I am tired or when someone like Faisal pokes at it. It passes. It just takes longer to pass than I would like.

entries.journal_entry:
I don't care what Priya thinks, I handled that IV alarm correctly. The patient was stable and I had already checked the line twice before she came over to "help." I've been on this unit for fourteen months now, I know the protocols.

Nudge: "What made her stepping in feel different from just helping out?"

Response: Because it was in front of the resident. Made me look like I didn't know what I was doing.

Still thinking about the Priya thing from earlier. I know I sounded annoyed in my last entry but there's more to it. She's been a nurse for nine years and sometimes she does this thing where she swoops in and takes over, and the doctors start looking at her for answers instead of me. I worked hard to get assigned to the step-down unit. Most new grads end up on med-surg for at least two years before they get a chance at cardiac, and I pushed for this placement because I want to be in the ICU by the time I'm twenty-five.

But here's what bugs me. When Mr. Okonkwo's wife was crying in the hallway after the cardiologist explained his prognosis, Priya was charting. I was the one who sat with Mrs. Okonkwo for twenty minutes, got her water, called her daughter. Nobody saw that. Nobody writes that in your evaluation. The doctors remember who looked competent with the IV pump, not who held someone's hand. And I did both today, but only one of those things is going to show up when they're deciding who moves to the ICU.

I'm not saying Priya's a bad nurse. She's great, actually. She taught me half of what I know about reading telemetry strips. I just wish the people making decisions about my career could see the full picture instead of whatever three-second snapshot they happened to catch.

Nudge: "You mentioned two things you did today — the clinical work and sitting with Mrs. Okonkwo. If you had to pick one to be known for, which would it be?"

Response: Both. That's the whole point. I don't want to pick. But if I'm being honest, the Mrs. Okonkwo thing is the reason I became a nurse in the first place. The ICU stuff, the certifications — that's how you get taken seriously so you can keep doing the other part. At least that's what I tell myself.
Three twelves in a row. Came home, microwaved leftover pasta, opened the CCRN study guide, and fell asleep on page four. Mom called but I let it go to voicemail. I'll call her back tomorrow.

Funny day. Got pulled into a rapid response on 4 North because they were short-staffed and the charge nurse remembered me from orientation. Patient was this 80-year-old guy, former high school football coach, and even while we were stabilizing him he was trying to crack jokes about his offensive line being tougher than his heart. We got him transferred to the ICU and the attending told me I did good work. Wrote it down in my phone immediately so I don't forget the exact words — "good clinical instinct." That's going in my ICU application.

Then I got back to my floor and found out my patient in room 412, Mrs. Patel, had been asking for me the whole time I was gone. She doesn't like the other nurses because she says they talk too fast and don't explain things. I felt bad. I sat with her for an extra ten minutes at the end of my shift even though I was already in overtime. My charge nurse gave me a look but Mrs. Patel smiled and said I remind her of her granddaughter, so whatever.

Nudge: "You stayed late for Mrs. Patel even after the charge nurse's look. What was going through your head in that moment?"

Response: It wasn't a big deal. Ten minutes. She needed someone to listen.

I'm so mad at myself. Mom needed me to drive her to her follow-up appointment today and I completely forgot because I swapped shifts to cover for Dana, who's going through a divorce and needed someone to take her Saturday. I've been telling everyone yes all week — yes I'll cover your shift, yes I'll precept the new grad, yes I'll sit on the unit council — and then I forgot the one thing that actually mattered.

My brother Tyler had to take the bus from school and then drive her in my car, and he's only had his license for three months. He was fine about it but Mom sounded so tired on the phone when she told me. Not angry, just tired. That's worse. If she yelled at me I could at least argue back but the quiet disappointment just sits in my chest like a rock.

The thing is, I covered for Dana because she was falling apart in the break room and I couldn't just walk away from that. And the new grad, Jess, she literally doesn't know how to titrate a drip yet and if I don't teach her someone's going to get hurt. And the unit council looks good on my resume for the ICU transfer. Every single thing I said yes to had a reason. But all those reasons don't change the fact that my mom sat in a waiting room chair that hurts her hip because I wasn't there.

I need to figure out how to stop doing this. I keep spreading myself across everyone else's emergencies and then there's nothing left for the people who were there first.

Nudge: "You listed the reasons for every yes. Is there one you would take back?"

Response: The unit council. That one was for me, not for anyone else. The rest I'd do again because people needed help. But the council meeting — I could have said next quarter. I just didn't want to lose the spot.

Called Mom after my shift ended. She said the appointment went fine, the surgeon is happy with her range of motion, and Tyler did a good job driving. She told me not to worry about it.

I ordered grocery delivery to her house for the week and signed up for a meal prep service for Tyler so he doesn't just eat ramen every night. Set a recurring reminder in my phone for all her upcoming appointments. I won't miss another one.

Studied for three hours this morning before my shift. Went through the hemodynamic monitoring chapter and took a practice quiz — scored 82%, which is passing but not where I want to be. My target is 90% on every practice test before I schedule the real exam. I made flashcards for the concepts I missed and I'm going to review them on my breaks this week.

At work, had a good shift. Got assigned to a fresh post-cath patient and managed the sheath pull site without any complications. Documented everything meticulously because I know the ICU charge nurses pull charts when they're reviewing internal transfer candidates. My manager Denise mentioned that there might be an opening on the ICU in March. She didn't promise anything but she said my name had come up.

After work, drove to Mom's even though it wasn't Sunday. Tyler had a rough day at school — some issue with a group project where his partners aren't pulling their weight. I helped him outline a plan for talking to his teacher about it. He's so much like me, takes everything on himself instead of asking for help. I told him it's okay to flag when something isn't working. The irony of giving that advice was not lost on me.

Nudge: "What irony were you thinking of?"

Had coffee with Priya before shift today. She told me she's leaving — transferring to a hospital in Phoenix because her husband got a job out there. I was surprised by how much it bothered me. We're not close exactly, but she's the person I learned the most from on this unit. She said she thinks I'm ready for the ICU and that she told Denise the same thing.

I should feel good about that. And part of me does. But there's this other part that's uneasy. If Priya leaves, I'm suddenly one of the more experienced nurses on the floor, and we have two new grads starting next month. That means more precepting, more questions, more of me being the person everyone leans on. I already can't say no. What happens when there's literally no one else to ask? I keep thinking about last week, forgetting Mom's appointment because I was stretched too thin. If the workload doubles, something is going to break. I just don't know if it'll be my schedule or me.

Nudge: "You said something will break. What would protecting yourself look like?"

Response: I honestly don't know. Saying no, I guess. But saying no to a new grad who doesn't know how to read a rhythm strip feels like putting a patient at risk. And saying no to my family isn't an option. So I don't really know what's left to cut.
CCRN practice test today, scored 88%. Getting closer. Tyler's coming over for dinner tonight, I'm making Mom's enchilada recipe.

Tyler told me over dinner that he's thinking about nursing school. He said he wants to be like me. I had to go to the kitchen for a minute because I didn't want him to see me tear up. It's the best thing anyone has said to me in months and also the thing that scares me the most.

Because what does he see? He sees me working hard, yes. He sees me helping people. But does he also see the dark circles and the missed appointments and the fact that I eat most of my meals standing up in a break room? I want him to have what I have — this feeling of doing something that matters — but I want him to do it without grinding himself down the way I do. I told him I thought it was a great idea and that I'd help him look at programs. And I meant it. I just hope he's better at the balance part than I am.

Nudge: "What would you want him to do differently than you?"

Response: Ask for help before he's drowning. Take a day off without feeling guilty. Remember that being good at your job and being there for your people shouldn't be an either-or thing. Basically everything I know I should do but don't.

entries.journal_entry:
Another sprint planning meeting where they want me to estimate the same maintenance tickets I have been doing for three years. I know exactly how long everything takes now. That is maybe the problem.

Nudge: "What's bothering you about knowing?"

The new architect they brought in, Jennifer, she wants to rewrite the payment gateway from scratch. Using Go instead of Java. Everyone in the room looked at me like I was supposed to object, since I built the original thing in 2019. But honestly? Part of me wanted to say yes, let us do it, let me learn something new instead of patching the same codebase forever.

I did not say that though. I said we should consider the timeline and the risk to the December release. Which is true. The business needs stability. But I walked out of that meeting feeling like I had swallowed something heavy.

Nudge: "You sided against yourself in there?"

Response: It is not that simple. Jennifer does not know what she does not know about the regulatory requirements. Someone had to be the adult.

Elena surprised me with tickets to a tech conference in San Francisco next month. Not the corporate kind with vendor booths and networking bingo, but one of those unconference events where people just propose sessions in the morning and see what happens. She said she noticed I have been looking at conference websites lately but never registering for anything.

I should be excited. Five years ago I would have booked the flight before she finished her sentence. Now I am already thinking about the sprint commitments, about whether Marcus can handle the on-call rotation alone, about what my manager will say when I request the days off. The conference is on a weekend. I would only need to take Friday off. Still, I hesitated.

What bothers me most is not the hesitation itself. It is that I used to make decisions faster. Move to Austin? Two weeks to decide. Take the startup job over the stable bank position? One evening with Elena and a bottle of wine. Now I research, I weigh, I consider all stakeholders. My mother would say I finally grew up. But it feels more like something got dimmer.

I will go to the conference. I already told Elena yes. I just wish the yes had come easier.

Nudge: "When did decisions start feeling heavy?"

Response: Maybe around the time my father got sick. 2018. Suddenly there were consequences everywhere I looked. Every choice felt connected to whether I would be there, whether I was reliable. He passed in 2019 and I thought it would lift, but it did not. The weight just found new things to attach to.

Long day. Production incident at 3 AM, the payment gateway again. Not the part Jennifer wanted to rewrite, ironically, but the legacy integration with the bank's SFTP server. I was on call so I got the page. Took four hours to trace it to a certificate expiration that nobody had on their calendar.

By 7 AM I had fixed it and documented everything. By 8 AM I was in the standup explaining what happened. By 9 AM I was getting messages from the VP asking how we let this happen. Not thank you for catching it. Not good job on the quick fix. Just how did we let this happen.

I did not let anything happen. I inherited this system. I have been asking for budget to modernize the certificate management for two years. But I did not say that either. I just said we would add better monitoring and put the renewal dates in a shared calendar. Safe answer. Team player answer.

Elena asked me at dinner why I looked so tired. I said work was busy. I did not tell her about the VP message because then she would get angry on my behalf and I would have to either agree with her anger or defend the company that made me feel small today. Both options felt exhausting.

I need to sleep but my mind keeps rehearsing the incident like I missed something. I did not miss anything. The certificate was not in any documentation I had access to. But I keep checking anyway.

Nudge: "You keep swallowing things down lately."

Response: Maybe I do. It is easier than the alternative. Every time I push back, I become the difficult one, the senior engineer who cannot adapt, the old guy who thinks he knows better. So I absorb it. I fix things quietly. I make myself small so the team can function. It is what my father did at his factory job for forty years. I used to think he was weak for that. Now I understand he was just tired.

The morning after a bad night is always strange. Everything looks normal. Coffee tastes the same. I just feel like I am watching myself go through the motions from somewhere slightly outside.

Nudge: "What motions are you watching yourself do?"

Response: The usual routine. Checking Slack before I get out of bed. Reviewing the overnight alerts. Logging into the standup. Answering questions I have answered a hundred times. It is not bad, exactly. It is just automatic. Like I am not really needed there, just my pattern.

Flew to San Francisco yesterday for the unconference. Staying at a hotel near the Embarcadero with a view of the bay. Weather is cold and gray but I brought my good jacket.

The conference format is interesting. You show up in the morning, people pitch sessions on index cards, everyone votes with stickers, and then the schedule emerges. No predetermined speakers, no approved slides. Someone proposed a session on rebuilding legacy systems with LLMs. Someone else wanted to discuss leaving tech after 50. I almost proposed something about maintaining software versus maintaining yourself, but I did not have the index card ready in time.

I attended four sessions. The legacy systems one was good, practical advice I could use. The leaving tech one was harder to sit through. A woman around my age talked about how she realized she had optimized her whole life for a company that laid her off in an afternoon. She cried a little. Everyone was respectful.

Tonight I walked along the waterfront instead of joining the dinner group. Watched the ferries come and go. Called my mother. She asked if I was eating well and I said yes, even though I had skipped lunch. Some lies are kindness.

Tomorrow is the last day. I feel different here. Not better or worse. Just different.

Nudge: "What stopped you with that index card?"

Response: I was still figuring out what I wanted to say. It felt too personal for a room of strangers. Maybe next time.

Back from the conference for a week now and people at work keep asking how it was. I say it was great, very inspiring, lots of good ideas. Which is true. But when they ask what I am going to do differently, I do not have a good answer. Learn Go? Propose a rewrite? Those feel like someone else's moves.

My manager suggested I do a lunch-and-learn to share what I learned. I said I would think about it. I probably will not do it. Public speaking was never my thing, and honestly, the sessions that meant most to me were the ones I cannot really present in a corporate setting. Nobody wants a slide deck about existential uncertainty in late-career engineers.

Nudge: "What did you actually bring back from there?"

Response: Some business card contacts. A reading list. The usual conference stuff.

December sprint started with three people out sick and a scope increase nobody agreed to. I have been working ten-hour days trying to keep us on track for the holiday release. Jennifer is frustrated because her Go rewrite keeps getting deprioritized. I am frustrated because I am the one who has to explain why to her without making leadership look bad.

Had dinner with Elena's cousin Miguel who is visiting from Monterrey. He runs a small construction company there. He asked me about my job and I tried to explain sprint planning and stakeholder management and he just laughed and said it sounds like I spend a lot of energy managing people's feelings about work instead of actually working. He did not mean it unkindly but it stuck with me. Maybe he is right. Maybe that is what corporate software engineering becomes after a while.

Nudge: "What would actually working look like for you?"

Response: Building something. Seeing it run. Not writing status reports about why it is not done yet. But that is not realistic at my level, I suppose.

I finally did it. I proposed to my manager that I move to Jennifer's team for the Go rewrite project. Not as a lead, not as an architect. Just as a developer. Someone who writes code and learns the new language and maybe remembers what it felt like to not know everything about a codebase.

He looked at me like I had asked for a demotion. Which, technically, I had. He said he would need to think about it, talk to HR, consider the implications. He asked if everything was okay at home. I said yes, everything is fine, I just want to learn something new before I am too old to learn.

I do not know why I said it like that. I am not too old. But something in me wanted to say it out loud, the fear underneath. Like naming it would make it less powerful.

Elena was proud of me when I told her. She said she has been waiting for me to do something like this for years. I did not know she was waiting. I thought I was hiding the restlessness well enough. Apparently I was only hiding it from myself.

Now I wait to see what they decide. Maybe they say no and I stay in my maintenance role forever. Maybe they say yes and I flounder in a new language at 57. Both possibilities feel more alive than certainty.
They said yes. Contingent on finishing the holiday release first, but yes. I start on Jennifer's team in January. I have been studying Go tutorials at night, feeling like a student again.

entries.journal_entry:
Got an email from my cousin Faris today, forwarding photos from his daughter's first birthday party back in Amman. The whole family was there — uncles, aunts, my mother holding the baby with that particular look she gets. I was not invited to skip it; I just was not there. No one said anything pointed but the group chat went quiet for a while after the photos dropped and I could feel the space where my name should have been.

Nothing happened, really. I ate leftover lentil soup, re-read a paper on morphological parsing, tried to focus. My flatmate Callum asked if I wanted to go to a pub quiz and I said no, then sat there wishing I had said yes.

Nudge: "What was the quiet in the group chat about?"

Response: It is not that they are angry. It is that they have stopped expecting me to show up. That is worse, I think. Like I have been reclassified from "absent" to "gone."

Supervisor meeting. Dr. Keane asked me to commit to one research question by next Friday and I heard myself say yes, absolutely, no problem. Walked home and could not stop thinking about the three other directions I had sketched out last month.

Nudge: "What stopped you from pushing back in the meeting?"

Response: Because pushing back feels like making a scene, and I do not make scenes. My father never raised his voice in a meeting in his life. But I keep wondering if the question I pick will be the one that bores me by October.

Library until six. Drafted the introduction for the narrowed thesis proposal, which took longer than it should have because I kept second-guessing paragraph transitions. Callum and I walked to the chippie on Clerk Street after.

Friday call with my mother went well for once. She asked about the thesis and I described the morphological analysis angle in simple terms — how computers learn to break Arabic words into their root pieces. She said it sounded useful, which from her is high praise.

I felt like myself on that call. Not performing, not deflecting her questions about when I am coming home. Just explaining something I actually care about to someone who wanted to listen. She even asked a follow-up about how the root system works differently in dialects. Small thing, but it settled something in me.

Second entry today because my brain will not shut off. After the call with my mother I went for a walk up Arthur's Seat. Halfway up I got this rush of clarity — or maybe it was just adrenaline — where I started thinking about what it would look like to pivot my thesis entirely. Not the careful narrowing Dr. Keane wants. Something bigger. Cross-linguistic transfer models for under-resourced dialects. Levantine, Gulf, Maghrebi. My heart was actually racing on the hill and it was not from the climb.

Then I sat on the bench at the top and the wind hit me and I thought about what pivoting would mean. Another meeting with Keane where I contradict what I said three days ago. My mother asking again when I will finish. My uncle Hisham, who helped fund the first year, shaking his head at another delay. Everyone back home already thinks this PhD was an impulsive detour, and changing direction again would prove them right.

So I walked back down, made tea, opened the narrowed proposal draft. Still sitting here with it. The cursor is blinking at the end of paragraph three and I have not typed anything in forty minutes.

Nudge: "What would happen if you brought the bigger idea to Keane?"

Department social last night at The Pear Tree. I went because Dr. Keane mentioned it would be good to show face, and I know what that means in academic circles — it means attendance is optional in the way that prayer is optional at a family gathering. So I went.

Ended up in a long conversation with Priya from the NLP group. She is working on code-switching detection in Hindi-English tweets and the way she talked about it — with this specific, focused energy — made me jealous. Not of the topic. Of the certainty. She has her question, her method, her timeline. She did not seem to need a detour.

I told her about the dialect transfer idea. She said it sounded fascinating and asked why I was not doing it. I said timing, funding, the usual. She shrugged and said "those are real constraints" in a way that felt like she was politely letting me off the hook. I appreciated it and hated it at the same time.

Nudge: "What did you want her to say instead?"

Response: Something like "do it anyway." Or "those are excuses." Someone outside the family orbit giving me permission to be reckless. Which is ridiculous because I am thirty-eight years old and should not need permission from a colleague I barely know.

Submitted the narrowed thesis proposal to Dr. Keane this morning. Forty-two pages, bibliography cleaned up, research questions scoped to morphological segmentation for Modern Standard Arabic. Felt like handing in a homework assignment I had done properly rather than one that kept me up at night, but there is something to be said for finishing.

Callum and I cooked mansaf tonight — or my version of it, which involves Greek yogurt because Edinburgh does not have decent jameed. He kept asking questions about the spices and I caught myself explaining things the way my mother does, with small corrections and zero patience. Made me laugh.

After dinner I sat with my laptop and, almost without thinking about it, opened a blank document and started outlining the dialect transfer idea. Not as a thesis. Just notes. No one has to see it. I wrote for an hour and it was the most alive I have felt in front of a screen in weeks.

My uncle Hisham called today. Not the usual Friday family call — a separate, pointed call. He asked how the PhD was going and I told him I submitted the proposal. He said good, asked about the timeline, and then said he hoped I was not "chasing new rabbits" — his phrase, not mine. Apparently my mother mentioned something about dialect research and he connected dots.

I told him everything was on track, the proposal was submitted, Dr. Keane was happy. All true. I did not mention the outline document from last night. It is not lying. It is managing information. People share what is relevant and I shared what was relevant.

Went to the Edinburgh Farmers' Market at Castle Terrace this morning, mostly for the za'atar bread from the Lebanese stall. Ran into Priya and her partner. We talked about the summer conference circuit and she mentioned a workshop on low-resource NLP in Copenhagen in September. She looked at me when she said it, like she knew.

I came home and emailed Dr. Keane asking for a meeting next week. Just to discuss, I wrote. I did not say what about. But I printed out the dialect transfer outline and put it in my bag. Maybe I will bring it, maybe I will not. It is in the bag though. That feels like enough for today.

Nudge: "Why print it out if you might not show it?"

Response: Because I need to know it is there. Like a door I have not walked through yet but I can see. If I leave it at home it is just a file on my laptop. In the bag it is something I chose to carry. I think I am going to show it. I think I have been working up to this for weeks and I just did not want to admit it because admitting it means it matters, and if it matters then Keane saying no will actually hurt.

entries.journal_entry:
Spent most of Sunday lesson planning for the week. Marcus's mom sent another email asking if I could provide extra worksheets for him to practice at home. I said yes before I even finished reading it. That makes four families now with custom homework requests.

Mom called during lunch. Same conversation we always have. She asked if I'm eating enough, if I'm sleeping. I told her I'm fine. She doesn't need to worry about me when she has her own stuff going on with Dad's knee surgery coming up.

Aiden finally raised his hand today. First time all year. He got the answer wrong, but I could tell he'd been thinking about it. I just smiled and asked a follow-up question that let him get partway there. Small moment, but I keep replaying it.

Nudge: "Why does that one stick with you?"

Response: He's been so withdrawn since his parents split up this summer. Seeing him try, even knowing he might be wrong—felt like he trusted the room enough to risk it. That's the whole point of what I'm trying to build in there.

Mrs. Patterson cornered me after pickup today. Spent twenty minutes telling me her daughter needs more challenging work and that the current curriculum isn't "stimulating enough." I nodded through the whole thing. Told her I'd look into differentiated assignments.

I wanted to tell her that her daughter is actually right at grade level and doing fine. That I have eight kids reading below benchmark who need my attention more. But I didn't. Just smiled and said I understood her concerns.

Now I'm drafting a custom enrichment packet at 9pm. For a kid who doesn't need it. Because her mom is loud.

Nudge: "Does that sit okay?"

Response: Not really. I keep telling myself it's just easier this way, that pushing back would make the rest of the year harder. But mostly I think I'm scared she'll go to Principal Davis. And I hate that I care more about avoiding that conversation than standing up for what I actually think is right for the classroom.

Staff meeting ran late. New reading assessment mandate for October. Everyone nodded along even though it means losing two instruction days. I didn't say anything either. Grabbed coffee with Lisa after—she's frustrated too but says there's no point fighting it.

The SEL committee met today and somehow I'm now also in charge of the parent workshop in November. I don't remember volunteering. Principal Davis just looked at me during the meeting and said "Sarah, you'd be great at this" and everyone turned and smiled and I heard myself saying sure.

I already run the book club. I already do morning bus duty twice a week. I don't know how to say no when everyone's looking at me like I'm the one who always says yes.

Called Mom after to vent and she just said "Sweetheart, you can't pour from an empty cup." Thanks, Mom. Very helpful.

Nudge: "What would happen if you'd said no?"

Response: I don't know. Probably nothing terrible. Someone else would've done it. But then they'd all be doing me a favor and I'd feel like I wasn't pulling my weight. It's fine. I'll figure it out.

Jaylen brought me a drawing today. Just a crayon picture of me and him and the classroom hamster. He said I'm his favorite teacher ever. Put it in my desk drawer with the others.

Some days that's enough. Today it was.

Sunday again. Grading. Always grading. Thinking about next week's parent conferences. Need to figure out how to tell the Millers their son might need an evaluation without them getting defensive. They're nice people, they just don't want to hear it.

Made pasta for dinner. Watched half a movie. Can't remember what it was called. Some romantic comedy. Fell asleep on the couch before it ended.

This is fine. This is just September. It always gets easier by October.

Parent conferences ran until 8pm. Smiled through all of them. Told every parent their kid was making progress, even when I had concerns, because who has the energy to have those conversations back-to-back for four hours?

The one I couldn't avoid was with Eli's dad. Had to tell him Eli's been struggling with focus and we might want to explore supports. He got quiet. Asked if I was saying something was wrong with his son. I backpedaled so fast. Said no, no, just thinking ahead, just wanting to help, never mind, he's doing great actually.

I know I should have held my ground. The kid needs help. But the look on his dad's face—I couldn't do it.

Nudge: "What stopped you from holding that line?"

Response: He looked scared. And hurt. And I just thought—this is his kid. His whole world. And I'm telling him something's wrong in a fluorescent classroom at 7pm on a Thursday. I couldn't be the one to make him feel worse. Even if staying quiet means Eli doesn't get the help he needs until someone else brings it up. That's on me now.

Caught up on emails. Planned next week's lessons. Went for a walk around the neighborhood. Nothing remarkable. Just a Saturday that felt like a Saturday for once.

Lisa texted about getting brunch tomorrow. Said yes. Probably the first social plan I've made in three weeks that isn't work-related.

Brunch with Lisa was good. We talked about school stuff but also about her sister's wedding and the trip she's planning for winter break. Normal friend things.

She asked how I was really doing. I gave the usual answer—busy, tired, fine. She just looked at me for a second and said "You know you don't have to be everything for everyone, right?"

I changed the subject. But I've been thinking about it all afternoon. I don't know how to be any other way. I don't know if I want to be, really. The kids need someone who shows up. The parents need someone who listens. Maybe that's just what teaching is. Maybe that's just what I am.

Nudge: "What did you change the subject to?"
