entries.journal_entry:
Spent the whole evening comparing fixed deposits on three different bank sites while the kids fought over the remote. Anil says we should lock in the rate before it drops again. I know he is right but I kept thinking about that weekend trip to Alibaug we never took. We had the dates picked and everything.

Nudge: "What happened to the Alibaug trip?"

Response: We pushed it. First it was school fees, then Anil's mother needed new glasses, then he said let's wait till Diwali. Diwali came and went. The dates are still saved in my calendar like some kind of joke.

My sister Meera called and asked when we are finally going on a proper holiday. I told her we went to Lonavala in June. She laughed and said "that does not count, it was one night at your cousin's farmhouse." I do not need her opinion on how we spend our time or money.

Nudge: "Did it bother you because she was wrong, or because she wasn't?"

Took Aarav and Rhea for their dental checkups. No cavities, which is a relief because the last round cost us eleven thousand rupees. Came home, made dal and rice, helped Aarav with his Hindi homework. He is struggling with the matras again. Called the tuition teacher and she can take him Tuesdays and Thursdays for two thousand a month.

Anil came back late. I had kept his food covered on the counter. He ate while scrolling his phone and went straight to bed. I watched two episodes of my show and finished the leftover gulab jamun from Sunday. The house was quiet. It should have felt peaceful but it just felt flat.

My mother-in-law brought up the gold investment scheme again at lunch. She wants us to put money into one of those jeweller savings plans, says every family needs gold as a safety net. Anil looked at me like I was supposed to agree. I smiled and said we would think about it.

Later I told him I do not want more gold sitting in a locker I forget the code to. He said it is about long-term protection, not about wearing it. Fine. But we have insurance, we have the FD, we have his provident fund. At what point do we stop saving for some imagined disaster and use the money to actually live a little? He got quiet and changed the subject.

Nudge: "What would 'living a little' look like right now?"

Response: Nothing extravagant. Maybe booking that resort in Goa I bookmarked three months ago. Getting the good cheese from the fancy store without feeling guilty. Taking an auto instead of waiting twenty minutes for the bus with two cranky kids. Small things that do not require a committee meeting.

I had a full breakdown over a cake. Not even a real breakdown, just stood in the kitchen with my hands shaking after I realized I forgot to order Rhea's birthday cake. Her party is Saturday. I had the tab open on my phone for two weeks, kept meaning to customize it, kept getting pulled away. She wanted the one shaped like a castle with purple icing and her name in gold. Now the bakery says they need five days minimum and I have three.

Called four other bakeries. One can do it but it costs almost double. I said yes without even checking with Anil because I could not bear the thought of my daughter's face when there is no castle cake.

Then I sat on the kitchen floor and cried. Not because of the cake. Because of everything the cake represents. How my days dissolve into errands and appointments and packed lunches and I cannot hold onto a single thing for myself. I used to coordinate events for two hundred guests. I used to keep spreadsheets and timelines and vendor contacts organized across six months. Now I cannot order a cake on time.

Anil found the charge on the credit card and asked about it. I told him the original bakery fell through. He said okay, no problem. He did not ask why I was upset. I do not think he noticed.

The worst part is that I know the party will be fine. Rhea will love the cake. The kids will run around the flat screaming. The mothers will sit in the living room and talk about school admissions. And I will clean up afterwards and load the dishwasher and think about the next thing. That is what bothers me. Not that it went wrong. That even when it goes right, there is nothing in it for me.

Nudge: "You said 'nothing in it for me' — what would something for you look like?"

Response: I honestly do not know anymore. That scares me more than the money stuff. At least with money I know what the number should be. But when I try to picture what would make me feel like I got something out of a day, I come up blank. I used to know. I used to have answers. When did I stop?

Rhea's party went well. She wore her purple lehenga and ate cake with both hands. I took about sixty photos. At one point she grabbed my face and said "best mummy" and I felt it go straight through me.

After everyone left and the kids were asleep, I poured myself a glass of wine I had hidden behind the pickle jars. Sat on the balcony with my feet up. Pune was noisy below me — auto horns, someone's TV, a dog barking. I just sat there drinking slowly and not thinking about anything. Forty-five minutes of nothing. It was the best part of my week and it cost me two hundred rupees and zero planning.

I need more of that. Not wine specifically. Just moments that belong to me.

Long week. Aarav had a stomach bug Monday through Wednesday so I was home with him while Rhea went to school. Spent three days washing sheets, making khichdi, and checking his temperature. He is fine now, already back to leaving Lego pieces everywhere.

Anil's company announced a restructuring. Nothing definite yet but he has been tense. Came home Thursday and said we should hold off on any big expenses for a few months, just in case. I agreed because what else would I say. We ordered in biryani that night and watched a movie together on the couch. The biryani was from the place near the station, the one that uses too much oil but tastes right. Anil fell asleep halfway through the film. I finished it alone and cleaned the containers.

Nudge: "What else would you have wanted to say?"

Sunday. Took both kids to the park in the morning. Rhea did the monkey bars for the first time without help and I clapped so hard another parent looked at me. Aarav just wanted to sit on the bench and read his comic book, which is fine, he has always been like that.

Came home and looked at my recipe blog for the first time in weeks. I have a draft post about paneer tikka that I never finished. The photos are on my phone, the notes are in a notebook somewhere. I thought about sitting down to write it but then the pressure cooker started whistling and Rhea needed help with her project and the moment passed. My phone buzzed with a notification that my blog traffic dropped forty percent this month. I closed the app.

Nudge: "What does the blog mean to you these days?"

Response: It used to feel like proof I still had a brain. That I could make something and put it out there with my name on it. Now it just feels like another thing I am failing at. One more tab I keep meaning to get back to.

Renewed our health insurance today. Bumped up the coverage to ten lakhs because Anil's colleague had a surgery that wiped out his savings. Felt right to have that cushion even though the premium went up. Also renewed Aarav's accident cover since he is starting football classes next month.

Made butter chicken for dinner using my grandmother's recipe. The one with the kasuri methi at the end. Both kids asked for seconds. Anil said it was the best I have made in months, which I will take as a compliment and not an insult. After they went to bed, I sat down and actually finished the paneer tikka blog post. Published it at eleven PM. Nothing fancy, but it is out there. Felt good to hit that button.

entries.journal_entry:
The meeting with Diego didn't go how I planned. He sat there scrolling through my routing proposal on his laptop, barely looking up, and when he finally said something it was about the formatting. The formatting. Three months of pulling delivery data, testing alternate corridors, calling warehouse contacts in Medellin -- and he wants to talk about fonts.

I should have pushed back harder. Instead I just nodded and said I'd revise it. The whole walk back to my desk I was already rewriting my argument in my head, all the things I should have said. The numbers speak for themselves. Our current route through Bucaramanga adds two days and the clients in the southern corridor keep complaining.

Part of me wonders if I backed down because he's been here fifteen years and I've been here eleven months. That shouldn't matter when the data is right there. But I sat quiet like it did matter, and now I'm stuck doing cosmetic edits on something that should already be approved.

Nudge: "Why do you think you went quiet?"

Still at the office. Everyone else left at six but I'm going through the client satisfaction numbers from Q4 because nobody else is going to do it. Found three accounts where response times slipped below target because the team was running the old dispatch sequence I already flagged two months ago. Two months.

My mom called while I was pulling the spreadsheets. I let it go to voicemail. She'll ask why I'm still at work, then she'll ask when I'm coming home for New Year's, and I don't have answers to either one right now.

Nudge: "Are you going home for New Year's?"

Response: Probably not. There's too much to clean up here before January. She'll understand. She usually does.

Spent New Year's Eve at Lucia's apartment with a few people from the office. Nothing big -- arepas, aguardiente, the countdown on the TV. I didn't check my phone once the whole night, which felt strange at first but I stopped noticing after a while. Lucia made fun of me for not knowing any of the songs on the playlist.

On January first I went for a run along the ciclovia and came back with an idea for restructuring the southern corridor drops. Not the full routing plan Diego shut down -- something smaller, a pilot with just the Cali deliveries. If I can show a cost reduction on one leg, the rest of the argument builds itself. Nobody has to approve a pilot. I can just run it with the three drivers who already told me the current route doesn't make sense.

I drafted the pilot scope on my laptop sitting on Lucia's couch while everyone was watching a movie. Felt good to have something concrete again instead of waiting for permission. I want to own how this unfolds, start to finish.

The Cali pilot is running. Three drivers, rerouted through Palmira instead of the standard loop. I didn't tell Diego. I told the drivers it was a test week and tracked the first four deliveries myself from the dashboard. Average time dropped by six hours per run. Six hours. That's not a font issue.

I built a one-page summary with the numbers and sent it to the full operations chat, not just Diego. Maybe that was aggressive. Lucia said I should have gone through him first, and she's probably right in terms of politics. But if I go through him it sits in his inbox for two weeks and nothing changes. I've watched that happen before.

He hasn't responded yet. Part of me is nervous but mostly I feel like I did the thing I actually wanted to do instead of waiting around editing slide decks. If the numbers hold through Friday, the argument is over. I want this to stand on the work, not on who approved it.

Diego pulled me into his office yesterday and I thought he was going to tear into me about the pilot but he just asked for the full data set. No commentary, no lecture, just "send me the spreadsheet." I sent it within the hour. He forwarded it to the VP of ops in Mexico City. I know because the VP emailed me directly asking for a call next week.

That call could be anything. Could be a reprimand, could be an expansion. Either way the work is out there now and it's mine. I keep refreshing my email like that's going to make the calendar invite appear faster. Need to stop doing that.

Nudge: "What are you hoping she says?"

Response: I don't know. Something that makes the last three months feel like they counted.

Call with the VP happened. Forty-five minutes. She asked detailed questions about methodology, cost projections, scalability to other corridors. She knew what she was talking about, which made it feel real -- not like presenting to someone who just wants to check a box.

At the end she said she wants me to present to the regional leadership group in February. A full presentation, not a one-pager. My stomach dropped when she said it, not because I'm scared but because I've never had that kind of stage before and I want it to go perfectly. I already know I'm going to over-prepare.

Called my mom after to tell her. She said "mija, eat something, you sound wired." She's not wrong. I had an empanada for lunch and nothing since. But this is the thing I've been pushing for since I got here and it's actually happening. Not because someone handed it to me -- because I built it and put it out there myself.

Nudge: "What does a perfect presentation look like to you?"

Response: Numbers that nobody can argue with, questions I already have answers for, and walking out knowing they couldn't have gotten that analysis from anyone else on the team.

Stayed up until three working on the deck. Caught myself copying Diego's formatting from an old presentation because I thought it would make the leadership group take it more seriously. Closed the file and went to bed feeling off about it.

Lucia told me today that some people on the team think I'm "trying to skip levels." She said it like she was doing me a favor by telling me, and maybe she was, but it stung. I've been busting myself on this project and the fact that doing good work gets read as political maneuvering makes me want to throw my laptop into the Magdalena.

I asked her who said it and she wouldn't tell me. Fine. But now I'm sitting here wondering if I should tone it down for the February presentation or just go harder. Toning it down feels like giving up ground I earned. Going harder might prove their point. I don't know.

Finished the presentation draft this morning. Fourteen slides, every number sourced, three scenario projections. I ran through it out loud twice in my apartment and timed it at eighteen minutes, which leaves room for questions. My neighbor probably thinks I'm losing it.

Went to my abuela's for almuerzo after. She made sancocho and didn't ask about work at all, just told me about her friend Doris who's fighting with her landlord. Sat there eating soup and listening and realized I hadn't thought about the presentation for almost two hours. That's probably the longest I've gone without thinking about it in weeks.

Nudge: "How did the two hours feel?"

Response: Good. Like my brain got to stop running for a bit. I forgot what that was like.

Diego sent me "feedback" on the presentation draft. Half of it is adding his name to the second slide as a co-contributor. He didn't contribute. He sat on my original proposal for weeks and only engaged after I went around him. Now he wants credit on the version that works.

I know the smart move is probably to add his name and keep the peace. Everyone keeps telling me to "play the game." But I didn't build this by playing the game. I built it by ignoring the game and doing the actual work. If I put his name on it, what am I even presenting? Someone else's stamp on my project. I'm going to push back. Maybe that's stupid. I don't care.

Nudge: "Do you actually not care?"

Presentation went well. The VP asked me to lead a corridor optimization working group across three countries. Diego shook my hand after and didn't mention the co-credit thing. I slept eleven hours last night and I'm still tired, but it's the kind of tired where you know you did the thing.

entries.journal_entry:
My aunt called again about the civil service exam. She does this every Chuseok season, like clockwork — finds some cousin's daughter who just passed and drops it into conversation as though I asked. I said the same thing I always say. I'm fine. The studio is fine. Rent is covered.

She doesn't believe me. I know because she paused the way she does when she's holding something back, and then she said Umma worries. Which is just her way of saying she worries, but putting it on my mother so it lands harder. I didn't take the bait. Told her I had to go check the kiln.

There was nothing in the kiln. I just sat in the studio and threw a slab that I didn't need. The wheel was cold and the clay was too wet because I hadn't wedged it properly. It didn't matter. I wasn't making anything. I was just moving my hands so I wouldn't think about the conversation. Normal Wednesday.

Gallery owner asked if I'd consider doing a collaboration with a streetwear brand. Custom glaze finishes on sneaker displays. Good money. I said I'd think about it but the whole walk home I kept hearing halmeoni's voice — that clay remembers what your hands were thinking when you shaped it. Felt cheap even considering it.

Woke up to a message from Ji-hye. She got into the residency in Arita. The one we both applied to, the one that would have meant three months studying under a fifth-generation potter and learning noborigama firing. She got it and I didn't, and she told me by text at 7am like it was news about the weather.

I know I should feel happy for her. She's my closest friend in the studio and she works harder than anyone I know. But all I can think about is how I spent two weeks on that application, reworking my portfolio statement, asking my old professor for a letter, calling my mother to say I might be in Japan for the spring so don't plan the family trip yet. All of that for a standard rejection email.

The worst part is I can't even be angry about it openly. Ji-hye keeps texting me details — the studio setup, the housing, the firing schedule — and I keep sending congratulations emojis and exclamation marks. My face hurts from performing enthusiasm on a screen. I closed the chat and went back to bed. It's not even noon.

Nudge: "What were you hoping would be different after Arita?"

Response: That I'd come back with something my family could point to. Studying under a master in Japan — that's something even my aunt would understand. It would have bought me time.

Went to the studio after moping around all morning. Didn't want to, but the pieces for the January show won't fire themselves. Started trimming the set of moon jars I threw last week. The proportions on the third one are actually close to what I've been chasing — that slight asymmetry halmeoni always said separates hand from machine.

Nobody cares about that distinction anymore, probably. The gallery visitors want to know the price and whether it fits on a shelf. But I held the jar up to the window light and the walls were even, the foot was clean, and the curve of the shoulder did exactly what I meant it to do. That matters to me even if it doesn't matter to anyone else.

Nudge: "Who taught you to look at the shoulder curve like that?"

Still at the studio. Eun-bi came by to pick up her tools and asked about the streetwear thing. I told her I'm not doing it and she looked at me like I'd said I don't like money. She said I should be practical, that these collaborations are how young artists build a name now. I told her I already have a name, it's just small.

She means well. But there's something about turning the craft into content that sits wrong with me. Halmeoni spent forty years in the same workshop selling to the same neighborhood. Nobody filmed her. Nobody asked her to put her glazes on handbags. She just made things that people used, and when she died the funeral was standing room only. That's the kind of career that makes sense to me.

End of the day. Checked my account and the last gallery payment was smaller than expected — they took a higher commission this quarter for "marketing costs." Didn't argue. Just transferred Umma's share and looked at what's left. The emergency cushion is thinner than I'd like going into winter.

Nudge: "How thin is thin?"

Response: Two months of rent and studio fees. Maybe two and a half if I skip materials. It's not nothing but it's not enough to stop the math running in my head at 2am.

Sunday morning, studio's empty, just me and the space heater humming. Loading the kiln for the bisque fire — the moon jars plus a few smaller bowls I made for the lunar new year gifts. Every year I make a set for the family, rice bowls with a celadon glaze my grandmother used to mix by feel. I have her ratios written down in a notebook but I still adjust by eye the way she showed me, adding a pinch more calcium when the humidity is high.

Fired up the kiln, set the cone, sat on the floor with tea. The studio smells like dust and wet earth and something burning off the shelves. Nothing urgent. Nothing broken. The jars are in there doing what they'll do and I can't control it from out here. So I'm just sitting. The heater clicks off and on. That's the whole update.

entries.journal_entry:
Can't stop thinking about today. There was this older man, Mr. Hernandez, and he reminded me so much of my tio — the way he called me "mija" and told me about his grandchildren back in Honduras. He needed his blood pressure medication but couldn't afford the copay, kept apologizing like he was asking for something unreasonable. And I just... I knew we have samples. I know where they are. I could have grabbed a month's supply and no one would have noticed or cared.

But I didn't. Followed protocol. Gave him the paperwork for financial assistance that takes six weeks to process. He thanked me anyway, squeezed my hand, said God would bless me for my kindness. Kindness. I filled out a form.

The rules exist for a reason, right? That's what my supervisor says. That's what I tell myself when I'm lying in bed at 3am staring at the ceiling instead of sleeping. The system works when everyone follows it. My abuela used to say you earn respect by doing things the right way, even when the right way is hard. She volunteered here for decades and never took a single bandaid home that wasn't accounted for.

But also — what is the system actually doing for Mr. Hernandez? He's 68, worked construction his whole life, and now he can't afford pills that cost the hospital pennies. I went into nursing because I wanted to help people. Real help. Not paperwork.

I keep telling myself I'll feel better about this tomorrow. I won't.

Nudge: "What would your abuela have done?"

Response: She would have followed the rules. She always did. But things were different then — there wasn't so much bureaucracy, people looked out for each other more. Anyway, it doesn't matter. I have to get ready for my shift.

Told my mom I can't come home for Christmas this year because of work. It's not entirely a lie — I could have requested off earlier. Just didn't.

Nudge: "Why didn't you?"

Response: Because part of me wanted an excuse. Everyone will be there — tias, primos, the whole family asking when I'm getting married, why I'm not going to church anymore, if I'm eating enough. I love them but I come back feeling like I'm failing at being who they expect me to be. At least at the hospital I know what I'm supposed to do.

Good shift today. We had a Guatemalan family come in — the grandmother only spoke K'iche' and was terrified. I don't speak it fluently but I know enough from my own abuela to say the important things: you're safe, we're going to help you, your family is right outside.

She grabbed my hand during the IV and prayed. I prayed with her, quietly, in Spanish. Felt right. The daughter cried when I came out to update them. Said they'd never been treated with such dignity at a hospital before. That's the job. That's why I'm here.

I hate the new scheduling software. It's supposed to be "fair" and "optimized" but it has me working Christmas Eve AND Christmas Day, back to back twelve hour shifts. Meanwhile Sarah who has no family obligations gets the whole week off because she requested it first. Cool. Great. Love that for her.

Called my mom to complain and she just said "Ay mija, that's how it is. At least you have a good job." Which I know is true. I know I'm lucky. I know people would kill for this position. But I'm allowed to be annoyed, right? I haven't had a Christmas off in three years. Three years of sending gifts instead of being there. Three years of video calls while everyone eats together.

Nudge: "What's the hardest part of not being there?"

Response: Watching them on the screen and knowing they're making memories without me. My little cousin is seven now and she barely knows who I am. Last year she asked my mom if I was the one who sends the presents. Not tia Valentina. Just "the one who sends presents." I'm becoming a stranger to my own family and I can't do anything about it because someone has to cover the holiday shifts and I'm the one without kids.

Worked sixteen hours. A man died on my floor — no family, no one to call. I held his hand at the end because no one should die alone. My family sent me a video of them opening presents and I couldn't watch it.

Nudge: "You could be there for him but not for them?"

Everyone's going out tonight. I'm eating leftover tamales my neighbor brought me and going to bed early. Told myself I'd call my mom at midnight her time but I'll probably be asleep. Another year of choosing this life over the one I was supposed to have.

Nudge: "What life were you supposed to have?"

Response: Married by now, probably. Living close to home, seeing my family every Sunday, raising kids who speak Spanish as their first language and know how to make tamales and go to mass. That's what my mom did, what her mom did. Instead I'm alone in an apartment eating someone else's cooking and pretending this is enough because I'm "making a difference." I don't know if I'm brave for building something new or just selfish for leaving everything behind.

entries.journal_entry:
Spent the whole afternoon with Abu Mahmoud at his farm outside Karameh. Three hours sitting on plastic chairs drinking tea while he showed me where the irrigation canal used to run before the allocation changed. His hands, the way he traced the old waterline in the dirt like drawing a map from memory. Forty years he worked that land. Now his grandson is driving trucks in Amman because there is nothing left to grow.

I recorded everything but the recording will not capture how quiet he got when I asked about the cooperative's petition. How he looked past me toward the orchards, half of them dried stumps now. Dr. Khalil wants regression models and policy impact matrices. What I have is Abu Mahmoud saying "we wrote letters until we ran out of paper." This is what I came here for. Not the degree, not the credentials. This.

Nudge: "What made you leave engineering for this?"

Response: Thirty years I designed systems to move water from where it was to where it was needed. Good work. Useful work. But always I was working for someone else's priorities, someone else's calculations about who matters. The last project, we diverted a whole tributary for a development that displaced six villages. I signed my name to those drawings. Came home and could not sleep for a week. Told my husband I needed to understand the other side of my own blueprints.

Committee meeting today. Dr. Khalil brought up timeline again, asked when I would have my methodology chapter finalized. I told him the fieldwork is taking longer because I am doing it properly, not rushing through interviews like they are survey forms. He gave me that look, the one he gives when he thinks I am being difficult. Said something about how at my stage of career I should know how to work within constraints.

At my stage. Meaning old. Meaning I should be grateful they let me in at all, should play by the rules without questioning. He does not say this directly but I hear it. The younger students in my cohort, they nod along, they revise when told to revise. Maybe that is smarter. Maybe I am wasting everyone's time insisting my approach matters. But every time I sit down to write the chapter his way, my hands just stop. Something in me refuses.

Nudge: "What specifically about his way makes your hands stop?"

Response: He wants me to treat the farmers as data points. Sample size, response rates, coding categories. I understand the logic, I am not stupid about methodology. But when I sit down to write it that way, I keep seeing Abu Mahmoud's face. He trusted me with his story. Reducing it to a variable in a regression feels like betraying that trust. Like I am doing the same thing I did as an engineer, just with different paperwork.

Roommates had people over until 2am again. Studied at the library until they kicked me out, then sat in my car in the parking lot reading by phone light. Called Farshad and he laughed, said I am the most stubborn person he knows.

Nudge: "What were you reading in the car?"

Response: Papers on participatory action research. Found this scholar from Brazil who worked with fishing communities fighting industrial pollution. Her whole methodology is about the community being partners in the research, not subjects. Reading her citations like following a trail. If Dr. Khalil will not accept my approach, maybe I can build enough of a foundation that he cannot dismiss it as naive.

Presented the participatory framework to the committee today. Dr. Khalil called it unconventional. Dr. Haddad said she found it compelling. Got approval to continue with modifications. Walked out of that room feeling like I had earned something that was mine.

entries.journal_entry:
Had my quarterly review today and Fatima said I exceeded expectations on every metric. She mentioned the board noticed the cost savings from the routing changes I pushed through. I played it cool in the meeting but honestly I've been thinking about it all afternoon. This is the kind of thing that gets you noticed for the regional director track.

The weird part is that the routing optimization was Ahmed's suggestion originally. I refined it and made it actually work, but still. I didn't bring that up in the meeting. He's been here eight years and reports to me — I'm not about to split credit and make myself look like I need hand-holding from my own team.

Nudge: "You refined it, but you know whose idea it was."

Response: Ahmed's got job security either way. I don't.
Spent the weekend at Atlantis with Youssef and Khalid. Pool day Saturday, Nobu Saturday night, recovery brunch Sunday. Needed that reset after the push to close Q1 numbers.

I'm so tired of Rami second-guessing everything I decide. Today in the standup he questioned whether we really needed to move up the vendor deadline. In front of everyone. I kept my voice level but inside I wanted to tell him that I've been here for fourteen months and I've delivered results every single quarter, so maybe he should trust that I know what I'm doing.

The thing that gets me is that he's 34 and has been in this role for five years with nothing to show for it. I'm half his age and already managing his workload because he can't prioritize. But I'm the one who has to justify my decisions? It's exhausting. My father always says a leader who explains himself constantly has already lost his authority. I'm starting to understand what he meant.

After the meeting I pulled Rami aside and told him if he has concerns, he can bring them to me privately. He apologized but I could tell he didn't mean it. Whatever. I'm not here to be liked. I'm here to deliver.

Nudge: "That's your father's rule. Is it yours?"

Response: I don't know. I've never really separated the two. My whole life, the men I respected ran their households and their businesses the same way — you make decisions, you don't ask permission, people follow or they don't. That's what being in charge looks like. But sometimes I wonder if they were actually happy, or if they were just... in control. Those aren't the same thing. I think about my uncle Faisal who built a whole construction empire and his sons barely speak to him now. He's still the boss though. So did he win?

Team dinner tonight, my pick, chose that new Lebanese place on JBR because I wanted good food and I wanted people to see us there. Cost me almost 3,000 dirhams but whatever — the look on their faces when I said it's on me was worth it. Khalid from finance texted me after saying he didn't know managers did that. I told him real leaders invest in their people.

Except now I'm home and my account is going to be tight until the 25th. The car payment comes out Thursday. I'll figure it out. Always do. Maybe I'll skip the boat party this weekend. Or maybe I'll just put it on the card and deal with it later. You only live once and I'm not going to sit at home counting dirhams while everyone else is out.

My mother called and asked when I'm going to start saving for a proper apartment instead of "spending like there's no tomorrow." I told her I'm building relationships that will pay off later. She doesn't understand how things work here. You can't network from a studio in Sharjah.

Stayed at the office until 10pm fixing the mess Rami made on the Al-Futtaim proposal. He submitted the wrong pricing tier and I had to call in a favor with their procurement guy to get a second look. It worked — we're still in the running — but I'm running on fumes.

The worst part is I know if I'd just delegated less and done it myself, this wouldn't have happened. But then everyone says I micromanage. So I step back and this is what I get. It feels like I can't win. Either I control everything and get called difficult, or I trust people and they drop the ball.

Ordered sushi to the office and ate alone at my desk. Not exactly the Thursday night I planned. Youssef sent photos from the rooftop at Five — I should've been there.

Nudge: "What would your Thursday have looked like if nothing went wrong?"

Response: Gym after work, quick shower at home, meet the guys atCia by 9, maybe end up at White or somewhere on the marina. Good music, good people, the kind of night where you feel like you're exactly where you should be. Instead I'm eating salmon rolls under fluorescent lights. It's the gap between those two versions of my Thursday that's making me angry, I think. Not Rami specifically.

Took myself to brunch alone today. First time in months I didn't organize it for a group or invite anyone. Just me, a table by the pool at Sofitel, unlimited sparkling and eggs royale. It felt strange at first — I kept checking my phone like I was waiting for someone. But by the third glass I was actually relaxed in a way I haven't been in weeks.

I've been thinking about why I always need an audience. Even good things feel less real if no one sees them. Like, if I have a nice meal alone, did it even happen? That's probably not healthy. My grandfather used to take long walks by himself every evening and he seemed at peace. I can't imagine doing that. The thought of unwitnessed time makes me uncomfortable.

But today was good. The food was good. The sun was good. Maybe I don't need everyone to know I was there. Maybe the experience is enough. I'm not convinced yet but I'm thinking about it.

Nudge: "What would you lose if no one saw?"

Response: Proof, I guess. That I'm living the life I'm supposed to be living. If no one sees it, how do I know it's real? How do I know I'm not just some guy eating alone because he has no one? The photos, the posts, the reservations at places with waitlists — that's evidence. Without it I'm just... trusting my own experience to be enough. And I don't know if I trust that yet.

Got the regional recognition for Q2 results. Fatima presented me with a plaque at the all-hands. Took a photo with the CEO. Already thinking about what's next.

Nudge: "You got what you worked for. How does it feel?"

Cancelled on Sara for the third time this month. She said she understands but her voice was flat. I told her work is crazy right now and she just said "it's always crazy." She's right. I don't know what to tell her.

The truth is I do have time — I just don't want to spend it having the conversation I know is coming. She wants to know where this is going. I want to keep things light. Those are incompatible. But I also don't want to lose her. She makes me look good. We look good together. That matters in this city.

I know that sounds terrible. She's also smart and funny and I like being around her. It's not just about appearances. But I can't say appearances aren't part of it either.

Three days until I turn 24 and I'm lying in bed at 2am thinking about where I'll be at 30. Regional director? Easy, if I keep this pace. But then what? I've been chasing this ladder since I was 19 and I've never actually pictured what's at the top. A corner office? More money? More people asking for my approval?

Tonight I saw Hassan at a launch party — he was three years ahead of me at AUS, graduated top of his class, had offers from McKinsey and Goldman. Now he runs a small dive shop in Fujairah. He looked... relaxed. Happy maybe. We talked for ten minutes and he didn't mention his job once except to laugh about how his commute is now a five-minute walk to the beach.

I thought I'd feel sorry for him. Instead I felt something closer to envy and I don't know what to do with that. His life is small by every metric I care about. No status, no visibility, probably makes less than my car payment. But he's not performing anything. He's just there, doing what he wants, and it seems like enough for him.

I can't imagine that being enough for me. And yet the fact that it bothers me is bothering me more.

entries.journal_entry:
Made the kheer for Amma's birthday exactly how Nani used to make it. The cardamom from the Indian shop on Belgrave Road, not the supermarket stuff. Sat there stirring for forty-five minutes, watching the milk thicken, and it felt right. Like the kitchen back home, like Nani's hands guiding mine when I was eight years old and barely tall enough to reach the stove.

Called Amma after. She cried a little when I described the smell. Said she could almost taste it through the phone. She asked when I was coming next, and I told her maybe Diwali if I can get the time off. She said she will save her sari for me to help her drape it, like I always do.

Nudge: "What is it about draping the sari together?"

Response: It is the only time I feel like her daughter again, not just someone who calls and sends money. She cannot do the pleats properly anymore, her hands shake. But she waits for me instead of asking the neighbour. It is our thing.

Double shift again because Meena called in sick. I do not mind covering, but by hour fourteen my feet were just throbbing and I kept thinking about how I had promised to video call Appa before his morning walk. He waits by the phone like a child waiting for sweets. I texted him instead, said I was still at work, and he just replied "okay beta" with that period at the end that always makes me feel like I have disappointed him even when he says nothing is wrong.

Reena stopped by the nurses' station around two in the morning with tea. She said I look tired, which is her way of saying I look like death. I laughed it off, but she is right. I have been covering too many shifts. The money is good—I put extra aside for Meera's wedding fund this month—but I cannot remember the last time I sat down for a proper meal that was not eaten standing in the break room.

The new doctor on rotation asked why I work so much. I did not know how to explain that it is not really a choice. My brother's shop in Ahmedabad is struggling, my parents' medical bills keep growing, and Meera's wedding will not pay for itself. This is what daughters do. But then I missed the call with Appa, and I wonder if I am getting the balance wrong. He will not be around forever, and I keep choosing the extra shifts.

Nudge: "Do you actually think you're getting it wrong, or is that the exhaustion talking?"

Response: Both, maybe. Sometimes I think the money is the excuse. Easier to send the transfer than to sit with the silence on the phone when Appa forgets what he was saying mid-sentence. The shifts fill the time I would otherwise spend worrying.

Sunita auntie came over uninvited, which is very her. She brought that horrible orange mithai from the temple and sat in my kitchen for two hours talking about how her nephew just got married to "a good Gujarati girl." Then she looked at Meera's photo on the fridge and asked if there was "any news."

I told her Meera is focusing on her career right now, and Sunita auntie made that face. The one where her eyebrows go up and she says "career is good, but..." I just kept refilling her chai until she left. Did not argue, did not defend. Just smiled and nodded because that is what you do. And honestly, I was not even angry. This is just how aunties are. Meera will find someone when Meera finds someone, and no amount of auntie pressure will change that. I have made my peace with it even if they have not.

Nudge: "How did you get to that peace?"

Response: Watching Meera with her patients. She is good at what she does, really good. I did not have that when I was her age—I went into nursing because my mother did and her mother did. Meera chose it. That matters more than a wedding date.

Hospital was busy with the usual winter rush. Three admissions on my shift, two respiratory cases and one fall. Paperwork took longer than the actual care, which is frustrating but normal. The new electronic system is meant to make things faster but everyone is still learning it.

Brought leftover rotli from yesterday for lunch. Ate at my desk while filling in charts. Raj called to say he would be late picking up the groceries so I stopped at Tesco on the way home. Nothing interesting, just milk and bread and the biscuits Appa likes for when he visits next.

Back-to-back shifts this week. Monday bled into Tuesday and now it is whatever day it is. Slept for four hours between them and dreamed I was still at work, which felt cruel. Woke up confused about whether the patient in room 4B had actually been discharged or if I had just imagined it in the dream.

Made daal for dinner because it is the only thing that requires no thought. Watched something on telly but could not tell you what. Raj asked if I wanted to go for the walk we used to take around the park but I just wanted to sit. He sat with me instead. Neither of us said much. Sometimes that is enough.

Skipped the temple this morning. Told Raj I was too tired, which was true, but also I just did not feel like sitting through the puja with everyone asking about the family and when we are going to India next. Felt guilty about it all day.

Nudge: "What's the guilt actually about?"

Had a patient today who reminded me why I do this. Mrs. Okonkwo, eighty-three, recovering from a hip replacement. Her family is all in Nigeria and she kept asking when someone would come. I sat with her during my break, just talking about nothing—the food, the weather, whether the NHS would survive another winter. She held my hand and called me "daughter."

I know I cannot replace her actual children, but I can be there for the fifteen minutes between her medication rounds. Brought her a cup of chai from the nurses' station even though it is not exactly protocol. She said it reminded her of something her mother used to make. We are not so different, Mrs. Okonkwo and I. Both far from home, both making do.

Nudge: "What does 'making do' look like for you?"

Had the conversation with Meera about Canada again. She has a job offer in Toronto, a good one, at a hospital that specializes in what she wants to do. I should be proud, and I am proud, but I also spent the whole dinner feeling like I was swallowing something sharp every time she talked about visa applications and apartment hunting.

She said I was being quiet and I said I was just tired. Which was not a lie, but it was not the whole truth either. The truth is I keep thinking about how I moved to England and left my parents behind and now my daughter wants to do the same to me. It is not fair to think that way. She has every right to build her life. I did the same thing.

But Amma stayed. My grandmother stayed. All the women in our family stayed close, within calling distance, within sari-draping distance, within "come over for chai" distance. And I broke that chain when I came here, and now Meera is breaking it further, and I cannot decide if this is progress or loss. Raj says I should be happy she is ambitious. I am happy. I am also something else that does not have a word.

Nudge: "What's the something else?"

Meera and I cooked together this evening. She asked me to teach her the dhokla recipe, the one from my mother's side that we only make for special occasions. Said she wanted to learn before she goes. We stood in the kitchen for two hours, me showing her how to test the batter, her writing everything down in that little notebook she keeps.

She asked about Nani, questions she has never asked before. What songs she used to sing, what her hands looked like, whether she was strict or soft. I found myself telling stories I had not thought about in years. The way Nani would wake before sunrise to pray. The way she saved the best pieces of mango for the grandchildren. Meera listened like she was memorising it all, and maybe she was. Maybe this is how it continues—not by staying in the same place, but by carrying the recipes and the stories wherever you go.

Nudge: "Do you believe that—that carrying the stories is enough?"

Response: Ask me again in a year when she is actually gone.

Sent the dhokla recipe to my mother. She called immediately, laughing, saying I had written down the measurements when she never measures anything. Told her Meera made her promise to keep it exactly as written. Three generations passing the same batter between their hands.

Nudge: "How did it feel, sending it?"

Response: Like handing over something I did not know I was holding. Heavy and light at the same time.

entries.journal_entry:
Had a small win today. Closed a bulk deal with a buyer in Shenzhen, margins were solid, the whole thing came together in like two hours from first DM to payment confirmed. These are the moments where I feel like I actually know what I'm doing. Not pretending, not faking it. Just reading the situation right and moving.

My mum asked about it at dinner and I gave her the short version. She nodded but didn't say much. A year ago that would've stung. Tonight it just felt fine. I don't need her to get it. The deal speaks for itself and I know what it took.

Nudge: "What's different about tonight?"

Spent the afternoon sourcing new drops, scrolling through pages of the same stuff everyone else is flipping. Getting harder to find edges. Might need to branch into vintage or accessories, something less saturated. Called James about it and he's keen to explore Taipei suppliers. Brain's moving fast today, not in a bad way, just restless.

Nudge: "Bored or scared of stalling?"

Response: Neither really. Just how my brain works, always scanning for the next angle. Anyway I need to finalize the Taipei logistics before I spiral into planning mode.

Auntie May came for dinner. The usual questions: when am I going back to school, do I have a real plan, what happens when this "internet thing" stops working. I smiled and gave polite non-answers. Changed the subject to her son's new job because I knew that would keep her busy for twenty minutes.

It's a skill at this point, steering conversations away from anything that makes them uncomfortable about me. I don't even get annoyed anymore. It's just the cost of sitting at that table without ruining everyone's evening. My cousin Jason does the same thing with his music career. We exchanged a look across the table. That was enough.

Dad asked me this morning if I'd consider taking a part-time role at Uncle Raymond's logistics company. Stable income, benefits, "good for your resume." I said I'd think about it. He looked relieved, which is probably the part that's bothering me most.

The business is actually growing. March numbers are tracking 40% above February. I have a potential partnership with a streetwear brand in Seoul that could change the whole trajectory. But none of that registers with him because it doesn't look like a job. I didn't bring up the Seoul thing. No point until there's a signed contract he can hold in his hands.

Nudge: "Are you thinking about it or just buying time?"

Been sitting with the uncle's company thing all day. Part of me knows the smart move is to say no and keep building. The numbers back me up. But there's this other part, the part that watched my dad's face relax when I said I'd consider it, and that part wants to just take the damn job so everyone can stop worrying.

It's not even about the money or the role. It's about what saying no does to the room. Every holiday, every family dinner, I'm the loose thread. The one who doesn't fit the pattern. And I can handle that most days but sometimes I wonder if I'm burning through goodwill I'm going to need later. Like what happens when I actually need their support for something and they've already written me off as the kid who wouldn't listen.

The Seoul partnership could be real. If it lands, it validates everything. But if it doesn't, I'm just the dropout who turned down a stable offer to chase another long shot. I keep toggling between these two versions of how this plays out and neither one lets me sleep.

Nudge: "What support are you actually afraid of losing?"

Response: Honestly, probably not financial. They'd help if things went really bad regardless. It's more like... being taken seriously. Right now I'm tolerated. If I keep going against what they want and it doesn't pay off spectacularly, I become the cautionary tale they tell my younger cousins. That's the version I can't stomach.

Mum just came into my room to say dad's feelings were hurt that I haven't given an answer about uncle's company yet. It's been twelve hours. Twelve. I told her I was still thinking and she gave me the look, the one that means she's already decided what the right answer is and is waiting for me to arrive at it.

I'm not being ungrateful. I know they're looking out for me. But every time they frame some fallback option as "just in case," what they're really saying is they don't think this works. And maybe they're right, but I need to figure that out on my own terms, not because I got guilted into hedging. Anyway. I told her I'd talk to dad tomorrow. That bought me another day at least.

entries.journal_entry:
Got back from the studio late and Baba was already asleep but Mama was waiting up. She didn't say anything about me being late, she just set out the leftover kibbeh and sat across from me while I ate. The whole time I could feel her watching me and I knew she wanted to ask about the commission meeting. I told her it went fine, which it did, mostly. The client wants something for his hotel lobby, large scale, blues and golds, very safe. I said I'd do it.

But the thing is I'd spent the whole morning working on something else entirely, this series I've been sketching for months. Faces in crowds, people looking away from each other. Disconnection in tight spaces. It's the most honest work I've done in years and I can't show it to anyone. Not because it's bad but because every time I imagine hanging it somewhere or putting it in a show I can already hear my uncle asking why I'm painting something so depressing, or my cousin making that face she makes when she doesn't understand something but doesn't want to say so. So instead I took the hotel lobby job and told myself the other work can wait. It can always wait.

I keep doing this. The safe thing first, then the real thing never.

Nudge: "You said the real thing never happens. What would have to change for it to?"

Response: I'd need to stop caring what people think, which is basically asking me to be a different person. Or I'd need someone to tell me it's okay, which is pathetic at my age.

Friday lunch was the usual production. My aunt brought her friend who has a daughter studying dentistry and somehow the conversation kept circling back to me and whether I'm seeing anyone. Mama smiled the whole time and said nothing, which is her way of agreeing without being the one to push. I just sat there eating and deflecting. "I'm focused on work right now." "Inshallah, when the time is right." All the correct answers.

My sister pulled me aside after and said I handled it well, which felt more like pity than praise. She knows I've been seeing someone casually, someone who isn't from the community, and she knows I'll never bring it up at Friday lunch. Not because I'm ashamed but because I can already map out the exact sequence of reactions and it's exhausting to even think about. My father asking quiet questions. My mother suddenly having a headache. My uncle making a joke that isn't a joke.

People think I'm easygoing because I never push back. The truth is I've just gotten very efficient at predicting which battles aren't worth starting. That's not the same thing as being at peace with it.

Nudge: "You said that's not the same as being at peace. What does the gap feel like?"

Response: Like holding your breath in a room full of people who think you're breathing normally. It's fine until it isn't.

Had a conversation with Rami at the studio today that's been sitting with me all evening. He got accepted into a group show in Beirut, contemporary stuff, political, boundary-pushing. They asked if I wanted to submit something too. I told him I'd think about it, which we both knew meant no.

He didn't push, which I appreciated, but later he said something offhand while cleaning his brushes. He said, "You're the most talented person I know who makes the least interesting work." He wasn't being cruel. He actually looked sad when he said it. I didn't know how to respond so I just laughed it off and changed the subject.

The thing is he's right. The hotel lobby commission is technically proficient and completely forgettable. The faces series sitting under the tarp in the corner of my studio is the best work I've ever done and no one has seen it. I keep telling myself I'm being strategic, building a reputation with the commercial work first, earning credibility. But at some point strategy becomes avoidance and I think I passed that line a while ago. I just don't know what to do about it because every option I can think of involves disappointing someone or drawing attention I'm not ready for.

Long day finishing preliminary sketches for the hotel commission. The client wants revisions already, more warmth, more welcoming. I nodded through the whole meeting and made notes. On the drive home I realized I'd been clenching my jaw for hours and my neck is completely locked up.

Mama called to remind me about my cousin's engagement party this weekend. I said I'd be there. Of course I'll be there. Everyone will be there. I'll wear the right shirt, say the right things, congratulate the right people. I'm good at this part.

Engagement party was fine. Loud. Lots of people I hadn't seen in months asking the usual things. How's the art going, meaning are you making money. Are you eating enough, meaning you look tired. When are you next, meaning why haven't you settled down. I smiled through all of it because that's what you do.

Danced with my mother at one point, which was actually nice. She was happy, genuinely, and I like seeing her like that. My father sat in the corner with the other men drinking coffee and nodding. He looked proud of my cousin. I wonder if he looks at me and feels something different, not disappointment exactly but maybe confusion. His son the painter. He's never said a negative word about it but he's also never asked to see my work. That silence says more than any criticism would.

After everyone left I helped stack chairs and my aunt squeezed my arm and told me I was such a good boy for always helping. I'm thirty-nine years old and I'm still being called a good boy. The worst part is I felt relieved by it. Like that's still the thing I'm chasing, the approval, the confirmation that I'm doing it right. Whatever "it" is.

Nudge: "You noticed feeling relieved at being called 'a good boy.' What does that tell you?"

Rami brought up the Beirut show again. He said the deadline was extended and there was still time to submit. I told him I appreciate him thinking of me but the timing doesn't work with the hotel commission. Which is technically true. But also I could find the time if I wanted to. I just don't want to deal with the aftermath of showing work that actually says something.

It's not cowardice. I know what it looks like from outside but it's more complicated than that. My family has a certain idea of what I do and who I am and disturbing that comes with a cost. Not a dramatic one, no one's going to disown me. It's subtler than that. It's the shift in how people look at you when you stop being predictable. I've seen it happen to other people and I've decided I'd rather keep things smooth. That's a choice, not a failure. At least that's what I keep telling myself.

Can't sleep. Spent the last two hours looking at Rami's work online, the pieces he's submitting to Beirut. They're raw and unfinished-looking on purpose and there's this energy in them that I used to have. I pulled out the faces series from under the tarp and laid them out on the studio floor and just stood there staring at them. They're good. I know they're good. That's not ego, that's honest assessment after twenty years of doing this.

I took a photo of the pieces laid out and almost posted it. My thumb was literally on the button. Then I imagined my mother scrolling past it and asking why the faces look so lonely, or my uncle forwarding it with a comment about how art these days has no beauty. So I put my phone down and covered everything back up.

I keep thinking about what Rami said. The most talented person he knows who makes the least interesting work. It's like a splinter I can't get out. If I showed the real work and people responded to it, everything would change. But if people in my life reacted badly, that would also change everything. I don't trust myself to handle that without losing something I need. The approval, the ease, the feeling of fitting in. People act like fitting in is small but when you've built your entire life around it, it becomes the foundation. I'm terrified of what happens if I pull a brick out.

Nudge: "What would pulling that brick out actually look like, concretely?"

Response: Submitting to a show, probably. Or just leaving the tarp off the canvases when my family visits the studio. Small things. Anyway, I need to sleep.

Something shifted today, or maybe it's been shifting for a while and I only noticed now. I was working on the hotel commission, painting this massive blue gradient that's supposed to evoke the sea, and I caught myself adding tiny figures along the bottom edge. Almost invisible unless you're close. Faces turned away from each other, like my other series. I painted over them before anyone could see but for a few minutes they were there and the piece was better for it.

My sister came by the studio to drop off food Mama had made. She lingered, looking at canvases against the wall. She pointed at one of the commercial pieces and said, "This is pretty." Then she pointed at the corner where the faces series was partially visible under the tarp and said, "What's that one?" I told her it was nothing, just experiments. She said it looked interesting and I should show her sometime.

That's the thing about my sister. She asks without pushing. If I ever show the real work to anyone in my family, it'll start with her. Not because she'll necessarily understand it but because she won't make me feel wrong for having made it. That counts for more than most people realize.

I drove home feeling lighter than I have in weeks. Nothing has actually changed. I still didn't submit to Beirut. But someone in my family expressing even mild curiosity about the work I actually care about made me feel less like I'm carrying this alone.

Delivered the hotel commission today. Client was thrilled. Shook my hand and said it was exactly what he envisioned, which I think is the most depressing compliment I've ever received.

Nudge: "Why does hearing 'exactly what I envisioned' feel depressing to you?"

Took my sister up on her offer. Showed her three of the faces pieces at the studio today. She looked at them for a long time without saying anything. Then she said, "These are different." I waited for the follow-up, the concern, the suggestion to maybe not show Baba. Instead she said, "You should make more of these."

I didn't know what to do with that so I made us tea and we sat on the studio floor talking about other things. But the pieces stayed uncovered the whole time. I didn't put the tarp back until after she left. Small thing. But I noticed it.

Nudge: "You noticed you left the tarp off. What do you think that means for you?"

Response: Maybe I'm getting tired of hiding the work. Or maybe I just needed one person to see it and not flinch. Either way the tarp felt heavier putting it back on than it used to.

entries.journal_entry:
Staff meeting today went sideways. The principal announced they're changing the Class III syllabus mid-term and everyone just nodded along like this was normal. I sat there wanting to say something because I've already prepped my unit plans through June and this throws everything off. But Mrs. Kulkarni was agreeing, and she's been teaching twenty-three years, so what do I know. I just kept quiet and wrote notes.

After the meeting Neha—she joined the same year as me—asked if I thought the change made sense. I said it was fine, it'll be fine. She looked at me like she knew I was lying. I probably was. The thing is, even if I did speak up, what would I say? "Excuse me, ma'am, I disagree"? That's not how it works here. You're new, you keep your head down, you follow the lead of people who've been around longer. That's what Amma always says.

But walking home I kept replaying it and feeling this tight, hot thing in my chest. Not anger exactly. More like frustration at myself for not even trying. I had a valid point and I swallowed it whole. I do this constantly. In meetings, at family dinners, everywhere. I just smile and go along with things and then come home and feel this restless itch like I've betrayed something. I don't even know what.

Nudge: "What do you think you betrayed?"

Response: Maybe my own opinion? Like the fact that I even had one. Everyone assumes I'm just agreeable by nature but sometimes I have things I want to say and I just... don't. And then it's like those thoughts don't count because I never let them out. It's exhausting pretending you don't have a voice.
Quiet week at school. Adjusted the unit plans to fit the new syllabus. Amma said she was proud of me for not making a fuss.

Parent-teacher meetings ran until seven tonight. I'm so tired I can barely think straight. Had a difficult conversation with one father who was upset about his son's handwriting marks. He kept raising his voice and I kept saying "I understand, uncle" over and over like a broken toy. Mrs. Kulkarni was in the next room, she could hear everything, and afterwards she said I handled it well. "You didn't escalate," she said, like that's the highest praise.

And honestly, part of me is glad I stayed calm. That's how you're supposed to handle these things. You listen, you don't react, you let the other person feel heard. Amma taught me that. Baba would say the same—don't give anyone a reason to complain about you.

But another part of me wanted to tell that man his son's handwriting isn't poor because of me, it's because the boy doesn't practise at home. I had the words right there. I didn't say them. I never do. Came home, ate dinner in silence, helped Bhabhi clear the dishes. Now I'm lying in bed typing this and I can still hear his raised voice in my head. I wish it didn't stick.

Nudge: "What is it about raised voices that sticks with you?"

Response: I think it's because I associate them with something going wrong. Like if someone raises their voice at me, I've failed at keeping things smooth. Which I know is ridiculous because he was being unreasonable. But my whole body just goes into this mode where I have to fix it, calm it down, make it okay. I've been like this since I was small. Loud voices in the house meant someone was unhappy and it was everyone's job to make it stop.

Dadiji called today to discuss Rakshabandhan plans even though it's months away. She wants everyone at her place in Nashik, full family, all the cousins, the usual production. I said of course, yes, we'll be there, before even checking with Baba or Amma. Didn't even occur to me to say maybe or let me check. It was automatic, like a reflex I didn't know I had.

Neha invited me to a weekend workshop on creative teaching methods in Mumbai that same weekend. I said I couldn't go without even thinking about it. She asked why and I just said family commitments. She didn't push but I could tell she wanted to.

Nudge: "Did you want to go to that workshop?"

Response: It doesn't really matter, does it? Dadiji's plans are set. You don't skip family things for a workshop. That's just how it is.

Something happened at lunch today. A group of the senior teachers were discussing a student in my class—Arjun, the one who's been acting out. They were saying he needs stricter discipline, maybe a note home to his parents. I know Arjun. His parents are going through a separation and he's been coming to school with unwashed uniforms. He doesn't need punishment, he needs someone to be patient with him.

I almost said something. I opened my mouth and Mrs. Deshpande looked at me and I just said "yes ma'am, I'll send the note." I felt sick walking back to my classroom. Still do.

Nudge: "You said you felt sick—what part exactly?"

Response: Because I know I'm right about Arjun and I still didn't say it. It's one thing to stay quiet about syllabus changes or meeting agendas—that's just politics. But this is a child. I'm supposed to be looking out for him. Instead I folded because a teacher with more years looked at me a certain way. What kind of teacher does that make me?

Writing again because I can't sleep. I keep thinking about Arjun and the note I'm supposed to send. I haven't written it yet. Part of me wants to just talk to his mother directly, gently, without framing it as a disciplinary thing. I could say something like "I've noticed he seems distracted, is everything okay at home." That's not disobeying anyone. That's just being a teacher.

But then I imagine Mrs. Deshpande finding out I went around her suggestion and the thought makes my stomach drop. She's the one who writes my probation review. She's been teaching since before I was born. Who am I to decide my approach is better?

And yet I keep coming back to Arjun's face when I told the class to take out their notebooks yesterday. He didn't have his. He just sat there looking small. I smiled at him and quietly gave him a spare. Nobody noticed. That felt right. The note home doesn't feel right. But doing what feels right and doing what you're told are two different things and I don't know when I got so afraid of the gap between them.

Spoke to Arjun's mother after school today, just casually near the gate. Kept it light, asked how things were going. She got teary and said it's been a hard few months. I told her I'd keep an eye on him and she held my hand and thanked me. I didn't mention the note.

Mrs. Deshpande asked if I'd sent it. I said I spoke with the mother directly and she seemed satisfied. Mrs. Deshpande paused, then said "fine." It wasn't warm but it wasn't a reprimand either. I survived it. My heart was pounding the entire time though.

Friday and I'm sitting on the balcony while everyone watches that serial on TV. Bhabhi asked if I wanted to join and I said in a minute but I just want to sit here with the noise from the street and not talk to anyone for a bit.

This week has been strange. The Arjun thing—I keep turning it over. I did something different. I didn't just follow orders. I found a way to do what I thought was right without directly confronting anyone. Maybe that's the only way I can operate. Not rebellion, not silence, something in between. Like, I bent the expectation without breaking it. And nothing bad happened. Mrs. Deshpande didn't yell at me, Arjun's mother was grateful, the world didn't end.

I think I've been telling myself a story that any deviation from what's expected will result in disaster. Like if I push back even slightly, everything will collapse—my reputation, my relationships, my family's opinion of me. But today I'm sitting here and nothing collapsed. The serial is still playing, Bhabhi is still laughing, Amma just brought me chai without asking. Everything is the same except I feel a tiny bit less compressed. Like there's a millimeter more room inside my chest.

Nudge: "That millimeter of room—what does it feel like?"

Cousin Meera announced she's moving to Bangalore for a job. Whole family is upset. Dadiji called it selfish. I sat there thinking she's brave but I said nothing because everyone was already piling on.

Nudge: "You thought she was brave?"

Response: I mean, it takes guts to do something your family doesn't approve of. That's all. I'm not saying she's right or wrong. It's not my place.
Amma asked me at breakfast if I'd ever "do something like Meera." I said no, of course not. She looked relieved. I don't know why that relief felt like a small door closing.

End of term assessments all week. I'm marking papers every night until midnight and still helping Bhabhi with the kids in the morning because she has a cold. I don't mind helping—that's what family does. But I haven't had an hour to myself in I don't know how long. Every minute is either school or home duties.

Neha asked me to grab chai after school and I said I couldn't because I had marking to finish. That was true but also I knew if I went home late Amma would ask where I was and I'd have to explain and it just felt like more effort than it was worth. Sometimes the path of least resistance is the only path I have energy for.

Nudge: "What would you have talked about with Neha if you'd gone?"

Response: Probably everything I can't say at home. She's the only person who gets it—being new, being young, trying to figure out if you're a good teacher or just an obedient one. Last time we talked she said she argued with the vice principal about textbook choices and won. I was equal parts impressed and horrified. I wanted to ask how she does it, how she speaks up without her whole body shutting down. But I ran out of time.

Report cards went home yesterday and a parent emailed the principal to say I graded her daughter too harshly in Hindi. The principal called me in and asked me to "reconsider." Not directly, not as an order—just that little tilt of the head and "maybe you could take another look." I know what that means. It means change the grade.

The grade is accurate. I checked three times. The girl is struggling with grammar and I wrote detailed comments about how to improve. But the parent is on the school trust and the principal doesn't want problems. So now I'm sitting here trying to decide whether to bump a B+ to an A- to keep everyone comfortable.

I hate this. This is exactly the kind of thing I got into teaching to fight against—marking a child on who her parents are instead of what she's learned. But if I refuse, the principal remembers. My probation review is in August. Is one grade worth the trouble?

I already know what I'm going to do. I'm going to change it and tell myself it doesn't matter in the long run, and feel this same sick feeling I felt about Arjun, and go to bed and wake up tomorrow the same person who keeps the peace at any cost. And one day I'll wonder when I stopped trusting my own judgment. Or maybe I'll just stop noticing. That's the scarier thought.

Nudge: "What would it take for you not to change that grade?"

Response: Someone to tell me it's okay to hold my ground. Which is pathetic because I shouldn't need permission. But I've spent my whole life being told that keeping things smooth is the right thing to do, and when every single person around you believes that, it's hard to trust the one voice saying otherwise—especially when that voice is just mine.

entries.journal_entry:
App keeps glitching today, lost two rides because of it. My head's all over the place.

Nudge: "Sounds like a frustrating day. What's weighing on you most right now?"

Mom called asking if I could cover her prescription copay this month since Dad's physical therapy bills are piling up. Of course I said yes but then I looked at my account and it's like, where is this money supposed to come from? I'll figure it out, I always do.

Nudge: "You said yes right away, then checked your account. What was going through your mind in that moment between?"

Twelve-hour day yesterday and another one today. Holiday surge is good for earnings but my back is killing me from sitting in the car so long. At least I was able to drop off the pharmacy money to Mom on my way between zones. She tried to give me back half of it and I told her no, just keep it in case something else comes up.

Had a passenger tonight who was heading to the airport for some tropical vacation and I sat there thinking about how I haven't taken more than a day off in probably six months. Not complaining really, it is what it is. Just tired.

Nudge: "Six months without a real break. If you could take a day off tomorrow, what would you actually do with it?"

Response: Honestly? I'd probably sleep until noon. Maybe drive out to the mountains just to sit somewhere quiet without my phone buzzing. But then I'd probably spend the whole time calculating how much money I'm losing by not working, so maybe that defeats the purpose.

Sister texted asking if I'm coming to Christmas dinner at her place this year. I want to say yes but I'm looking at the schedule and Christmas Eve and Christmas Day are usually the best earning days because everyone else takes off. Last year I worked both and just stopped by Mom and Dad's for an hour between rides.

Part of me thinks I should just take the hit and be there for the whole dinner. Dad's been doing better but who knows how many of these we get, you know? But then another part of me is like, one good day of driving could cover half my car insurance payment. I don't know. I'll probably end up doing what I always do which is work until the last possible minute and then rush over stressed and half-present anyway.
