entries.journal_entry:
Had the tech lead conversation with Martijn today. He laid out what the role would look like — running standups, making architecture calls, representing the team in cross-department meetings. I said I was interested, which is true, but the whole time I was thinking about how I'd actually handle it. I build things well when people leave me alone to think. That's not what this role is.

But I also know I did the right thing saying yes. Everyone on the team expects it, Noor would think I was insane to turn it down, and frankly it would look odd to pass on a promotion you're being handed. I can figure out the rest as I go. Probably.

Nudge: "What part of it actually interests you?"

Long week. Spent most of it pairing with the new hire, walking her through the codebase and our deployment pipeline. Didn't get to touch my own backlog at all but she's ramping up faster than the last person we onboarded so I'll take it.

Nudge: "Was it worth not having your own time?"

Sprint retro got a bit heated. Devika suggested we rethink the approval flow for deployments because she thinks it slows us down. She's right, technically — it does add a half-day lag. I had a whole alternative sketched in my head while she was talking, a lighter-weight check that would still catch the critical stuff.

Didn't say it though. The senior architects set up the current flow and they were in the call. Felt like bringing up a redesign right there would come across as me trying to show off, especially with the tech lead thing floating around. So I just nodded and said I could look into Devika's point offline. She seemed a bit deflated that nobody backed her up more directly. I get it.

Weird day. Started normal — coffee, standup, tickets. Then around lunch Pieter from platform team pinged me about a side project he's been hacking on, this internal tool for tracking deployment health across services. He wanted to know if I'd help build the backend. Not sanctioned work, just something he thinks would be useful and wants to pitch to leadership once it's built.

First thought was yes obviously, this is the kind of thing I actually want to spend time on, building something new without twelve layers of sign-off. Second thought was that Martijn would not love me spending cycles on an unsanctioned project right when he's evaluating me for lead. Third thought was that Pieter didn't ask anyone else, he asked me, and that felt good in a way I don't fully understand. Like someone saw the part of me that wants to just build.

I told Pieter I'd think about it over the weekend. Which is code for "I want to do it but I need to figure out if I'm allowed to want to do it." That sounds ridiculous written down. I'm a grown man deciding whether to write some code on the side. But it feels more loaded than that right now.

Told Pieter I'd help with the tool. We set up a repo and I spent an hour last night after Noor went to sleep scaffolding the API layer. Felt more engaged in that hour than I have all sprint on the actual work.

Today was back to normal though — reviewing PRs, answering Slack threads, sitting through a planning session where half the decisions had already been made before we walked in. The usual. I'm not complaining, it's fine. I just noticed the contrast, that's all.

Nudge: "You said it's fine twice now. Is it?"

Response: Yeah. It's work, it doesn't all have to be exciting.

Noor and I went to dinner with her parents in Rotterdam on Saturday. Her dad asked about the promotion and I gave the standard answer — it's in progress, I'm grateful for the opportunity, etc. He seemed pleased. On the train back Noor said she was proud of me and I felt genuinely good about it. Like I'm on a track that makes sense to the people I care about.

Then on Sunday I stayed up way too late working on Pieter's tool and got completely absorbed refactoring the data ingestion layer. Noor asked what I was doing and I said "just work stuff" which wasn't really a lie but wasn't the whole truth either. I don't know why I didn't just explain it. Maybe because explaining it would mean admitting how much more alive that project makes me feel, and then someone might ask what that says about everything else.

Nudge: "What would it mean if you did explain it to her?"

Response: Probably nothing bad. She'd understand. I think the issue is more that saying it out loud makes it real — that the path everyone's excited about for me isn't the one that lights me up. And then I'd have to figure out what to do with that.

Martijn officially put me on the lead shortlist. Two candidates, me and someone from the other squad. I thanked him and said I'd put my best foot forward. Which I will. Whatever doubts I have aren't anyone else's problem to carry.

Team drinks after work. Everyone was congratulating me about the shortlist like it's already decided. I kept saying it wasn't confirmed yet but nobody listens. Devika made a joke about me being her future boss and I laughed but it sat wrong with me for the rest of the evening.

I don't think I'm being fake about it. I genuinely want to do well, and the role comes with better pay, more visibility, things that matter. But there's this gap between how I talk about it to other people and what actually runs through my head at 11pm when I'm staring at Pieter's repo. Not sure what to do with that. For now I'm doing both, which is sustainable enough.

Presented the deployment health tool to Pieter's skip-level manager today. She liked it. Asked if we'd want to bring it under the platform roadmap officially. Said yes without hesitating.

Nudge: "That was quick for someone who usually thinks things over."

Response: Some things don't need overthinking.

Martijn pulled me aside after standup. Apparently someone mentioned to him that I've been spending time on Pieter's tool and he wanted to know if it was affecting my sprint commitments. It isn't — I've been keeping my velocity up — but the way he framed it made it sound like a warning. "Just want to make sure your priorities are clear," he said.

I told him it wouldn't be an issue and that the tool is now being folded into the platform roadmap anyway, so it's legitimate work. He nodded and dropped it. But I've been replaying the conversation all afternoon. The rational part of me knows he was just doing his job. The other part feels like I got caught doing something I wasn't supposed to enjoy. I hate that I felt the need to justify it. I hate more that I did justify it, immediately, without pushing back at all.

Nudge: "What would pushing back have looked like?"

Response: Saying something like "I'm delivering on everything you've asked, so what I do with my remaining time is my call." Which is true. But I keep imagining how that would land — confrontational, difficult, not a team player. So I default to the smooth version. And then I'm annoyed at myself for it later, which is its own kind of exhausting.
Got the tech lead offer. Accepted on the spot because that's what you do. Texted Noor and she sent about fifteen celebration emojis. Calling my parents tonight.

Same day, later. Parents were thrilled, mum teared up a little. Had a glass of wine with Noor and she kept saying she knew it would happen. Everyone's reaction confirmed that this was the right move, or at least the expected one, and those two things feel like they should be different but tonight they're the same and I'm too tired to untangle it.

I keep coming back to something small. When Martijn told me, my first feeling wasn't excitement. It was relief. Relief that it's done, that I don't have to hold the performance anymore, that I can stop wondering and just be in the role. Maybe excitement comes later. Or maybe relief is enough. I genuinely don't know and I'm going to stop thinking about it and go to bed.

entries.journal_entry:
Arjun's old company reached out to him about a relocation package to Bangalore. Good salary, housing allowance, school fees covered for Meera. He brought it up over dinner like it was just information, but I could tell he was testing the water. If we moved, I'd have to give up my lab placement and start over somewhere, if I even could.

The sensible thing would have been to say let's talk about it seriously. He's been carrying us financially for four years now and the offer was generous. Instead I just said I'm not ready to leave yet. He didn't push it. But I spent the rest of the night doing the math in my head — how much longer the PhD will take, how much we've burned through savings, whether Meera even remembers what it was like when we weren't watching every expense. I keep choosing this and I don't fully know why anymore.

Nudge: "What would finishing feel like, if not relief?"

Response: Like proof that it was worth it. That I didn't just burn four years and all our savings on something selfish. But sometimes I wonder if I'm just trying to outlast the doubt at this point, like if I keep going long enough the answer will show up on its own.

Submitted the revised chapter draft to my supervisor this morning. Picked Meera up from after-school care, stopped at the wet market for vegetables, made dal and rice for dinner. Nothing remarkable. Normal day.

Meera drew a picture of me at my desk with all these little papers flying around and wrote "Amma working hard" underneath. I stuck it on the wall next to my monitor. Some days that's enough to keep going.

Got an email today from a former colleague, Deepa, about a senior position opening at the water authority back in Chennai. Permanent role, government pension, the whole thing. She said she thought of me immediately. My mother would cry with happiness if I took it. Appa would finally stop asking pointed questions about "what the plan is." Arjun's parents would stop whispering about how their son married a woman who can't settle down.

I sat with the email open for maybe twenty minutes. I could picture the whole life — the flat in Adyar, Meera going to the same school I went to, weekend lunches at my parents' house. Predictable. Comfortable. Everything mapped out for the next thirty years. And I felt this thing in my chest, not panic exactly but something close. Like the walls of that life were already closing in even though I haven't said yes to anything. I know the stipend is barely enough. I know Arjun is tired of being the only one earning properly. I know my savings are basically gone. I know all of this.

But I closed the email and went back to my data. I'll probably tell Deepa thanks but not now, maybe later, which is what I always say. The part I can't explain to anyone is that the uncertainty here feels more honest than the certainty there. That sounds ridiculous when I say it out loud but there it is.

Nudge: "What does 'honest' mean here — honest about what?"

Response: About what I actually want, I think. The Chennai job would be fine. Good, even. But it would be someone else's version of my life. The one everyone expected me to have. Here I'm struggling but at least the struggle is mine.
Lab equipment broke down again. Spent the whole day troubleshooting instead of running samples. Came home to Meera already asleep and leftover rice in the cooker.

Amma called tonight and casually mentioned that Deepa's husband just bought a new flat in OMR. Then she said, "You know Deepa was asking about you, whether you've thought about that position." So apparently the whole family knows now. Great.

I told her I'm focused on my research and she did that thing where she goes quiet for a few seconds and then says "of course, of course, we just worry." Arjun was listening from the other room and afterward he said maybe we should at least talk about a timeline. He wasn't being pushy about it but the fact that he's now aligned with my mother is — I don't know. I told him my timeline is when I finish and he said that's not a timeline, that's a hope. He's not wrong. But the alternative is giving up something I've worked four years on because everyone else is tired of waiting. I'm tired too. But tired isn't the same as done.

Nudge: "What would it take for tired to become done?"

Had a good meeting with my supervisor today. She said the fieldwork chapter is strong and that the analysis is heading in the right direction. First time in weeks I've felt like I actually know what I'm doing. She also mentioned a conference in March where I could present, which would be good for the CV.

Afterward I walked to the hawker centre near campus and got mee goreng and just sat there eating by myself for a while. It was raining and the whole place smelled like wet concrete and frying oil. I watched the aunties running the stalls — same thing every day, same dishes, same rhythm — and I thought about how there's a kind of steadiness in doing work you've chosen, even if nobody else sees why you chose it.

I called Arjun on the way home and told him about the conference. He sounded genuinely happy for me, which I needed more than I realized. I know this path doesn't make sense on paper. The money is bad, the timeline is uncertain, and every aunty and uncle back home thinks I've lost the plot. But today felt like one of those days where I remembered why I started. That has to count for something.

entries.journal_entry:
My hands are still shaking from the critique session. Professor Mansour put my naskh panel up on the wall next to everyone else's work and asked the class, "Where is the artist in this?" Like I was just copying. Like three months of grinding ink and cutting reeds and adjusting every single letter weight was nothing. Somebody said it looked like something you'd find in a mosque gift shop. People laughed.

I didn't say anything. I sat there and took it. Walked straight to the bathroom after and just stood there gripping the sink. The worst part is I keep replaying it and I still can't figure out what I should have said. I know they don't get it. But that doesn't make the laughing stop echoing in my head.

Nudge: "What would you have said if you could go back?"

Response: That the script isn't decoration. That every stroke has a genealogy — my jiddo's hand over mine, guiding the qalam for years. That I didn't choose this style from a catalogue. But I don't know if saying that would have changed anything. They'd probably still just see an old alphabet on paper.

Spent the morning helping Teta Hana sort through jiddo's old papers again, she wants to frame some of his practice sheets, the ones with his warmup strokes, hundreds of little alifs in neat rows fading where the ink ran dry. She held one up and just smiled and didn't say anything. Put it aside for me.

Then I go to studio and Layla is laser-cutting plexiglass and Rami's doing something with projectors and sand and I'm sitting there with my inkwell and bamboo and I keep thinking do I look like a museum exhibit to them, like am I doing something real or am I just holding onto something because letting go would feel like — I don't know. I keep not finishing that thought.

Nudge: "Feel like what?"

Response: Like forgetting him. Like those practice sheets stop meaning anything. I know that doesn't make logical sense, I could still remember jiddo without doing calligraphy. But it would feel like I walked away from the table where he taught me and never came back.

Professor Mansour pulled me aside after class and said she sees potential in my work but wants me to "break the script apart." She used words like deconstruct, fragment, subvert. She wants me to take the naskh letters and tear them up, layer them over industrial textures, make them unrecognizable. She said it would be a strong thesis direction.

I told her I'd think about it. Walked home the long way through Gemmayze. Something about the idea makes my stomach tight. Not because it's technically hard. I could do it. I know how to cut letterforms apart and reassemble them. It's that I keep picturing jiddo watching me do it. And Teta seeing the result on a gallery wall and not being able to read a single word. I said I'd think about it and I will, but the thinking itself feels wrong.

Everyone keeps acting like I'm afraid of experimenting. I'm not afraid. I just don't think ripping something apart is the only way to prove you understand it.

Nudge: "What would proving it look like for you?"

Had dinner at Uncle Fadi's last night and my cousin Reem asked about my thesis. I said calligraphy and she nodded like that was obvious, like of course Nadia's doing that, and I wanted to snap at her. I don't know why. She wasn't being mean. She was being supportive. But the way she said "of course" made me feel like a fixed point everyone's already mapped. Like no one wonders what I'd do if I chose freely.

Then mama brought up jiddo and told the story about him lettering the sign for the pharmacy on Rue Hamra, how the owner cried when he saw it. Everyone smiled. I smiled too. It's a good story. I love that story.

But I sat there holding my plate thinking about Professor Mansour's suggestion and how if I told this table I was going to fragment the script and collage it over concrete textures, my grandmother would go quiet in that particular way she does when she's hurt but won't say so. And my mother would change the subject. And I'd sit there knowing I made a room full of people uncomfortable for the sake of a grade and a gallery slot. So I won't do it. But I keep circling back to the fact that I want to, or at least part of me does, and I can't tell if that part is ambition or betrayal.

Nudge: "You said part of you wants to — which part?"

Response: I don't know. Can we not do this right now.

I did something small today that I keep thinking about. I was in the studio early, nobody else there yet, and I pulled out a fresh sheet and started writing the bismillah in naskh the way jiddo taught me. Perfect form, clean lines. Then without really deciding to, I took a second sheet and wrote it again but let the letters drift apart, stretched the connections, let the lam curve wider than it should. It still read as the phrase. Just looser.

I stared at both sheets for a long time. The second one wasn't ugly. It wasn't disrespectful. It was the same words with more air in them. Teta could still read it. Jiddo would have recognized the hand.

I don't know what this means for my thesis. I rolled both sheets up and put them in my bag. I haven't shown anyone. Part of me thinks if I show Professor Mansour she'll want me to go further, push harder, and I'll end up somewhere I didn't agree to. And part of me thinks if I show Teta she'll look at the second sheet and ask why I'm being careless. So for now they're mine. Two versions of the same prayer, sitting in my bag, and I'm the only one who knows about both.

entries.journal_entry:
My mother-in-law rearranged Sami's entire nap schedule without asking me. She told Tariq it was better for the baby to sleep earlier so she could take him to the park in the afternoon. Nobody asked what I thought. I had finally gotten him into a rhythm that gave me two hours in the evening to work on a logo project. That's gone now.

I stood there in the kitchen holding a cup of tea and I just didn't say anything. Tariq looked at me like he expected me to agree. His mother was already moving the crib blanket to the other room. I could feel my jaw clenching but I smiled. I hate that I smiled. Something about the way everyone just decided felt like they forgot I'm his mother, not a guest in this arrangement.

Nudge: "You smiled but didn't want to. What stopped you?"

Sami had a fever all night. I was up every two hours. Tariq slept through it. His mother came in at 5am and took over, told me to rest. I should be grateful but it felt like being dismissed. I'm the one who knows what dose of medicine to give, what temperature means we go to the clinic. I didn't rest.

Nudge: "What would the right kind of help look like?"

Response: If she had asked. Just asked. "Can I sit with him so you can sleep?" instead of walking in and lifting him out of my arms. It's not the help. It's that nobody checks if I want it first.

Sami's fever broke. Normal day otherwise. Did laundry, made lentil soup, swept the hallway because nobody else notices the dust. Tariq came home and asked what's for dinner. I said soup. He said his mother already made something. So the soup is in the fridge now, I guess.

Finished a draft of the logo I've been working on. The client wants revisions but at least the base concept is solid. I had to work on it in the bathroom because Sami was asleep in the bedroom and the living room was occupied. Sat on the edge of the bathtub with my laptop balanced on the hamper. It's ridiculous but it's the only place I can close a door without anyone asking what I'm doing.

Can barely keep my eyes open. Sami is teething and it's been three nights of broken sleep. I had to email the client and ask for an extension on the logo revisions. Felt pathetic typing that out. My mother called to say my cousin Rania is getting engaged and when am I bringing Sami to visit. Everything requires something from me and I have nothing left to give right now.

Eid preparations are consuming the entire house. Tariq's mother has been cooking since dawn. She made ma'moul and knafeh and keeps sending me to the store for things she forgot. I don't mind the errands but she keeps correcting how I arrange the pastries on the tray. Told me that's not how they do it in their family. It's been two years, I still arrange things wrong apparently.

Tariq gave me money to buy a new outfit for Eid. I picked something I liked -- a dark green dress with embroidery on the sleeves. When I showed it to his mother she said it was pretty but maybe something more "appropriate for a young mother" would be better. I don't even know what that means. I put the dress in my closet anyway. I'll wear it.

My own mother sent a voice note about how she wishes I could spend Eid at their house this year. First Eid is always with the husband's family, she knows that, but she said it anyway. So now I feel guilty about being here and guilty about not being there. Sami won't remember any of this. I'm doing all of it for people who have opinions about trays and dresses.

Nudge: "You said you'll wear the dress anyway. What's riding on that?"

Response: It's just a dress. I liked it, I bought it, I'll wear it. Not everything has to be a statement.

Later. Wore the dress. Tariq said I looked nice. His mother didn't comment. My sister-in-law Huda actually asked where I got it. Sami grabbed the embroidery and pulled a thread loose, which honestly made me laugh. Small thing but I felt like myself for the first time in weeks. I held Sami on my hip and I felt steady.

Got the payment for the logo project. Not a lot -- 45 JD -- but I earned it. Put it in the envelope I keep in my underwear drawer. I have about 200 JD saved now. I don't know what I'm saving for exactly. Maybe a better laptop. Maybe just proof that I can do something that isn't cooking and wiping noses.

Tariq doesn't know about the envelope. I'm not hiding it because I think he'd be angry, more because he'd want to "help manage it" and then it wouldn't feel like mine anymore. My father always handled my mother's money. She'd get her teacher's salary and hand it over and he'd decide what the household needed. She never complained about it. I think about that a lot. I don't want to not complain about it. I want a reason not to need to.

Nudge: "What changes if Tariq finds that envelope?"

Response: He'd probably be fine with it. Supportive even. But then it becomes a shared thing, a family decision. Right now it's the only thing in this house that belongs to just me. I'm not ready to give that up. Maybe that's selfish. I don't care.

Took Sami to the park near the mosque this morning. He's getting better at walking on his own, barely needs my hand now. He kept crouching down to look at ants. We stayed for almost two hours. I didn't bring my phone on purpose.

Ran into Um Fadi from the building. She asked if I was working these days, meaning a real job. I said no, just home with Sami. She nodded the way people nod when they think they're being polite but their face says everything. I used to let that bother me. Today I just picked Sami up and pointed at a bird and we moved on.

Tariq's mother made mansaf for dinner and I helped her roll the jameed. She showed me her mother's technique with the wooden spoon and for once it wasn't corrective, it was like she was sharing something. I actually paid attention. Her hands move fast for someone her age. The rice came out perfectly and she said "you're learning" and I think she meant it warmly. These moments confuse me because they make me forget why I'm frustrated. Or maybe they're exactly why I stay patient through the rest of it.

Friday. Tariq took the day off and we went to his parents' village with Sami to visit his grandfather. The old house has a courtyard with a lemon tree that Tariq's grandmother planted. His grandfather told Sami the same story he tells everyone about building the house stone by stone. Sami just chewed on a lemon leaf.

I sat with the women while the men did their thing. Tariq's aunt made sage tea the old way, on a brass tray over coals. It was good. Quiet. I sketched some patterns on a napkin -- the tile work in the courtyard is beautiful, these geometric interlocking shapes I've been wanting to use in a design project. Nobody noticed me drawing, which was the whole point.

New client reached out through Instagram. Wants a logo for a women's fitness studio in West Amman. Paying 80 JD. I said yes before thinking about when I'd actually do the work. Sami's not sleeping well again and the house is never empty. But 80 JD is 80 JD. I'll figure out the hours somehow. I always do.

Nudge: "You keep saying you'll figure it out. Figure out what exactly?"

Response: How to be two people at once. The one who takes care of Sami and the house and keeps everyone comfortable. And the one who wants to make things and get paid for it and have something that's mine. There's no schedule that fits both. I just keep squeezing one into the gaps of the other.

Tariq and I had an actual conversation last night. Not a fight. He asked why I've been staying up so late and I almost lied but I was too tired to come up with something so I told him about the freelance work. All of it. The clients, the envelope, the bathroom laptop sessions.

He was quiet for a long time. Then he said he wished I'd told him sooner. Not because he wanted to control it but because he felt stupid not knowing. That stung. I told him it wasn't about trust, it was about needing something that was just mine. He said he understood that but it still felt like I didn't trust him. I don't know if he's wrong.

We didn't resolve anything exactly. He said he'd talk to his mother about giving me more space during the day so I don't have to hide. I told him I don't want him to fight that battle for me. He looked confused. I think he genuinely doesn't understand why I'd rather struggle alone than have him fix it. Honestly, I'm not totally sure I understand it either. But the thought of his mother knowing about the work and having opinions about it makes my chest tight. Some things I need to handle my own way, even if my way is harder.

Nudge: "He offered help and you said no. What are you protecting?"

Response: Can we not do this right now. I already spent all night turning this over in my head. I know it doesn't make total sense. I just need to sit with it for a bit.

Same day, later. Tariq's mother asked me at dinner if everything was okay between us. She must have heard us talking last night. I said everything was fine. Tariq backed me up. She didn't push it. But the way she looked at me -- not unkind, just knowing -- made me feel exposed. I keep building walls in a house made of thin ones.

entries.journal_entry:
Noor brought up the London program again at dinner. I could feel my chest tighten but I just said we'd talk later and cleared the plates. Haven't been able to sleep since.

Nudge: "What part of her bringing it up hit you the hardest?"

Response: She'd already looked up flat prices. Decided without me.

Spent the morning making maamoul with Hana and Tariq. They came over early, flour everywhere, the kitchen smelled like the house used to when Samira was alive. Hana shaped hers exactly the way her mother did, pressing the mold with both palms. I watched her and something settled inside me, like proof that what I've kept going all these years still matters to them too.

Tariq asked if we could do this every Friday, not just during holidays. I said of course. Small thing, but it sat well with me the rest of the afternoon.

Nudge: "Why did Tariq's request matter so much?"

My brother Walid called and said I should let Noor go, that I'm being selfish. Easy for him to say from Dubai with his kids in boarding school. Nobody in this family understands that keeping people close isn't the same as holding them back.

Came back to this tonight because Walid's call kept eating at me. After I hung up I sat in the garden for a long time. The jasmine Samira planted is still growing along the back wall, fifteen years now. I thought about how she would have handled this. She was always braver about letting the children try things. I think she would have been scared too but she wouldn't have let the fear win.

I don't know if that changes what I'll say to Noor. But I realized the anger I felt toward Walid was really just the sound of my own doubt getting louder.

Nudge: "You said Samira wouldn't have let fear win. What's the fear, exactly?"

Response: That if she leaves, the rest follow. House falls apart.

Noor cooked mansaf tonight. The whole thing, from scratch, without asking for help. She set the platter on the table the way I always do, rice shaped just right. Felt like she was showing me something without saying it.

Still thinking about the mansaf. The thing that keeps coming back to me is that she learned all of it here, in this kitchen, from watching me. Which means even if she's somewhere else, she carries it. I don't know why that's so hard for me to trust.

My father never let any of us leave Amman until we were married. I accepted that. But I also remember the years I spent wondering what it would have been like to study abroad when I had the chance. I told myself it didn't matter because I had responsibilities. Maybe it didn't matter. Or maybe I just never forgave myself for not pushing harder, and now I'm doing the same thing to her.

Nudge: "Is this about Noor, or about your own regret?"

Response: Maybe I'm afraid her thriving proves I didn't need to sacrifice everything.

Eid preparations started today and the house was full. Hana brought her husband and the baby, Tariq came with sweets from that bakery on Rainbow Street, even Walid video-called from Dubai to wish us well. Noor was in the kitchen all morning helping me roll grape leaves. For a few hours it felt exactly right, all the noise, the food, children running through every room. This is what I stayed home for.

But then after everyone left and it was just Noor and me cleaning up, she said quietly that she'd been offered a partial scholarship. She wasn't asking permission, not exactly, more like she was letting me know it was real now. I didn't say anything. I just kept drying the dishes and stacking them slowly.

I know I need to talk to her. I know what I'm leaning toward. But saying it out loud makes it final in a way I'm not ready for. The house will be so quiet without her.

Told Noor this morning she should accept the scholarship. She cried. I cried. We sat at the kitchen table for a long time not saying much. She kept saying she'd come back every summer, and I told her she should come back when she wants to, not because she feels she has to.

Spent the rest of the day reorganizing the pantry because I needed to do something with my hands. Found a jar of Samira's pickled turnips behind everything, must be from Hana's last batch. Put it on the counter where I can see it. The house is the same but it already feels like it's shifting.

Nudge: "Why put that jar where you can see it?"

Noor's been researching London neighborhoods all week, asking me to help her compare rental listings. Funny how she still wants my opinion on every detail even though she's the one leaving. I caught myself actually enjoying it, looking up transport maps, checking which areas have halal shops nearby. Like I'm planning a trip I'll never take.

Tariq suggested we start a family group chat so Noor can send photos when she's there. I said fine but I'm not learning any new apps. He laughed. I think this is how it goes, you hold something tightly and then you open your hand and it doesn't disappear. It just changes shape. I'm not all the way there yet but I can see the outline of it.

Nudge: "What does that outline actually look like?"

entries.journal_entry:
Mummyji made a comment at lunch about the indigo stains on my fingernails. She didn't say anything directly, just that thing she does where she looks at my hands and then looks at the dal and raises her eyebrows. Ravi was right there and said nothing. I know he thinks the dyeing thing is fine, he's told me so, but he won't say it in front of her. So I'm the one who looks like I'm neglecting things.

The thing is, the kitchen was spotless. Lunch was on time. Arjun's tiffin was packed before school. I don't know what more she wants from me. Or maybe I do know and I just don't want to give it, which is this complete surrender of any time that's mine. Like the house should swallow me whole and I should be grateful for it.

I scrubbed the indigo off after lunch. Took ten minutes with lemon and baking soda. I shouldn't have had to do that. But I did it anyway and I'm not sure what that says about me.

Nudge: "Why did you scrub them off?"

Response: Because it was easier than sitting through another silent dinner with her staring at my hands. I pick my fights and that one wasn't worth it. But it felt like erasing something.

Arjun's parent-teacher meeting today. The class teacher mentioned he draws during free period instead of playing cricket with the other boys and asked if everything is okay at home. I wanted to say, yes, everything is fine, my son likes drawing, why is that a problem? But I smiled and nodded and said I'd encourage him to be more social.

Came home and told Ravi about it and he said maybe Arjun should join a sport, that it would be good for him. And I thought, here we go. Everyone has an opinion about what the boy should be doing except the boy himself.

Nudge: "What stopped you from saying that to the teacher?"

Response: I don't know. You just don't, do you? Not in that setting.

Made aloo paratha for breakfast the way Appa used to, with extra ajwain in the dough. The smell filled the whole kitchen and for a second I was back in our old house in Mysore, watching him roll them out, flour everywhere.

Spent the whole morning at the Diwali mela helping set up the community puja. I did the rangoli at the entrance the way my grandmother taught me, rice flour with turmeric borders, none of the acrylic paint shortcuts the younger crowd uses. Three women came up and asked me to show them how. Felt good.

Later, back home, I worked on a new indigo resist pattern. Nobody was around to comment on it. Ravi had taken Arjun and Vihaan to the cricket match. Two hours of silence and my hands doing what they wanted to do. I didn't even check my phone.

Nudge: "Which part of the day stuck with you more?"

Mummyji wants me to start teaching Vihaan the Vishnu Sahasranama. He's seven. She says she learned it by six. I said I'd start next week, which I will, because it's important, Appa made sure I knew it by heart and I'm glad he did. But there's this edge to the way she asks, like she's testing whether I'm doing enough.

Ravi's cousin Priya called in the evening. She started a small catering business last year and wanted to know if I'd be interested in supplying hand-dyed table linens. I said I'd think about it. Didn't mention it to anyone at home yet.

Nudge: "Are you going to tell Ravi about Priya's offer?"

Sat down this morning to teach Vihaan the first twenty verses. He's quicker than I expected. He kept asking what the words meant and I found myself explaining them properly, not just the rote way I was taught but actually talking about what each name describes. He asked why Vishnu has so many names and I said because one name is never enough for something that big. He seemed satisfied with that.

It reminded me of sitting with Appa in the puja room, early mornings, the smell of camphor and sandalwood paste. He was patient. He never rushed me or made it feel like a chore. I want to give Vihaan that same feeling, not the pressure of getting it right, but the warmth of sitting together with something that matters. The sense that this is ours, this thread that connects us backward through generations, something nobody can take away or schedule over.

Mummyji passed by the room and listened for a while and actually smiled. She didn't say anything, didn't correct his pronunciation or mine. She just stood there and smiled. That was enough for today.

Spent the afternoon cataloguing my dye samples. I have fourteen shades of indigo now, ranging from almost-grey to a deep navy that I got by double-dipping with a fermentation vat I built from a recipe in an old Kalamkari manual. Labelled each one with the mordant used, the dip count, the fabric type. Cotton takes differently than silk, and I'm getting more consistent results with the cotton muslin I sourced from a weaver in Solapur.

Priya messaged again about the linens. She has a wedding order for February. Twelve table runners, twenty napkins. The money would be decent. I told her I need to think about scale. My terrace setup can handle maybe four meters of fabric at a time. I'd need to figure out drying space and timing around the kids' schedules.

Vihaan came up to the terrace after his homework and asked if he could dip his hand in the vat. I let him. His palm came out blue and he ran downstairs to show everyone. Mummyji was not pleased.

Nudge: "So are you doing it?"

Response: Haven't decided. Need to work out the logistics first.

Told Ravi about Priya's proposal over chai after the boys went to bed. He was more supportive than I expected. He said if I wanted to try it, he'd help me figure out the money side. He even suggested converting part of the back store room into a workspace. I hadn't thought about that.

What surprised me was my own hesitation. He was offering exactly what I thought I wanted, backing, space, permission. But when it was right there in front of me I felt this pull backward, like, who am I to run a business? My mother didn't. Mummyji didn't. All the women I grew up watching ran homes, not enterprises. And I love running this home. That's the part that gets lost when people hear I want to do something else. I don't want to leave, I want to also.

Nudge: "What does 'also' actually look like day to day?"

Response: Mornings with the boys, school drop-off, puja, cooking. Afternoons when they're in school or doing homework, that's my window. Three, maybe four hours. It's not a lot but it's mine. I just need people to stop treating those hours like leftover time that belongs to the house.

Mummyji brought up the store room idea at dinner, in front of Ravi's parents who were visiting. She said something like, "Meera is thinking of opening a factory in the house." She was smiling but it was the wrong kind of smile. Ravi's father asked what she was talking about and I had to explain the dyeing and the linen idea in front of everyone while trying not to sound like I was defending myself.

Ravi stepped in eventually and said it was just a small project. The word "small" stuck. I know he was trying to help but it felt like he was shrinking it to make it palatable. I didn't say anything after that. Finished dinner. Cleared the plates. Went upstairs.

Can't sleep. Ravi's parents left after dinner and everyone is being normal now but I keep replaying the table conversation. The worst part isn't what Mummyji said, it's that nobody asked me a real question about it. Nobody said, what kind of fabric, what's the process, how did you learn. They only wanted to know if it would interfere with anything.

I keep thinking about Appa's workshop in Mysore. He repaired watches. Tiny, patient, precise work. Nobody ever questioned whether he should be doing it. It was just what he did. I want that. Not the permission, not the approval. Just the quiet assumption that of course I do this, it's mine.

Nudge: "What would Appa say about the indigo?"

Took the boys to the Ganesh temple this morning for the monthly abhishekam. Vihaan recited the first twenty verses of the Sahasranama on the auto ride there, unprompted. I didn't even know he'd been practising on his own. The priest noticed and said something to me about keeping these things alive. I felt proud but also this strange defensiveness, like I needed to prove I wasn't only about the dyeing, that I still carry all of this too.

Why do I feel like I have to choose? Like every hour I spend with the indigo is an hour stolen from something else. Nobody told Ravi he has to choose between his consulting work and being a father. It's just assumed he can be both.

Nudge: "Who's actually making you choose?"

Called Priya back tonight and said yes to the February order. Twelve runners, twenty napkins, indigo with a kalamkari-inspired border block I've been working on. She sounded happy. I felt terrified and then calm, in that order, like jumping into cold water.

Haven't told Mummyji yet. Told Ravi, who said congratulations and then asked if I needed help setting up the store room this weekend. I said yes. Vihaan overheard and asked if he could help with the blue. I said yes to that too. I don't know if I'm doing the right thing. But I know I'm doing my thing, and right now that has to be enough.

entries.journal_entry:
Spent most of the morning at the hospital with Mother again. Her blood pressure readings have been erratic and the doctors want to adjust her medication. I sat in that waiting room scrolling through my literature review notes, pretending I could focus on methodology while watching the clock. By the time we got home and I made sure she ate lunch and took her pills, half the day was gone.

I signed up for this PhD knowing it would be challenging. What I didn't account for was how often life would demand I put it aside. My advisor sent a gentle reminder about the chapter draft due next week. I haven't written a single new paragraph in twelve days.

Strange to be the oldest person in every seminar, still learning to say "I need more time" without feeling like a failure.

Nudge: "What does failure look like to you here?"

Response: I suppose it looks like proving everyone right — my former colleagues who raised eyebrows when I resigned, my brother who thinks this degree is a vanity project. They expected me to enjoy retirement properly: travel with my husband, babysit grandchildren, maybe volunteer at the temple. Instead I'm chasing citations at fifty-eight.

But if I'm honest, the deeper fear is wasting whatever sharp years I have left. There's a clock now that wasn't there at thirty. Every delay feels heavier.

My daughter called crying at eleven last night. Her husband is traveling for work again and the baby has been colicky for three days straight. She asked if I could come stay for the weekend to help. I said yes before she finished the sentence, and then hung up and stared at my calendar.

The weekend is when I was supposed to finally sit down and write. I had blocked off Saturday and Sunday entirely — no hospital visits, no groceries, no family dinners. Just me, my laptop, and the quiet of an empty apartment. I was looking forward to it the way you look forward to a small holiday.

Now I'm packing a bag instead. And I feel terrible for resenting it, even a little. She's my daughter. She's exhausted and alone and I'm her mother. What kind of person calculates their writing time when their child needs them?

But another part of me is tired of always being the one who drops everything. My husband won't go — he says he's "not good with babies," as if that's a permanent condition and not something you can simply choose to do. So it falls to me. It always falls to me.

I'm bringing my laptop. I'm telling myself I'll write at night after the baby sleeps. We both know that's not going to happen.

Nudge: "What would happen if you said no, just this once?"

Back from my daughter's. Wrote nothing. Slept maybe four hours each night. The baby finally settled on Sunday evening, just in time for me to catch the train home.

I keep thinking about the pork belly buns from that little shop near her apartment. We walked there Saturday afternoon while the baby napped in the carrier. Twenty minutes of sunshine and soft dough and my daughter laughing at something I said. Those moments felt like breathing.

Maybe that has to be enough for now.

Submitted the draft. Not my best work but it exists. My advisor responded within an hour with comments — mostly structural, nothing devastating.

Made congee for Mother tonight. She said it tasted like my grandmother's, which is the highest compliment she gives. Small victories.

Nudge: "How are you feeling now that it's submitted?"

Response: My cohort is organizing a hot pot dinner for next week. First time they've included me in something social — usually they forget the old lady in the corner. I'm looking forward to it more than I probably should. Haven't had good hot pot in months.

The hot pot was wonderful. Crowded restaurant, too loud to hear anything, steam fogging my glasses every time I reached for the lamb slices. Xiao-Wei sat next to me and we discovered we both grew up eating the same brand of sesame paste that nobody makes anymore.

Funny how food carries so much. My mother used to mix that paste into noodles on cold days. Now I'm in a room full of people young enough to be my children, fishing mushrooms out of boiling broth, and for two hours I wasn't thinking about deadlines or hospital appointments or whether I'm falling behind.

I ordered a second round of the taro dumplings even though nobody asked. Sometimes you have to be the one who says "let's keep going." They didn't seem to mind.

Walked home through the night market after. Bought a bag of pineapple cakes I definitely don't need. Didn't feel guilty about it even once.

Nudge: "This sounds different from the weekend at your daughter's."

Response: It is different. No one needed me to be anywhere else. No responsibility I was avoiding by being there. I was just having dinner because I wanted to, with people who expected nothing from me except to pass the chili oil.

Maybe that's the permission I've been missing — not from anyone else but from the situation itself. When there's no competing obligation, the enjoyment feels clean.

My brother called this morning to remind me that Mother's 80th birthday is in three weeks and "everyone is expecting a proper celebration." By everyone he means himself and the cousins who haven't visited her in months. By proper celebration he means I'll be the one booking the restaurant, organizing the guest list, and making sure the gift is tasteful enough to photograph.

I told him I'm in the middle of my comprehensive exams and he laughed. Actually laughed. Said something about how I could take a break from "playing student" for one day.

I hung up on him. First time I've ever done that. My hands were shaking.

I know I should call back and smooth things over. That's what I always do. But I'm so tired of being the one who manages his feelings while he dismisses mine.

Three hours later and I still haven't called him back. My husband thinks I'm overreacting. "He didn't mean it that way," he said. "You know how he is."

Yes. I know exactly how he is. I've known for fifty years. That's precisely the problem.

When I gave up my career advancement to take care of our father during his illness, my brother sent flowers. When I spent six months coordinating Mother's hip surgery and recovery while working full time, my brother called on weekends. When I finally, finally decided to do something entirely for myself — to pursue this degree I've wanted since I was thirty — he treats it like a hobby I can set aside.

I'm not even angry at him specifically anymore. I'm angry at the role I've been assigned in this family, the one I keep accepting because the alternative feels like abandoning people I love. The reliable one. The one who handles things. The one whose own aspirations come last because someone has to hold everything together.

My chest hurts. I should make tea and calm down but I don't want to calm down. I want to stay angry long enough to remember this feeling the next time I'm tempted to say yes to something I don't have room for.

Nudge: "What would it look like to not be the reliable one?"

Response: Terrifying. And also, maybe, free?

I think about my classmate Hui-Fang who went to Portugal last month just because she wanted to see the ocean. She didn't ask permission or coordinate schedules. She just booked a ticket and went.

I can't even imagine making a decision that clean. Every choice I make has to pass through a filter of who else it affects, who will be disappointed, who will need me to be somewhere else. I've been doing it so long I don't know if I'd recognize myself without it.

But there's something appealing about the idea of someone else handling Mother's birthday. Just this once. Just to see what happens.

Called my brother back. Told him I'd handle the restaurant booking but the guest list and gifts are his responsibility this year. He sputtered but eventually agreed.

Then I made myself a proper cup of oolong and sat on the balcony watching the city lights come on. It's cold but I wrapped myself in the blanket my daughter gave me and stayed out there until the tea was gone.

Small rebellion. Small pleasure. Both felt earned.

Comprehensive exams tomorrow. Studied at the library until closing, then walked the long way home through the park where the winter jasmine is blooming.

Stopped for beef noodle soup at my usual place. The owner remembered my order — extra green onion, light on the chili. There's something comforting about being known.

Feel as ready as I'll ever be.

Nudge: "What's going through your mind tonight?"

Passed. The committee seemed genuinely interested in my research, asked questions I hadn't anticipated but could answer because I've been living inside this topic for two years. Dr. Huang said my clinical experience brought a perspective the literature lacks. Coming from him, that means something.

Called my mother first. She asked if this meant I was finally finished and I had to explain that no, there's still the dissertation, probably two more years. She sighed and said at least I'd be a doctor eventually and she could tell her mahjong friends. I'll take it.

My daughter sent flowers. Pink lilies and a card that said "So proud of you, Mom." I cried in my office for a solid five minutes.

Tonight my husband is taking me to the French restaurant we've been saying we'll try for a year. White tablecloths, a tasting menu, the whole indulgence. I bought a new dress — dark green, silk blend, probably too expensive. I'm going to enjoy every course without thinking about calories or cost or whether I should be home checking on Mother.

For once, I'm just going to let myself have the moment.

This degree might be a vanity project. It might be impractical at my age. It might mean missing things, disappointing people, constantly negotiating between who I want to be and who everyone needs me to be.

But today, sitting in that conference room, defending ideas I've been developing for two years — I felt like the version of myself I imagined when I was twenty-five and too afraid to try. She showed up after all. Just took her a few decades.

entries.journal_entry: Lit the incense this morning before the client call. Small thing, but my grandmother would approve. Felt steadier going into the pitch.

Met up with Jason and Wei Liang for drinks after work. They kept asking about my "startup life" with that tone they always have, like it's some extended gap year before I grow up and take over the family business like I'm supposed to.

I didn't argue this time. Just said business is fine, ordered another Tiger, changed the subject to Jason's wedding plans. I'm tired of defending myself. They don't understand that I can run my own thing and still be a good son. Those aren't opposites.

Sunday dinner at Ah Ma's was the usual. She made her braised pork belly the way she's made it for forty years, using the same clay pot that belonged to her mother. I watched her in the kitchen for a bit while everyone else was in the living room watching the news. She moves slower now but she still won't let anyone help with the cooking.

My cousin Melissa brought her new boyfriend, some finance guy from Hong Kong. Ah Ma was polite but I could tell she didn't like that he only spoke English and didn't touch the pig's ear dish. Little things. After dinner she pulled me aside and asked about my business. Not in the way my parents ask, like they're waiting for me to fail. She just wanted to know if I was eating enough and sleeping enough. Then she said her father also left his village to try something new, and that took courage, but he always sent money home. She wasn't lecturing. Just telling me.

I don't know why that hit different. Maybe because she's the one person who doesn't make me feel like I'm choosing between two lives.

Nudge: "What makes her different from the others?"

Response: She's lived through actual hard things. The war, losing her first shop, raising five kids. I think she just has perspective that my parents don't. They grew up comfortable because she made it possible. So now they're anxious about protecting what they have. She already knows you can lose everything and rebuild.

Still thinking about what Ah Ma said about her father. I looked up the village name after I got home. It's in Fujian, some small place I can barely find on the map. He left with nothing and built something in Singapore. Opened a provisions shop. That became my grandfather's import business. That became my father's company.

And I left that to start a tech consultancy. Three generations of building, and I walked away. At least that's how my father sees it. He's never said it directly but I can feel it every time we talk. Like I've broken some chain.

But Ah Ma's father left too. He left his family, his village, everything familiar. And that's why any of us are here. So why is my leaving different? Maybe because I'm not leaving out of necessity. I'm leaving because I want something else. Maybe that's what they can't forgive.

Nudge: "Is it something they can't forgive, or something you can't?"

Response: Both, probably. I don't know if I've actually forgiven myself for not wanting what they want for me. Every time the business does well, part of me wonders if I'm just proving I was right to leave. And every time it struggles, I wonder if I should have just stayed.

Closed the deal with the logistics company. Three-month engagement, decent budget. Should keep the lights on through Q2.

Called my mother to tell her. She said "that's good" and then asked if I'm coming back for Qing Ming next week. Didn't ask anything about the contract. I said yes, I'll be there.

Nudge: "How did that feel, her not asking about it?"

Qing Ming was fine. We went to the cemetery at 6am before it got too crowded. I helped my father carry the offerings, burned the paper money, did the three bows. Same as every year.

My uncle asked when I'm going to "stop playing" and help run the real business. Right there at my grandfather's grave. I just smiled and said business is good. Didn't want to cause a scene. But I kept thinking, would Gong Gong have been disappointed in me too? He built something from nothing. I'm building something from nothing. It's not the same thing, but it's not that different either. I wanted to say that. I didn't.

Nudge: "What stopped you from saying it?"

Fight with my father tonight. Real one, not the passive aggressive stuff. He came to my apartment unannounced, said he was "in the area" which is never true. He saw my laptop open with the client dashboard and started asking questions. Then he said he doesn't understand why I need to prove something when there's already a business that needs me.

I tried to explain that I'm not proving anything. I just want to build my own thing. He said his father didn't have the luxury of wanting. He had to build because there was no other choice. Then he said "you have a choice and you're choosing wrong."

I don't remember exactly what I said back but it was something about how maybe I'm not wrong, maybe he just can't see it because he never had to question what he was supposed to do. He left without finishing his tea.

My mother called an hour later to say he's just worried about me. I know. I know he's worried. But being worried doesn't make him right.

Nudge: "What happened after you hung up with your mom?"

Response: I sat on the couch for a while. Didn't do anything. Then I cleaned the apartment, which I do when I'm upset. Washed the teacup he didn't finish. Went to bed early but couldn't sleep. Kept replaying what I should have said differently.

Went to the temple with Ah Ma this morning. She goes on the first and fifteenth of the lunar month, always has. I don't usually join but I had time and she seemed pleased when I offered.

We lit joss sticks and she showed me how to hold them properly, three in both hands, bow three times. She does it with such certainty. I like that about her generation. They don't question these things. They just do them because their parents did them and their parents before that. I used to think that was mindless but now I think maybe there's something to just... participating. Being part of something older than yourself.

Had to turn down a potential client today. They wanted me to work on a project for their online gambling platform. Good money, probably six months of runway. But I kept thinking about what my parents would say if they found out. Not that they would, but still.

My grandfather's name is on my business registration. Lim Wei Keong Consulting Pte Ltd. I named it after him because I wanted to feel connected to something larger than just me and my laptop. So when I thought about taking money from a gambling company, it felt wrong. Like I was attaching his name to something he would have disapproved of.

I don't even know if he would have cared. He died when I was twelve. I barely remember his voice. But I remember my grandmother saying he never gambled, not even mahjong, because his own father lost everything that way. So I said no to the client. Probably stupid from a business standpoint. But it felt like the right thing.

Nudge: "What do you actually remember about him?"

Response: Mostly just impressions. The smell of his pipe tobacco. His hands, very rough, always moving. He used to let me stamp the shipping documents with the company chop when I visited the warehouse. Made me feel important. I remember his funeral more clearly than I remember him alive. Everyone crying. The paper house they burned for him. My father reading the eulogy in a voice I'd never heard before.

Mother's birthday dinner at the seafood restaurant in Geylang. The one we've been going to since I was a kid. Same lazy Susan, same chili crab, same argument about who's paying the bill.

Dad and I didn't talk much but it wasn't hostile. Just quiet. I gave her a jade bracelet, the kind she likes. She cried a little, said I didn't have to spend money on her. Then she asked if I'm eating properly. Same as always.

Nudge: "Where do things stand with your dad now?"

Response: Same as always. He's stubborn, I'm stubborn. We'll figure it out eventually. Or we won't. Family, right?

Client emergency all day. Their website went down during a promotion and I had to fix it remotely while pretending to work from a cafe because my home internet was out. By the time everything was stable it was 9pm and I'd missed Sunday dinner at Ah Ma's.

First time I've missed in maybe two years. I called to apologize and she said it's okay, she saved me some food. But I could hear something in her voice. Not anger, just... something. Like she was expecting this would happen eventually. Like she's been waiting for me to become the kind of person who misses family dinners for work.

I am that person now, I guess. The kind who chooses the client over the grandmother. My father probably missed a hundred family dinners building the company. My grandfather probably did too. So why does it feel different when I do it? Maybe because I was supposed to be different. I was supposed to be the generation that has it figured out. The one who doesn't have to sacrifice everything.

Nudge: "What did you think you were supposed to be?"

Picked up the leftover braised pork from Ah Ma's this afternoon. She'd kept it in the fridge all week, even though I told her she could give it to someone else. Stubborn woman. I sat with her for a while in the living room, watching her fold paper offerings for the upcoming Vesak Day.

She didn't mention me missing dinner. Just asked about business and whether I was sleeping. Then she told me a story about my grandfather I hadn't heard before. Apparently he used to sneak out of family dinners early to check on the warehouse. His own mother would scold him. "The goods won't walk away," she'd say. But he couldn't help it. She laughed telling me this. Said I reminded her of him. First time anyone's said that in a way that wasn't a complaint.

Nudge: "What did it mean to hear that from her?"

Response: I don't know. Good, I guess. Like maybe I'm not as lost as I thought. Or at least I'm lost in a way that runs in the family.

entries.journal_entry:
Spent most of the day doing Deliveroo runs in the rain. The app kept sending me to Kralingen and back, which eats petrol. Made about seventy euros after costs. Not terrible, not good.

Drove past the old Fenix warehouse on the way home. They've turned it into some kind of food hall now. Mam and I used to go there when I was small — it was still a working warehouse then, real dockworkers loading ships. She'd bring me along when she visited Tante Lien. Strange seeing the same walls but everything inside different. The bones are there but the life has changed.

Called Mam tonight. She asked when I'm coming for Sunday. Told her this week looks difficult because of a booking in Dordrecht. She didn't say anything but the silence was louder than words.

Nudge: "What was it about that warehouse that stayed with you?"

Long day. Did a furniture assembly job in Capelle through Werkspot, then four hours of deliveries in the evening. My back is killing me. Should probably see someone about it but I don't have the coverage for a physiotherapist right now. The basic insurance only goes so far.

Had a strange moment at the assembly job. The couple who hired me — young, maybe early thirties — they were hanging family photos on the wall of their new flat. Grandparents, wedding photos, kids at christenings. The man said something about wanting the living room to feel like his grandmother's house. I understood that completely. But then he also had this app controlling all the lights and the thermostat and I thought, you can't have your oma's living room run by a computer. It doesn't work like that.

Too tired to think about it more. Eating leftover stamppot and going to bed.

Nudge: "Sounds like that bothered you more than just being tired."

Response: Maybe. It's not about the smart lights, really. It's that everyone wants the feeling of the old way without actually living it. They want the photos on the wall and the gezelligheid but they also want the algorithm to set the temperature. You can't curate warmth. My grandmother's house was warm because she was there all day, baking, mending, talking to the neighbours. It wasn't a setting you could select. I think what bothers me is that I understand the impulse — I want it too — but I can see it slipping into something hollow. And I'm not sure I'm doing any better. I drive around all day for an app. My father would not have understood this life at all.

Can't sleep so writing again. Mind is jumping around. Listened to a recording of the Zeeland dialect my cousin Jaap posted in the family WhatsApp group — some old farmer telling a story about the flood of '53. My grandfather survived that flood. He never talked about it directly but it shaped everything about how he lived afterward. Every decision was about making sure the family had solid ground.

That's what I keep coming back to. Solid ground. My grandfather rebuilt after the water took the house. My father kept the shop going for four decades in the same spot. And here I am checking an app to see if someone in Rotterdam-Zuid wants their Pad Thai delivered before it gets cold.

But tonight something small happened that felt right. One of my regular delivery customers — an old woman on Nieuwe Binnenweg — she recognised me and asked me to come in for a moment. She'd made a pot of coffee and had nobody to drink it with. I said I had five minutes. We talked about the weather and she showed me a photo of her late husband. I was fifteen minutes late on my next delivery and got a warning from the app. Don't care.

Sitting with her felt like the most honest thing I'd done all week. Like something my father would have done without even thinking about it.

Missed Koningsdag with the family because I took a full day of bookings. Needed the money. Mam sent photos of everyone at Uncle Willem's — the kids in orange, Opa's flag on the pole like always. My niece asked where I was. I hate that she had to ask. I should have been there. The money isn't worth what I gave up.

Saturday. Drove to Zeeland after my morning shifts. Made it in time for coffee and cake at Mam's. She'd kept the leftover appeltaart from Wednesday's church group. We sat in the kitchen and she told me stories about Dad fixing the shop's awning every spring. Fell asleep on her couch before dinner. Woke up feeling more rested than I have in weeks.

Nudge: "What was different about that rest?"

Response: It's the house. The sounds. The clock in the hall that's been ticking since before I was born. Mam's radio on low in the kitchen. No notifications, no app telling me where to go next. Just the shape of a day I recognise from childhood. My body knows how to rest there because nothing in that house is trying to get my attention. Everything is just where it's been for thirty years.

Had a conversation with my ex-wife Anneke today. She called about logistics for our son Bram's birthday next week. Bram's turning twenty-two. We kept it practical — who's bringing what, what time, where. But at the end she said something that stuck. She said, "You sound tired, Gerrit. Not just body tired."

She's not wrong. I've been thinking about what my life looks like from the outside and whether it matches what I thought it would be at this age. My father at fifty-one had the shop, the house, the family under one roof, the church council seat. He knew where he'd be every day for the next ten years. I don't know where I'll be next Tuesday.

I'm not envious exactly. He also had worries I don't have — inventory, rent, employees. But he had a shape to his life. A pattern you could trace. Mine feels like scattered dots.

The strange part is that within the gig work there are small rituals I've built. Same route through Delfshaven in the mornings. Same café for my break. Same nod to the guy at the parking garage. It's not nothing. But it's not the same as what was handed down to me. I'm making something, but I can't tell yet if it holds.

Nudge: "What would it take for those rituals to feel like enough?"

Response: Honestly, I think it would take other people sharing them. My father's routines meant something because the whole family moved through them together. Opening the shop, closing the shop, dinner at six, church on Sunday — these weren't just his habits, they were ours. My café stop in Delfshaven is mine alone. Nobody notices if I skip it. That's the difference. A ritual that only you observe is just a habit. I think for it to hold, someone else would have to care whether I kept it or not.

Bram's birthday dinner tonight. Anneke organised it at that Indonesian place he likes. We were all there — Anneke, her partner Maarten, Bram, Bram's girlfriend, me. Could have been awkward. Wasn't, really.

Bram asked me to say grace before the meal. Nobody else at the table does that anymore. Anneke looked surprised. Maarten looked at his plate. But Bram asked, so I did. Kept it short. Thanked God for the food and for Bram being healthy and here with us. My voice cracked a bit on the last part but I don't think anyone noticed.

After dinner, Bram walked me to my car and said, "Thanks for doing that, Pa. Oma would have liked it." He's right. She would have. That one sentence carried me all the way home.

Got a notification today that Deliveroo is changing the payment structure again. Lower base rate, higher "surge" bonuses. Which means they want us competing against each other for the busy slots instead of earning a steady amount. Every few months they adjust something and we just have to absorb it. There's no negotiation. There's no union rep to call. There's a feedback form that goes nowhere.

My father would have walked out. He walked out of a supplier deal once because the man changed the terms after a handshake. Said if a man's word doesn't hold, his product won't either. That was the end of it. He found another supplier that afternoon. I can't find another Deliveroo. I mean, I can switch to Uber Eats or Thuisbezorgd, but they all do the same thing. There's no door to walk out of because there are no walls — just an app you log into.

I talked to another driver at the pickup point about it. He shrugged. He's twenty-four. He doesn't think about it the way I do. For him this is temporary — a step between university and a real job. For me this is the job. This is Monday. This is how I pay rent.

I'm not angry at him. I'm angry that I'm fifty-one and standing in the same spot as a twenty-four-year-old with a completely different horizon. We're doing the same work but living in different stories.

Went to church this morning. Not my usual one in Rotterdam — I drove to Zeeland for the service at the old church in Goes. Sat in the same pew where my family has sat for as long as anyone remembers. Third row, left side, near the pillar.

Pastor de Vries preached about the parable of the sower. Seeds on rocky ground, seeds among thorns, seeds in good soil. I've heard it a hundred times. But this time I thought about it differently. The rocky ground isn't always a place — sometimes it's a season. Sometimes the soil is fine but the weather is wrong. You still plant.

After the service, old Meneer Bakker shook my hand and asked about my mother. Said he remembered my father fondly. "A man who kept his word and kept his shop clean." That's two things, but they capture Dad pretty well.

Drove back to Rotterdam in the afternoon. The highway was busy, everyone returning from the weekend. I had a delivery booking at four but I took the long route through the country roads instead, past the fields and the old dykes. Got to Rotterdam ten minutes late. Didn't rush.

I don't have the life my father had. I might never have it. But I can still sit in that pew and drive those roads and remember that the ground under all of this is the same ground. It doesn't move, even when I do.

Nudge: "What does that ground hold for you right now?"

Response: Family. The language. The church. The way Mam still sets the table the same way every Sunday. The fact that Bram asked me to say grace, which means something got passed along even through the mess of the divorce and the years I wasn't fully present. The ground is whatever doesn't change based on what app I open or what rate I get paid. It's the part of me that existed before any of this and will still be there after. I can't always stand on it — sometimes I'm too far away, too busy, too caught up in the week. But knowing it's there is what keeps me from feeling completely adrift.
