entries.journal_entry:
My mother called to remind me about the novena for Tía Carmen's anniversary next week. Didn't need the reminder — it's been circled on my calendar since January. When I was small I used to think these gatherings were just adults talking over coffee, but now I understand them differently. Nine nights of prayer, nine nights of family filling up the house. There's something steady about it.

I taught my third graders how to fold paper flowers today for the altar at school. One little boy asked why we do it every year and I told him the same thing my grandmother told me: because the dead don't really leave if we keep making space for them. He seemed satisfied with that. Sometimes children accept what adults forget — that repetition isn't emptiness, it's a choice to keep something alive.

Carlos asked to meet my family this weekend. I made up something about it being a busy week with the novena. He looked hurt. I feel like I'm lying to two worlds at once — telling him we just need more time, telling my mother I'm too focused on work to date. Neither story is true. The true story is that I don't know how to bring a divorced man into a family that still whispers about my cousin's civil ceremony.

Nudge: "What would happen if you just told your mother the truth?"

Response: She wouldn't yell. That's not how she operates. She'd get quiet and then start asking gentle questions that feel like tiny knives — How long has this been going on? Has he been to confession? What does his family think about all this? And I'd have no good answers. I don't even know if I want to marry him. I just want space to figure that out without the weight of everyone's expectations pressing down on me before I've made up my own mind.

Spent the evening at Abuela's preparing food for tomorrow's final night of the novena. Chopped onions until my eyes burned. My cousin Daniela brought her new baby and everyone passed him around the kitchen like a small warm blessing. The prayers start at seven. I'll be there early to set up chairs like I have been every year since I was fourteen.

The new principal at school announced we're "modernizing" the curriculum. Tablets for third graders. Interactive lessons instead of textbooks. She presented it like everyone should be excited but I sat in that meeting thinking about Sister Magdalena who taught at this school for forty years and how her chalkboard handwriting was so beautiful my mother still talks about it. I'm not against technology — I use my phone constantly, I'm not a dinosaur — but something about the way the principal said "we need to move past old methods" made me want to dig my heels in.

After the meeting Roberto from fifth grade asked what I thought and I said it sounded fine. I don't know why I didn't say what I was really thinking. Maybe because I didn't want to be the young teacher defending old ways. Maybe because I don't even know if I'm right. The kids might love the tablets. Sister Magdalena isn't here to ask.

I keep thinking about how my mother taught me to read with a book of saints' stories, turning actual pages, her finger tracing the words. What gets lost when everything becomes a screen? Or am I just afraid of change and calling it something nicer?

Nudge: "You said you didn't speak up. What would you have said if you had?"

Response: That maybe we should pilot it in one classroom first instead of rolling it out everywhere at once. That the older teachers who've been here for decades deserve to have their methods respected, not treated like problems to be solved. That learning isn't just information transfer — it's also ritual, routine, the physical feeling of a pencil in your hand. But standing up in a staff meeting to say "I think ritual matters" sounds ridiculous. So I stayed quiet and now I'm typing it into my phone at 11pm instead.

My friend Valentina says I'm "too attached to the past." She means it gently but it still stings. She left Guadalajara for Monterrey and barely comes back for holidays now. Good for her. I'm not running from anything by staying. This is where I'm needed. My grandmother is eighty-three. My students know me. The roots here are mine.

Nudge: "What about her comment bothered you most?"

Carlos and I had a real fight. Not yelling — we don't do that — but the cold kind where you both know something broke. He asked if I was ashamed of him and I didn't answer fast enough. I'm not ashamed. I'm scared. There's a difference but I couldn't find the words to explain it while he was standing there looking at me like he'd already made up his mind about what my silence meant.

My parents have been married for thirty-two years. They met at church, got engaged with my grandfather's blessing, had the wedding everyone still talks about. That's the script. I don't know how to write a different one and still feel like myself. Carlos doesn't fit the script but neither does walking away from him. I lay awake until three trying to figure out which betrayal I could live with.

Nudge: "Is there a version of this where nobody gets betrayed?"

Response: I want to say yes. The optimistic answer is that my family loves me and they'll accept whoever makes me happy. My cousin got pushback about her civil ceremony and now everyone's fine at Christmas dinner. But I know my mother. She carries things quietly for years. She'd show up to the wedding and smile and then I'd catch her crying in the kitchen when she thinks no one's looking. I don't know if I can build a life on top of that image. Maybe that's my problem — I'm not choosing between Carlos and my family. I'm choosing between the version of me who disappoints people and the version who disappears herself to keep everyone comfortable.

Went to confession today for the first time in months. Didn't plan to — I was walking past the church after buying bread and the door was open. Father Miguel has known me since my first communion so there was no hiding behind anonymity. I told him I've been lying to my mother about something important. He didn't ask what. Just waited.

I ended up talking for twenty minutes about Carlos, about feeling stuck, about how the rules I grew up with don't seem to have instructions for this situation. He said something I keep turning over: "The tradition doesn't ask you to stop living. It asks you to bring your life to it honestly." I don't know exactly what that means yet but it felt like permission to stop pretending there's a perfect answer.

After confession I sat in the pew where my grandmother always sits and just breathed. The church smelled the same way it did when I was seven. Some things don't change and today that felt like comfort instead of a cage.

School starts back next week. Spent the afternoon organizing my classroom. Put up the same San Francisco de Asis poster my mother gave me my first year teaching. The corners are getting worn. Considered replacing it but decided against it. Some things are allowed to show their age.

I told my mother about Carlos. Not everything — not the divorce — but that I've been seeing someone. She asked when she could meet him and I panicked and said "soon" which is what I always say. She knows me too well. She could hear the hesitation and asked what I wasn't telling her.

I almost said it. The words were right there. But then my abuela came into the room asking about dinner and the moment passed and I felt relief and shame in equal measure. Relief because I got more time. Shame because I'm a grown woman hiding in the kitchen of my childhood home, still arranging my life around moments that are safe.

Carlos deserves better than being my secret. I deserve better than feeling like a coward. Something has to break.

Nudge: "What would breaking look like for you?"

Response: Telling the full truth and accepting whatever comes after. Not performing okay-ness at the next family gathering. Letting my mother feel her feelings without me trying to manage them. Maybe it looks like a difficult Sunday dinner where Carlos sits across from my father and answers questions about his first marriage. Maybe it looks like my grandmother going quiet in that way she does when she disapproves. I can survive that. I've survived other hard things. What I can't survive is another six months of living in two separate worlds that never touch.

Brought Carlos to Sunday lunch at my parents' house. My hands were shaking when we walked through the door. My mother had set an extra place — she knew I wouldn't come alone after our last conversation. She's always known more than she lets on.

It wasn't easy. There were silences that stretched too long and my father asked pointed questions about where Carlos sees himself in five years. But my grandmother held his hand when he talked about his daughter from his first marriage, and said children are always a blessing no matter the circumstances. That's not the same as approval but it's not rejection either.

After dessert, while Carlos helped my father clear plates, my mother pulled me aside and said "I don't understand all of it but I can see he's good to you." That's enough for now. Maybe that's what Father Miguel meant — bringing my life to the tradition honestly doesn't mean it fits perfectly. It just means I stop hiding the pieces that don't.

entries.journal_entry:
Twelve-hour shift and my feet are just wrecked. But honestly a decent day. One of my kids on the ward, Milo, he's seven and has been in and out for weeks with a recurring infection. Today he finally got the all-clear for discharge. His mom was crying and I was trying not to cry and he just kept asking if he could keep the stickers I'd been putting on his IV pole every morning.

I told him yes obviously. Grabbed a handful of extras from the supply closet and stuffed them in a ziplock for him. His mom hugged me on the way out and said I was the only nurse who remembered he was scared of the blood pressure cuff and always warned him first. I do remember that stuff. It matters.

Got home and Mom had made mac and cheese from a box, which she was very proud of. Her hip is doing better, she managed the kitchen without the walker. We ate on the couch watching Chopped reruns. I didn't have the energy to make anything real. But sitting there with the mac and cheese and Mom laughing at the contestants, I just felt like okay. This is fine. This is actually good.

Nudge: "Is that what good usually looks like?"

Lisa from work asked me again about the charge nurse thing. Said the manager is "disappointed" I turned it down and that I'm "wasting my potential." I told her I appreciate the concern but I'm good where I am. She gave me that look, the one where people think you're being lazy or afraid when really you just know what works for you.

I'm not afraid of more responsibility. I covered the entire unit by myself that time two nurses called out during the flu surge. I can handle it. I just don't want to lose my Saturdays and trade my shift for nights. Mom needs someone home in the evenings and I need at least a couple days a month where I do absolutely nothing useful. That's not a character flaw.

Nudge: "Does Lisa's opinion actually get to you or is it just annoying?"

Response: It gets to me more than I want it to. She's not wrong that I could do the job. I just don't want it right now. But hearing "wasting potential" sticks in my head longer than it should.

Mom had a rough night, woke up at 3am with hip pain and I ended up sitting with her for two hours doing ice packs and finding the right pillow arrangement. Then went straight to work at 6:30. Running on maybe three hours of sleep.

The thing that's eating at me is that at one point she was apologizing for waking me up and I snapped. Not badly, just a quick "Mom it's fine, please stop saying sorry." But I could hear the edge in my voice and she went quiet. She didn't deserve that. She didn't ask for any of this. I just — I was so tired and I had work in a few hours and the frustration came out sideways.

Day off. Slept in until 10 which felt almost criminal. Mom was already up, puttering around with her walker, watching the morning news at a volume that could wake the neighborhood. Made us both a proper breakfast — scrambled eggs with chives from the little herb pot on the windowsill, sourdough toast, fresh orange juice. She said it was better than the diner we used to go to on Sundays when I was little. I don't think that's true but it was sweet.

Spent the afternoon doing laundry and reorganizing the hall closet, which has been bothering me for weeks. Mom sat in her chair and directed me on where everything should go, like she was running a construction site. It was actually kind of fun. Found a box of old photos from when my brother Kevin and I were small. Mom got teary looking at them but in a happy way.

Kevin called in the evening. He lives in Denver now. Asked how Mom was doing and I gave him the update. He said he's sorry he can't help more and that he'll try to visit in August. I said that would be great, and I meant it, but I also know "try" usually means it won't happen. I'm not angry about it. He has his own life, two kids, a wife with a demanding job. It just is what it is. I'm the one who's here and that's the arrangement we've settled into.

Nudge: "When Kevin says 'try to visit' — what do you actually expect?"

Response: Honestly? Nothing. And I've made my peace with that mostly. He'll call, send money sometimes, maybe show up for Thanksgiving. But the day-to-day stuff is mine. It's been that way since Mom's surgery and probably won't change.

Picked up an extra shift for Dani because her kid had a fever. Sixteen hours on my feet. I can barely think straight right now. Got home and Mom had waited up for me even though it was almost midnight. She'd left a plate of pasta in the microwave with a little note that said "eat something, mija."

I feel bad because my Saturday is supposed to be this weekend and I don't think I can do it. Mom has a follow-up appointment Saturday morning and Kevin was supposed to call her PT coordinator back last week and never did, so now I have to sort that out too. I was going to do the sushi-and-movie thing. I've been looking forward to it all week. But I keep giving those days away and then wondering why I feel like a wrung-out dishrag.

Nudge: "What if you kept the Saturday anyway?"

Called Kevin about the PT coordinator thing. He apologized, said it slipped his mind. I told him it's fine but could he at least handle the insurance paperwork that's due next week. He said sure. We'll see.

I rescheduled Mom's appointment to Monday and I'm keeping my Saturday. I made the decision and I feel weird about it. Like I'm being selfish even though rationally I know a two-day delay on a routine follow-up doesn't matter. But there's this voice in my head that sounds a lot like Mom's voice — not the real Mom, the imaginary guilt-trip Mom — saying I should just handle it and stop complaining. Real Mom would probably tell me to go enjoy my day. She's not the problem. I'm the problem.

Took my Saturday. Hot bath, ordered the good sushi from Kiko's, watched that French movie I've had saved for a month. Nobody needed anything from me for eight whole hours and I didn't check my phone once.

Nudge: "How'd it feel compared to what you expected?"

Response: Better. Genuinely better. I kept waiting for the guilt to kick in and it mostly didn't. Just felt like myself again.

Took Mom to the rescheduled follow-up. PT says she's progressing well, might be able to transition to outpatient therapy in a couple months. That would free up a lot of evenings for me which I didn't let myself think about too much while the doctor was talking, but it was the first thing that popped into my head.

Started thinking about what I'd do with those evenings. Join a pottery class or something, go to that wine bar Hannah keeps inviting me to. Then immediately felt gross about it because my mom is still recovering and here I am mentally redecorating my free time. I don't know why I can't just want two things at once without feeling like one of them makes me a bad person.

Nudge: "Who told you wanting your own life makes you bad?"

entries.journal_entry:
My cousin Luc called to tell me his wife is pregnant and they just closed on a house in Barrhaven. Good for them. I told him things are going well with the courier gigs and he got quiet in that way people do when they're trying not to say what they actually think.

Nudge: "What part of that silence bugged you most?"

Response: Probably that he didn't even bother arguing with me about it anymore. Like he's decided I'm a lost cause or something. A year ago he would've pushed back, told me to finish school. Now it's just quiet. I don't know if that's worse.

Picked up a double shift on the app Thursday because rent's due. Did about fourteen hours between rides and deliveries. Ate a shawarma in the car between pickups around Plateau. Phone died at 9pm so I had to call it early anyway. Made enough to cover rent plus a bit extra.

Maman texted asking if I'm coming to Tante Diane's birthday thing in October. It's in Sudbury obviously. I said I'd try. She sent back a thumbs up which from her basically means she already knows I won't make it. I'll probably go though. Diane's turning seventy and she's the one who taught me to skate.

Nudge: "What happens if you skip Diane's seventieth?"

Response: I'm not going to skip it. I just said I'd try.

Switched my Tuesday route to cover the McGill area because a couple of the regulars tip well over there. Figured out that if I stage near the Roddick Gates between 11:30 and 1 I can grab three or four lunch deliveries without killing my legs on the hills. Small optimization but it adds up over the week.

Called Grand-mère after my shift. She was watching her game show and half-listening but that's fine, that's how she is. Told her about the McGill route and she laughed and said her father used to deliver ice blocks on Sherbrooke Street with a horse cart. She tells me that every time. I don't mind hearing it again.

Guy at a red light today rolled down his window and asked if I was hiring for my "company" because of the courier bag. Made me laugh. Told him it's just me and my bike. But it stuck with me after. I've been doing this almost two years now and I never once had to ask anyone's permission to take a day off or switch a route. That part still feels right even when the money doesn't.

There's something about the rhythm I've built here. Mornings I pick my own routes. Afternoons I can stop if I want to or push through. When I was in the business program in Sudbury every hour was accounted for by somebody else's schedule. I couldn't breathe in that. Maybe some people need that structure. I just know I'm not one of them.

Nudge: "When the money doesn't feel right, what does that actually look like?"

Response: It's not dramatic or anything. Just some weeks are tight. I figure it out.

Maman called tonight and it turned into a whole thing. She wants me to come home for Thanksgiving and also stay the extra week to help Uncle René move out of his apartment because his knees are bad. I said I can't do a week and a half off the app, I'll lose my momentum and the algorithm buries you if you go inactive. She hit me with the "family is more important than an app, Marcus" line and I kind of lost it.

I didn't yell or anything but I got sharp with her. Told her she doesn't understand how this works, that it's not like taking vacation days from a normal job, there's no paid leave, no one covers your shifts. She went quiet. I hate when I do that to her. She's not wrong that René needs help. I just can't be the one who drops everything every time. But saying that out loud to her felt awful.

Nudge: "You said she's not wrong. So who is?"

Response: Nobody's wrong, that's the problem. She's right that René needs the help and that family should show up. I'm right that I can't just vanish for ten days without it costing me real money I don't have a cushion for. It's just one of those things where both sides are true and somebody still ends up disappointed.

entries.journal_entry:
Had the regional partners call this morning and I could feel the whole thing slipping. The Vietnam office wants to renegotiate their revenue share, which fine, they can ask, but the way Minh framed it — presenting the new terms as a done deal in front of the whole team, slides already formatted — that's not a negotiation, that's a corner. He backed me into agreeing to "review" the proposal by Friday. Everyone on the call saw it.

Spent the rest of the morning replaying the moment. I should have cut him off. Should have said we'll discuss it offline. Instead I sat there nodding like I was considering it when really I was just blindsided. The Singapore guys were quiet, which means they noticed. Probably already talking about it in their group chat.

Called my CFO after to get the actual numbers. The Vietnam office is profitable but not indispensable. I need to figure out how to walk this back without looking like I'm retaliating. But honestly, part of me wants to fly down there next week and have the conversation face to face, in my building, at my table. Let Minh present his slides then.

Nudge: "What would've been different if Minh raised it privately first?"

Response: Everything. If he'd come to me one-on-one, I'd have heard him out, probably even agreed to some of it. The numbers aren't unreasonable. It's the staging of it that I can't let stand. You don't ambush the person who signs your contracts.
Booked the flight to Ho Chi Minh City for Tuesday. Told Minh I want to walk the warehouse floor and review Q3 ops in person. Didn't mention the revenue share. Let him wonder.

Back from Vietnam. The meeting went how I wanted — Minh pulled back to a five-percent adjustment instead of twelve, and I agreed on the spot so it looked generous. Shook hands in front of his team. I even took everyone to dinner after at that French place on Dong Khoi. Good evening, lots of laughing, Minh's deputy told me she's glad I came down personally.

But on the flight home something kept bothering me. Minh's face when I steered the conversation. He knew exactly what I was doing. He went along with it because he had to, not because he wanted to. I got what I needed, and the office stays profitable, and the relationship is technically intact. So why does it feel like I won a hand of poker against someone who couldn't afford to fold.

Nudge: "You said the relationship is 'technically intact' — what's the non-technical version?"

Response: Minh will do what I need him to do. But the next time he has a real problem — something going wrong on the ground — I'm probably the last person he calls. That's the non-technical version.

Alumni dinner last night went well. Three founders from the 2018 cohort asked me to mentor their series B pitch. Felt good to be the one they came to, specifically because they'd heard I don't sugarcoat.

Soyeon called from Zurich this morning, first time in two weeks. She sounded fine, talked about her roommate and some lake trip the school organized. Normal teenage stuff. But she didn't ask about home. Not about her mother, not about the dog, nothing. Just called to tell me she needed the tuition wire for the supplementary program, and could I do it today.

I did it immediately, obviously. Opened the banking app while we were still on the phone, confirmed the transfer, told her it was done. She said thanks, appa, and hung up. The whole call was maybe four minutes.

My wife would say this is exactly what she warned about — that sending Soyeon away was about my idea of what her life should look like, not hers. And maybe she's right that the school is more about the name and the network than what Soyeon actually needs at sixteen. But I also know what doors open when you have that name on your transcript. I know because I didn't have it and I spent ten years kicking down doors that would have just opened.

Sitting here now after the call, I keep thinking about the four minutes. She got what she needed and hung up. Efficient. I taught her that, I think.

Nudge: "Is 'efficient' how you want her to be with you?"

Response: No. But I don't know how to want something different without undoing the thing I built for her. The whole point of the school, the network, all of it — it's so she never has to struggle the way I did. If the cost is that she treats me like a service provider, maybe that's just what it costs. I just didn't think it would feel like this. Like I'm being handled by my own daughter.
Kept turning over that call with Soyeon. Ended up texting her a photo of the dog sleeping on her old blanket. She sent back a heart emoji. Stupid small thing but I stared at it for a while.

Long week closing the Series C extension. Twelve-hour days, four different law firms, and a lead investor who kept changing the valuation cap at the last minute. I held the line on the terms I wanted but it took everything. By Thursday I was running on instant coffee and sheer stubbornness.

Signed this afternoon. My cofounder — well, he's technically the CTO but he still calls himself cofounder even though he came on in year three — opened a bottle of something expensive in the conference room. Everyone clapped. I said a few words, mostly thanking the team, but honestly the thing I felt most was relief. Not celebration, just the weight coming off. Went home, ate leftover jjigae standing at the kitchen counter, and now I'm sitting on the couch and I can't even turn on the TV. Just tired in a way sleep probably won't fix.

Nudge: "What would celebrating actually look like for you?"

Response: I honestly don't know anymore. Used to be it was the next deal, the next target. Right now I think it would be someone saying, unprompted, that I did well. Not the team clapping because they're supposed to. Just one person who gets what it took.

entries.journal_entry:
Got back from the workshop an hour ago and I'm still thinking about the order we took this morning. A couple from Ghent wants a set of tiles for their renovated kitchen -- they want something "modern but with a nod to Flemish blue." I know exactly what Opa would do with it. I know exactly what I'd do with it too, and they're not the same thing.

I spent the afternoon sketching options. The ones that feel most like me don't really look like what the shop is known for. The ones that look like the shop feel like I'm just tracing someone else's hand. Maybe there's a middle ground but I haven't found it today.

Nudge: "What part of the shop's style feels most distant from your own?"

Response: It's less about the style and more about -- I don't know, I'll figure it out when I start painting.

Finished the Ghent tile set. Went with a design that layers the old Delft patterning under more fluid brushwork -- sort of like the heritage is showing through underneath. Opa looked at them for a long time and then said "that's not bad." From him that's practically a standing ovation.

Choir rehearsal Wednesday went well. We're preparing pieces for the Ascension service in late May. Meneer Peeters gave me a small solo line in the Lassus motet which felt good. I've been singing these pieces since I was fourteen and there's something about the way the harmonies move that I can't get from any other kind of music. It's not about performing, it's about being part of something that's been going for hundreds of years.

Also got an email from a gallery in Brussels that saw my portfolio online. They want to talk about a group show in September. I should be thrilled but I feel weirdly hesitant, like showing the abstract work publicly might make it harder to keep one foot in the workshop world.

Nudge: "What would change at the workshop if the gallery show happened?"

Response: Probably nothing concrete, it's more a feeling. Let me think about it.

Long day at the church restoration. The plaster on the south wall is in worse shape than the diocese estimated so the timeline is blown. Came home and Opa asked when the next batch of souvenir tiles would be ready and I just didn't have it in me to answer.

Writing again because I can't sleep. I know Opa wasn't trying to pressure me, he was just asking a normal question about the business. But it landed wrong. I'm stretched between the restoration job, the workshop orders, and now this Brussels thing, and I feel like everyone needs a piece of me that I haven't figured out yet.

My sister sent a photo of some mass-produced "Flemish style" coasters she saw at a tourist shop near the Markt. She thought it was funny. I didn't. That's what happens when the craft becomes just a product -- the hands behind it disappear. Maybe that's what bothers me about the gallery too, turning something personal into something on display for strangers to consume.

Nudge: "Tourist coasters and a Brussels gallery feel different from the outside. Do they feel different to you?"

Response: Yeah, I know they're different. The coasters erase the maker. The gallery at least credits you. I think what scares me is that once the work is out there, I can't control what it means to people.
Workshop all day, filled six orders. Hands ache. Opa showed me a photo of his father working at the same bench in 1952 and I couldn't tell you what I felt exactly but it was a lot.

Called the Brussels gallery back today. Told them I'm interested but need to see the space first before committing. The curator was friendly, said the theme is "inheritance and rupture" which is almost too on the nose for my situation. She asked if my work engages with craft heritage and I said yes before I'd really thought about whether that's how I want it framed.

After the call I went to Opa's room to tell him. He nodded and said something about how his uncle once turned down a commission from a department store because he didn't want to see the family name on something cheap. I think he was trying to tell me he understands being careful about where the work goes, in his own sideways way.

I keep waiting for someone to tell me it's fine to do both -- the workshop and the gallery, the old patterns and the new ones. Nobody's stopping me except maybe myself. But the art world wants you to pick a lane and the family just wants the shop to keep going, and I'm twenty-two and trying to hold all of it.

Nudge: "What would 'doing both' actually look like week to week?"

Response: I don't know yet. Maybe I'll figure it out after I see the gallery space.

Second entry today because something small happened. Was cleaning brushes after the evening session and put on the Lassus recording from our choir rehearsal. Started humming my solo line and Opa came in and sat down in the corner chair without saying anything. He just listened for a while.

When the piece ended he told me my grandmother used to sing that same motet at Sint-Salvatorskathedraal before I was born. I'd never heard that before. It felt like finding a thread I didn't know was there, connecting her to me through the same music, the same stone walls.

Visited the Brussels gallery space today. It's beautiful -- high ceilings, raw concrete, very contemporary. Standing in it I felt like my workshop pieces would look out of place and my abstract work would look like it's pretending to be something it's not.

Nudge: "What would your work be pretending to be in that space?"

Response: Pretending to be detached from where it comes from, maybe. I don't know, it's hard to put into words.

Church restoration is eating all my daylight hours. The diocese wants the south wall done before Ascension and the plaster situation means I'm basically re-doing the ground layer from scratch. My shoulders are wrecked and I keep getting home too late to work on my own pieces.

Had a thought while I was up on the scaffold today: the anonymous craftsmen who painted these murals five hundred years ago never worried about whether their work belonged in a gallery. They just made what the community needed. Part of me envies that clarity and part of me knows I'd suffocate in it. That's the whole problem, isn't it.

Opa asked if I've decided about the Brussels show. I said I'm leaning yes. He said "good, your grandmother would have liked that." Didn't expect that at all.

Nudge: "You sounded surprised by his reaction. What were you expecting him to say?"

Response: I think I was expecting him to say the shop needs me here. Maybe I even wanted him to, so I'd have an excuse not to go. Hearing him connect it to Oma instead -- that knocked something loose.

Ascension service rehearsal today. Sang the Lassus and it went well but afterwards Meneer Peeters said the choir might switch to a more contemporary repertoire next season because attendance is dropping. A few of us pushed back, said the old music is the reason some of us are still here. He looked tired and said he'd think about it.

I don't understand why everything has to be either/or. Why does updating mean throwing out what came before? The whole conversation put me in a bad mood and I skipped the coffee hour after, which I never do. Mama will probably hear about that and ask what's wrong.

Finished the south wall restoration yesterday, just under the wire before Ascension. Stood back and looked at it for a long time. The colours are brighter now but the composition is the same one some painter laid down centuries ago. I added nothing of my own and that felt right for this particular work. There's a kind of discipline in faithful restoration that I'm starting to respect more than I used to.

Told the Brussels curator I'll do the show. I'm going to bring pieces that sit between the two worlds -- the layered tile work like the Ghent set, where the old patterning bleeds through into something new. Not pure abstraction, not pure heritage, but the conversation between them. She seemed genuinely interested.

Opa helped me crate the Ghent samples this evening. He wrapped each tile in newspaper the way he's done it for fifty years, slow and precise. Didn't say much. Didn't need to. I think he knows this is me trying to carry the thing forward, not away.

Nudge: "A month ago the gallery felt threatening. What shifted?"

Response: Opa's blessing, mostly. And finishing the church wall. I'll think about it more later.

entries.journal_entry:
Had a one-on-one with my manager today where he brought up the new "technical design review board" they're implementing. Starting in January, any architectural change needs to go through a committee of three senior engineers before we can even start coding. I nodded along and said it made sense for quality control, but inside I was thinking about how this is the third approval layer they've added this year.

Part of me gets it. We've had incidents. There's regulatory pressure. The team has grown from 12 to 45 people and you can't have everyone doing their own thing. But I miss the days when I could just have an idea, prototype it over a weekend, and ship it by Wednesday if it worked.

Spent the entire morning in back-to-back meetings about the new microservice architecture. Everyone has opinions, nobody wants to make a decision, and we ended up exactly where we started. I finally just said "look, why don't I build a proof of concept this week and we can evaluate from there?" My tech lead looked uncomfortable and reminded me we need to follow the new process.

The thing is, I know the process exists for good reasons. Last year Tomas pushed that caching change without proper review and it took down production for three hours. But there's got to be a middle ground between chaos and paralysis. Came home irritated and Maja asked what was wrong. I said "nothing, just work stuff" because I didn't want to vent about the same complaints again.

Nudge: "You mentioned not wanting to vent to Maja again. How often do you find yourself holding back like that?"

Response: Probably more than I should. It's just that she's heard the same story before - Erik is frustrated at work, Erik thinks things should be different. At some point it starts to feel self-indulgent. She has her own stress with the kids' schedules and her job. I don't want to be the guy who comes home every day complaining about meetings and processes. So I just... keep it to myself, put the kids to bed, then tinker with my side projects until midnight. It's fine. It works.

Sunday dinner at Maja's parents' place. Her father asked me about work and I gave the usual "it's going well, keeping busy" response. What I didn't mention was that I've been quietly looking at job postings this week. Nothing serious, just... looking. There's a startup in Gothenburg that's hiring senior engineers and their tech blog posts suggest they actually let people build things without eighteen layers of review.

But then I think about what leaving would mean. We bought this apartment two years ago specifically because it's close to Maja's parents and the kids' school. My in-laws help with pickup three days a week. The kids have their friends, their routines. And honestly, the current job pays well, has good benefits, and the people are mostly nice. It's just the bureaucracy that gets to me.

Over dinner, my father-in-law started talking about how his generation valued loyalty to employers and how job-hopping is "a young person's disease." I just smiled and nodded and said something about stability being important. Maja caught my eye across the table and I could tell she knew something was off, but we didn't talk about it in the car ride home. The kids were tired and there wasn't space for that conversation.

Nudge: "You smiled and agreed with your father-in-law about loyalty, while quietly browsing job postings. What's it like holding both of those at once?"

Response: Uncomfortable, if I'm being honest. I'm not sure if I was lying to him or to myself. Part of me does believe in loyalty - I've given this company seven years and I've built things I'm proud of. But another part of me wonders if loyalty is just another word for being afraid to rock the boat.

The thing is, I don't actually know if I'd be happier somewhere else, or if I'd just find new things to be frustrated about. Maybe the startup in Gothenburg would turn into the same bureaucratic mess in three years. Maybe the problem is that I want to do things my way and the world doesn't work like that when you're part of a team. I haven't figured that out yet.

Submitted my first technical design document to the new review board today. It took me two hours to write up something I could have just built in four. Three diagrams, a risk assessment matrix, rollback procedures. The whole thing. And you know what? They approved it in the meeting with almost no changes. The tech lead even said it was "exactly the kind of thorough documentation we need."

I should feel vindicated but mostly I just feel tired. I did the thing. I followed the process. And now I get to actually code, which is what I wanted to do a week ago. Maybe this is just what being a senior engineer at a growing company looks like. You learn to work within the system instead of against it. On the way home I stopped to pick up the kids from football practice and my daughter asked if I could help her build a website for her school project. I said yes immediately. At least that's one thing I can still build without a committee.

entries.journal_entry:
My sister Huda called again about the retirement fund thing. She has this way of bringing it up like she is just asking a casual question but we both know where it is going. "Just curious, do you have anything saved for when you cannot drive anymore?" She knows the answer. She has known for years.

What she does not understand is that I did the office thing for fifteen years and it nearly killed something in me. I am not going back to counting someone else's hours so I can sit in a flat at seventy with money and nothing to show for actually living. Maybe I am wrong. But right now the idea of giving up my mornings at Abu Khalil's cafe to stack dirham in an account I might never touch makes me feel worse than the uncertainty does.

Nudge: "What does the uncertainty actually feel like?"

Response: It feels like Tuesday. Anyway, Huda worries enough for both of us.

Good week for rides. Had a couple of airport pickups that paid well and a half-day tour with a German family visiting Jerash. They wanted someone who could explain things properly, not just point at columns. Felt good using that part of my brain again. Have not done a proper tour in a while and I forgot how much I like it.

Spent the extra money on a proper dinner at Sufra with Tariq and Walid. Lamb mansaf, the real kind. We sat there for three hours talking about nothing important. Drove home with the windows down. Warm night still, even in October.

Nudge: "What made those three hours worth more than saving it?"
Long day, five rides back to back because I said yes to everything on the app. Knees ache and my shoulders are stiff. Going to bed early for once.

Cannot sleep. Lying here thinking about that German family from last week, how the father kept asking me questions about Umayyad architecture like I was a professor. I knew every answer. Fifteen years driving tourists around will do that. Strange how that one afternoon made the whole week feel worthwhile.

Nudge: "Is it the knowing, or someone caring that you know?"

Response: Both, honestly. But if nobody asks, the knowing just sits there.

Woke up still tired from yesterday's marathon. App is showing surge pricing downtown but my back says no. Skipped it. Felt relieved for ten minutes then started calculating what I am losing.

Nudge: "Which number matters more — what you lost or what your back saved you?"

Response: Ask me again when rent is due.
Went out for coffee instead of driving. Sat at Abu Khalil's and watched the street for an hour. Sometimes that is the whole day and it has to be enough.

Third time writing today which is unusual for me. Tariq stopped by the cafe this afternoon and mentioned his son got him set up on one of those delivery apps, the bicycle ones. He is sixty-two and cycling around Amman in this heat delivering shawarma. He laughed about it but I could see he was embarrassed.

That could be me in a few years if the driving work dries up. Or it could not. Hard to know. Tariq still ordered the expensive coffee though, the Turkish one with cardamom they do properly. We split a knafeh. He said the delivery money is not bad and he picks his own hours. Same thing I always say about driving. Maybe we are both just telling ourselves the right story.

Nudge: "What would the wrong story sound like?"

Response: That we gave up something real for comfort that does not last. That choosing the nice afternoon over the stable job was not brave, it was just easier. I do not fully believe that. But I hear it in Huda's voice sometimes.

Had a fare today, a businessman from Riyadh who spent the whole ride talking on his phone about quarterly targets and staff problems. Tipped well but looked miserable. Suit that cost more than my rent and he did not once look out the window at the citadel lit up at night.

People assume I ended up here, like gig work is where you land when everything else falls through. Maybe for some. For me it was a door I walked through on purpose. I know what a Monday morning in an office smells like. I know what it costs. I chose this, and today, watching that man bark into his phone while Amman glowed outside his window, I felt sure about it.

Nudge: "Do you always feel this sure, or just on nights like this?"

Response: Not always. The sure feeling comes and goes. But tonight it is here so I am writing it down.

Rent went up. Landlord sent a message this morning, very polite about it, but two hundred dinars more starting January. I sat with the phone in my hand for a while doing the math. It is tight. It was already tight but now it is the kind of tight where one bad week matters.

Part of me wants to call Huda and tell her she was right, just get it over with. But right does not mean I know what to do differently. I cannot go back to an office. I do not mean I refuse, I mean something in me genuinely cannot. The last time I tried I lasted four months and stopped sleeping. So what is the alternative. Pick up more rides, skip the cafe, eat at home every night. Become the version of me that survives but does not particularly want to.

Later in the evening now. Calmed down a bit. Called Walid and he said his cousin has a spare room in Jabal Amman, smaller place but cheaper, and the neighborhood is actually nicer for walking. Might look at it this weekend. Moving is not ideal at fifty-eight but it is not the end of the world either.

I keep thinking about what I wrote earlier about not being able to go back to office work. That is true but it is also a wall I have built. The real question is whether the wall is protecting something important or just keeping me stuck. I think it is protecting something. The mornings where I sit with coffee and watch the light hit the old stones. The afternoons where I take the long route home because the view from the fourth circle is good at sunset. The dinners that go three hours because nobody has anywhere to be.

These are small things. Nobody would list them on a CV or count them as assets. But they are the thread running through every good day I have had in the past fifteen years. If I have to move to a smaller flat to keep them, that is an easy trade. If I have to drive more shifts, I can do that too. What I cannot do is give them up entirely and still recognize myself.

Nudge: "When did you first realize those small things were the thread?"

Response: Probably the week I quit the logistics job. I walked out at lunch and sat in a park eating falafel and thought, this is the first time in years I have actually tasted my food. That was fifteen years ago and I still remember the taste.

entries.journal_entry:
App keeps glitching today, lost two rides because of it. My head's all over the place.

Nudge: "Sounds like a frustrating day. What's weighing on you most right now?"

Mom called asking if I could cover her prescription copay this month since Dad's physical therapy bills are piling up. Of course I said yes but then I looked at my account and it's like, where is this money supposed to come from? I'll figure it out, I always do.

Nudge: "You said yes right away, then checked your account. What was going through your mind in that moment between?"

Twelve-hour day yesterday and another one today. Holiday surge is good for earnings but my back is killing me from sitting in the car so long. At least I was able to drop off the pharmacy money to Mom on my way between zones. She tried to give me back half of it and I told her no, just keep it in case something else comes up.

Had a passenger tonight who was heading to the airport for some tropical vacation and I sat there thinking about how I haven't taken more than a day off in probably six months. Not complaining really, it is what it is. Just tired.

Nudge: "Six months without a real break. If you could take a day off tomorrow, what would you actually do with it?"

Response: Honestly? I'd probably sleep until noon. Maybe drive out to the mountains just to sit somewhere quiet without my phone buzzing. But then I'd probably spend the whole time calculating how much money I'm losing by not working, so maybe that defeats the purpose.

Sister texted asking if I'm coming to Christmas dinner at her place this year. I want to say yes but I'm looking at the schedule and Christmas Eve and Christmas Day are usually the best earning days because everyone else takes off. Last year I worked both and just stopped by Mom and Dad's for an hour between rides.

Part of me thinks I should just take the hit and be there for the whole dinner. Dad's been doing better but who knows how many of these we get, you know? But then another part of me is like, one good day of driving could cover half my car insurance payment. I don't know. I'll probably end up doing what I always do which is work until the last possible minute and then rush over stressed and half-present anyway.

entries.journal_entry:
Spent most of the morning reviewing the same pull request I started Friday. Nothing wrong with it, just one of those days where your eyes slide over code without absorbing anything. Had chai with Suresh at lunch and he was talking about some new cloud migration project, and I kept thinking about whether Amma took her evening medication yesterday. I should set a reminder but Priya already handles it. I just worry.

Ananya came home from tuition looking drained. She barely touched the sambar. I wanted to ask how her mock exams went but Priya gave me that look, the one that means let her eat first. So I just sat there passing the rice. Normal Monday, I suppose.

Everyone keeps asking why I didn't take the fintech offer. Suresh brought it up again. I have a good position, my PF is solid, Amma and Appa are right here, and I don't need to justify that to anyone.

Nudge: "What exactly does Suresh say that gets under your skin?"

Response: He implies I'm too afraid to try something new.

Appa's knee specialist wants to do a second surgery. The first one was only eight months ago. I told Priya we should get another opinion, but she thinks the doctor knows best. I didn't push it but something doesn't sit right.

Nudge: "What part of the doctor's reasoning bothers you?"

Response: Maybe it's not the doctor. Maybe I'm scared of Appa going through all that pain again.

Quarterly review went fine. Same feedback as always — solid, dependable, keeps the team stable. Raghavan mentioned they might need someone to lead the new payments integration and my name came up, which is nice I guess. Nothing I haven't done before. Sometimes I wonder if that's a compliment or just inertia. Like they know I won't leave so they keep handing me things.

Vikram didn't come for Sunday puja again. Third week in a row. Priya says he's sixteen and it's normal, but she didn't grow up going to the temple every week with her thatha the way I did. It mattered. Not the prayers exactly, but sitting together, the smell of camphor, the drive back when my grandfather would explain some story from the epics. Vikram doesn't have that and I can't seem to make him want it.

Ananya at least still comes, though she spends half the time on her phone. Small mercies.

Got an email from the fintech recruiter again. They bumped the package. Priya saw the notification on my phone and asked about it, and I told her it's nothing. She said she'd support whatever I decide, which somehow made it worse. If she'd just said don't take it, I could've closed the loop.

The thing is I looked at their Glassdoor page. Two rounds of layoffs in eighteen months. Equity that might be worth something or might be worth nothing. No real healthcare plan beyond some startup wellness thing. I have a daughter whose college fees start in two years and parents whose medical bills are only going up. People act like staying put is the coward's choice but they don't have my responsibilities.

Spent the rest of the evening helping Ananya with her physics. She's good at it. Better than I was at her age. That felt right, at least.

Made Paati's rasam recipe for the first time in months. The smell hit me and I had to step away from the stove for a minute. Priya finished it without saying anything.

Nudge: "What came up when you smelled it?"

Response: Her kitchen in Mylapore. Summer holidays. Being ten years old and not worrying about anything.

Vikram announced at dinner that he doesn't want to do engineering. He wants to study film. Film. I didn't say anything for a while and Priya jumped in with something supportive, and Ananya was watching me like she was waiting for an explosion. I just asked him if he'd thought about it seriously and he said yes. That was it. Dinner went on.

But I'm sitting here now and I don't know what I feel. My father was an engineer. His father was a civil servant. There was a path. Not that anyone forced me — I genuinely loved circuits and code — but there was a shape to things. A structure. You studied something that led to something stable, and that stability let you take care of the people around you. What does film lead to? I don't even know what questions to ask.

The worst part is I can hear my own father's voice in my head, and he would have said the same things I'm trying not to say. And Vikram would resent me for it just like I sometimes resented Appa for being so rigid about my sister's choices.

Nudge: "So what are you going to say to him?"

Response: I don't know yet. I just don't want him to stop talking to me about it.

Talked to Amma about Vikram's film idea. She surprised me — said your Paati always wanted to learn Carnatic music but her father said no, and she carried that her whole life. I didn't know that about my grandmother. All those years cooking her recipes and I never knew she had a dream that got shut down.

Doesn't change the practical reality though. The film industry is brutal and unpredictable. I told Vikram I'd look into some programs with him, actual structured ones, not YouTube tutorials. If he's serious, there should be a proper path. Priya thinks I'm trying to control it and maybe she's half right, but I can't just say go for it with no plan. That's not how I'm built.

Work was uneventful. Accepted the payments integration lead role. Raghavan seemed relieved. Seventeen years and I'm still the person they hand things to when they need it done without drama.

entries.journal_entry:
Today was the monthly temple committee meeting and I just cannot shake this strange feeling. Meena aunty said something about how "some people like to take charge of everything" and I know she was talking about me. Everyone was looking at their phones or the floor. I have been running this committee for six years and we have never had a failed event, never run out of food, never had volunteers not show up. But suddenly I am the problem?

I stayed quiet. What else could I do? If I defended myself it would look exactly like what she was accusing me of. But sitting there pretending I did not hear her felt like swallowing glass. My husband said I should just ignore it, that Meena aunty is just jealous because her daughter-in-law moved her out of the main house. Maybe. But that does not explain why no one else spoke up for me.

The thing is, I take charge because if I do not, things fall apart. Remember the Holi celebration last year when I was at Ravi's parents' house? Complete disaster. Cold food, wrong decorations, crying children. But sure, I am the one who is too controlling.

Nudge: "What held you back from responding?"

Dropped off three containers of biryani to the Sharma family down the street. Their son just had surgery and the mother looked like she had not slept in days. She cried when she saw me at the door. This is what it is all about, really. Showing up when people need you.

Nudge: "How did it feel when she cried?"

Response: Honestly, it reminded me of when Amma had her first stroke and neighbors brought us food for weeks. You never forget who showed up. I want to be that person for others. Sometimes I think this is the only thing I am truly good at.

Priya came home crying from school because her friend group excluded her from a birthday party. I spent an hour consoling her but the whole time I kept thinking about that committee meeting. Is this what it feels like? To be pushed out? Too tired to think about this more.

Nudge: "Are you thinking about yourself or Priya?"

Response: Priya, obviously. She is thirteen and everything feels like the end of the world at that age. I am just tired.

Ravi's sister called asking if we can host his parents for six weeks instead of two this summer. She has a work trip to Singapore that "cannot be rescheduled." I said yes before even thinking about it. What else could I say?

Now I am mentally reorganizing everything. Priya's room will have to be cleared for them. I will need to cook separate low-sodium meals for Appa. The bathroom schedule becomes a nightmare with seven people. But Lakshmi made it sound like I was the only solution, and I suppose I am.

Nudge: "Did you want to say something else?"

Response: Wanting and doing are different things. Lakshmi works, I do not. She has meetings in Singapore, I have school drop-offs. The math is simple. But yes, fine, I wanted to say "let me check with Ravi first" like a normal person. Instead I just absorbed it like always.

Nothing happened today, just the usual. But I have been thinking about something my cousin Anita said when she visited last month. She asked why I "gave up" my career to stay home, as if I am sitting here watching television all day. I manage a household, a family, a community. I coordinate schedules for five people, handle three different school systems' requirements, manage all the finances, plan every festival, and support whoever needs me.

Ravi could not find his passport last week. Who found it in twenty minutes? Me. Priya needed a costume for her Bharatanatyam recital. Who stayed up sewing until midnight? Me. Vikram's college applications needed someone to proofread seven essays. Me.

But because there is no salary, it is not real work. Because I do not have a LinkedIn profile anymore, I have "given up." Anita has a corner office and takes two vacations a year. Good for her. She also calls her mother once a month and sends birthday cards late. I am not saying one is better. I am just saying they are different, and I am tired of having to defend my choice.

Committee met again. I volunteered to step back from leading the Navratri planning. Let someone else handle the vendor contracts and volunteer schedules. Meena aunty looked surprised. Everyone did, actually.

Nudge: "How did it feel to step back?"

Made chai for myself this afternoon and sat on the balcony for fifteen minutes doing nothing. The house was quiet. Just me and the crows arguing in the mango tree. Cannot remember the last time I did this.

Nudge: "What made today different?"

Vikram called from university just to talk. Not because he needed money or help with something. Just to tell me about his professor who reminds him of his grandfather, and about the dosa place he found near campus. We talked for forty-five minutes.

I think I did something right with him. He is kind. He notices people. Last month he organized a food drive at his dorm because he saw international students struggling during spring break when the dining hall closed. He did not tell me about it - his roommate's mother mentioned it. That is the part that makes me proudest. He does not do it for recognition.

Nudge: "What do you think shaped him that way?"

Response: I wish I could take credit but honestly, I think he just watched. Watched me drop off food to the Sharmas. Watched his grandmother live with us for three years. Watched Ravi stay late at office but still make every recital and cricket match. Children learn what they see, not what you tell them.

Second entry today. In-laws arrived this evening, four days early. Lakshmi's flight got moved up. I had just finished cleaning but the guest room mattress has not been aired. Amma looked at the sheets and I know she noticed. Too tired to care right now.

Amma has been here four days and I am already losing my mind. She reorganized my spice cabinet. MY spice cabinet that I have arranged the same way for fifteen years. She said she was "helping" because it was "inefficient." I have managed three children, a husband, a house, and an entire temple committee with that "inefficient" cabinet.

I did not say anything. What would be the point? She is seventy-two and she is a guest. But Ravi saw my face when I opened the cabinet door and he just shrugged. Shrugged! As if his mother rearranging my kitchen is completely normal and I should just accept it.

The worst part is that she is not wrong. Her arrangement probably is more efficient. But that is not the point. The point is that this is my home, my space, the one domain where I should have some say. But apparently even here I am meant to just smile and adjust. I made everyone's favorite dinner anyway because what else am I going to do, let everyone starve over cumin placement?

Nudge: "What would having a say look like?"

Response: It does not matter. Four more weeks and then things go back to normal. I just need to survive until then.

Priya asked me today why I always have to be "in charge of everything." She was annoyed because I reminded her three times about her homework. Three times! Because she was on her phone and the deadline was in two hours. But apparently I am controlling.

I told her that someone has to keep track of things. She rolled her eyes and said "you could just let me fail once." What kind of mother lets her child fail on purpose? I did not hover over Vikram and look how he turned out - responsible, organized, successful. But Priya is different. She needs more reminders. If I do not push her, she will coast.

Ravi thinks I should "give her space." Easy for him to say. He is at office twelve hours a day. He does not see the daily negotiation of getting this child to do anything. When she fails a test, no one will ask where her father was. They will ask what I was doing.

But maybe everyone is right and I am the problem. Maybe I should just stop caring. Let the laundry pile up, let the homework go undone, let the spice cabinet stay however Amma wants it. Then we will see how things run.

Nudge: "What would happen if you did stop?"

Response: Chaos. Or maybe nothing. Maybe everything would be fine and I would realize I have been making myself essential when I am actually just in the way. That thought is worse than the chaos, honestly.

Amma and I had an argument today. Or not an argument exactly - she does not argue. She just makes observations. "In my house we always did it this way." "You work so hard, beta, but maybe if you organized differently..." "Priya seems stressed, have you thought about letting her breathe?"

I finally said something. I told her that I appreciate her concern but this is my house and my family and I have been managing fine for twenty years. She got very quiet and went to her room. Ravi says I was too harsh. Maybe. But she has been here two weeks and every day there is a new suggestion, a new correction, a new way I am falling short.

The thing is, she ran her own household for forty years. I understand the need to feel useful, to have a role. When she moved in with Lakshmi she lost all of that. So she comes here and tries to find her place by improving mine. I see the pattern. I am not blind to it.

But seeing the pattern does not make it easier to live with. And I keep wondering - is this my future? Will I be the mother-in-law who cannot stop fixing things because fixing things is all I know how to do? Will Priya write journal entries about me reorganizing her kitchen?

Nudge: "What would you want to be remembered for?"

Response: Not for being right. Not for having the most organized spice cabinet or the best-run committee. I want Vikram and Priya and even little Arun to remember that I showed up. That when things fell apart, I was there. That I fed people not because I needed to control the menu but because I believe you cannot love someone properly if you let them go hungry. That is why I do all of this. I think I forgot that somewhere along the way.

entries.journal_entry:
Orientation session today. Sat in the back row out of habit, then realized I was the only one over forty in the entire lecture hall. The program coordinator kept talking about "building your professional foundation" and I wanted to raise my hand and say that some of us already had a foundation, thank you, before it was demolished without warning.

I kept my mouth shut. That is what you do. You do not embarrass yourself by complaining about circumstances nobody asked about. But walking back to my apartment afterward, I felt it — that heaviness in my chest. I am not sure if this was the right decision. Forty thousand dollars a year to be lectured about organizational behavior by someone who has never actually run an organization.

Nudge: "What would make this decision feel worth it by the time you graduate?"

Response: A title that commands respect again. A role where people come to me for guidance, not the other way around. I suppose I want to walk into a room and have people assume I belong there, the way they used to.

Group project assigned today. I was placed with three twenty-somethings who immediately started a WhatsApp thread I had to ask them to explain how to join. They scheduled our first meeting for 10 PM on a weeknight.

Could not sleep after writing that last note. The 10 PM meeting is bothering me more than it should. Back when I managed the retail lending division, if someone scheduled a meeting for 10 PM, there had better have been an emergency — a systems failure, a regulatory audit, something serious. These kids treat it like nothing.

I know I am supposed to adapt. That is the whole point of coming back to school — proving I can learn new ways of working. But there is a difference between adapting and abandoning every standard you spent a career developing. I ran teams. I built processes. I had an executive assistant who managed my calendar. Now I am asking a twenty-three-year-old named "Brayden" how to download an app on my phone.

The worst part is that I smiled and thanked him. What else was I supposed to do? Throw a tantrum about how things used to be? I have seen those people. They become cautionary tales whispered about in break rooms. So I will show up to the 10 PM meeting and I will contribute my ideas and I will be pleasant about all of it. That is the only path forward.

But I am writing this at 11:47 PM because I cannot stop replaying the moment when Brayden said "it's super easy, here let me show you" with that patient voice you use for elderly relatives who cannot work the remote control.

Nudge: "You mentioned you smiled and thanked him. What would have happened if you'd responded differently — said something honest about how that moment felt?"

Professor Chen called on me in class today and I gave a detailed answer drawing on my experience restructuring our branch network in 2019. She nodded politely and said "that's one perspective, but let's look at what the research shows." Then proceeded to present a case study that contradicted half of what I said.

I stayed after class to discuss it further and she said my "practitioner insights are valuable" but that academic rigor requires us to "question our assumptions." I am not sure what assumptions she thinks I have. I was there. I saw what worked. The branch network I restructured is still operational — or it was until the whole bank got acquired.

My daughter texted me later asking how school was going. I told her it was fine.

Nudge: "When you told your daughter things were fine, was that to protect her from worrying, or something else?"

Response: She has her own problems to deal with. My job is to handle mine.

The 10 PM meeting went until midnight. Brayden talked for forty-five minutes about "leveraging synergies" without saying anything concrete. I drafted the entire project outline afterward because someone had to. Submitted it to the group chat at 2 AM.

Interesting development. The group used my outline almost word for word. Brayden presented it in class as "our collaborative framework" and the professor praised the "clear structure and practical grounding." Nobody mentioned that I wrote it. I did not correct them.

Here is the thing though. I felt something shift when I saw the professor nodding along. That familiar feeling from the old days — when you put something together that works and people respond to it. The recognition does not need to be public. I know what I contributed. The professor knows the quality improved dramatically between our brainstorm and our deliverable.

Maybe this is how it works now. You do the work, you let other people take the stage, you build influence through competence rather than title. I am not sure I like it. But I am starting to see that there might be a way to operate here that is not completely foreign to who I am.

My ex-wife used to say I cared too much about getting credit. She meant it as criticism. Maybe she had a point, or maybe she did not understand what it is like to have your competence be the only thing you can rely on. Either way, the work got done. That matters.

Nudge: "What made you decide not to correct them about the outline? Was it strategic, or did something else hold you back?"

Response: I suppose I was testing something. Whether I could let go of the credit and still feel like myself. And I did, mostly. But there was also this — if I had spoken up, I would have been the old man demanding acknowledgment. I have seen how quickly people dismiss that. Better to let the work speak and see who notices.

Career services appointment today. The counselor — probably thirty years old at most — asked me what kind of "pivot" I was hoping to make. I explained that I am not pivoting, I am rebuilding. She looked confused.

I walked her through my background and she said the market is "challenging" for candidates my age but that I have "transferable skills." Transferable. As if thirty years of leadership is a foreign currency that needs to be exchanged for something more useful. I thanked her for her time and left with a folder of job search resources I will probably never open.

Midterm grades posted. I received the highest score in the section on my case analysis. Professor Chen left a comment: "Exceptional integration of theory and practice." I printed it out and put it in my desk drawer.

Nudge: "Why did you print it? What will having that piece of paper there do for you?"

Response: Evidence. Proof that I was not imagining things — that I do know what I am doing, even in this environment. On the days when I feel like an artifact, I can open that drawer and see that someone with credentials agrees I have something to offer.

Group presentation today. I suggested we rehearse beforehand but was outvoted. "We'll figure it out live," Brayden said. So we went in unrehearsed and it showed — stumbling over transitions, contradicting each other on key points. Professor Chen gave us a B-minus and commented that our "individual insights were strong but integration was lacking."

I wanted to say something afterward but held back. What would be the point? They would just see me as the cranky old guy who cannot roll with informality. Better to take the grade and move on. But it grates. I built my reputation on preparation. On never walking into a room without knowing exactly what I was going to say and why. Watching twenty years of professional instincts get overridden by committee feels like swallowing glass.

Sunday afternoon. First time in weeks I do not have something due tomorrow. Went to a coffee shop near campus and just sat for a while. Watched the students come and go with their laptops and their confidence and their entire futures stretching out in front of them.

I remember that feeling. Not the specifics — I was not a coffee shop person at twenty-five — but the sense that time was on my side. That I could make mistakes and recover. That every door led to another door. Now I feel like I am running out of doors. Or maybe I already picked one and the rest all closed behind me.

Here is what I have been circling around for weeks: I came back to school because I could not stand being unemployed. Being introduced at parties as "Harold, he's figuring out his next chapter." The pity. The sudden availability of career advice from people who had never managed anyone. An MBA gave me something to say when people asked what I was doing. A credential that signals I am still in the game.

But sitting here today, I wonder if that is enough of a reason. If I am just collecting another piece of paper to avoid facing the question of what I actually want from whatever time is left.

Email from a former colleague today. She is now VP at a regional credit union and asked if I would be interested in consulting on a branch optimization project over the summer. The pay is modest but the work is exactly what I know how to do.

I said yes immediately, then spent an hour wondering if I should have negotiated. Old habits. But the truth is I would have done it for free just to be back in a room where people want my opinion, not because of an assignment but because I have answers they need. That probably sounds desperate. Maybe it is.

Nudge: "You said you would have done it for free. What does that tell you about what you're really looking for right now?"

Response: That I need to matter somewhere. The MBA is about credentials, about what looks right on paper. But this — someone calling because they remembered I knew what I was doing — that is about actually being useful. I suppose I have been confusing the two.

End of semester. Submitted my last paper at 11:58 PM. Overall GPA: 3.7. I should feel accomplished. Mostly I feel relieved that I can stop pretending to be something I am not for three months. The consulting project starts Monday.

entries.journal_entry:
Spent the morning glazing the new bowl series — that deep blue with copper undertones I've been chasing for months. Watched the light shift across the wet surface and forgot about everything else for a while. This is why I do this. The moment when the color catches and something ordinary becomes something worth looking at.

Then Mama called to ask if I could take her to the pharmacy. Of course I said yes. Packed up the brushes, washed my hands, drove her across town. We stopped for knafeh after and she held my hand in the car on the way back, said she's grateful I came home.

I am too, most days. But I also think about how I used to have whole weeks where nobody needed me for anything, and I could just make things until my hands hurt. I don't know if I miss that or if I just think I should miss it.

My cousin Nadia asked me to paint something for her new apartment. Not commissioned — just asked, the way family asks. Like my time and materials are a gift I owe her because we share blood. I said yes because what else do you say when your aunt is standing right there and Nadia's going through a divorce and everyone's been worried about her.

I don't even know what she wants. Something cheerful, she said. Blue maybe. Something to look at in the morning.

So now I'm supposed to make something cheerful and blue on top of the three workshop sessions I'm prepping, the gallery order that's due next week, and driving Mama to her appointments. And the worst part is I can't even be properly upset because Nadia's having a hard year and I should want to help. I do want to help. I just also want someone to ask me what I need instead of what I can give.

Had a glass of wine on the balcony after everyone left tonight. The city noise, the warm air, the taste of it — that small hour was the best part of my whole week. And I feel stupid admitting that because it's nothing, it's just sitting there, but everything else is for other people and that was just for me.

Nudge: "What would you need right now if someone asked?"

Response: I think I'd want someone to take over the pharmacy runs for a week. Just one week where I don't have to be anywhere at a specific time. I'd finish the gallery order early and maybe drive to the Dead Sea with Reem and Dana, just for a day. Float in the water, eat too much, laugh at nothing. Come back tired in a good way instead of the way I usually come back tired.

It sounds selfish when I write it out. But I used to do things like that more often, and I was better company. I was a better artist too, I think. Less resentful. I don't want to become someone who counts favors.

Workshop went well today. Eight kids, ages seven to twelve. We made pinch pots and one girl named Sana made hers into a turtle. She was so proud she carried it to her mother with both hands like it was made of glass.

My brother Tariq said he might take Mama to the pharmacy on Thursday. We'll see if he actually follows through.

Nudge: "What happens if he doesn't follow through?"

Can't sleep. Tariq texted to say he has a work thing Thursday and can I cover after all. Of course I can. Of course I will.

I keep thinking about what Reem said last month, that I should just say no sometimes and see what happens. But she doesn't have a mother who needs her, and she doesn't have a brother who conveniently forgets his commitments, and she doesn't understand that if I don't do it then it just doesn't get done or Mama has to take a taxi alone and feel like a burden.

The thing is I don't even want to stop helping. I want to stop being the only one who helps. There's a difference but it gets lost in translation when I try to explain it to anyone in my family.

Started a new sketch tonight though. Something abstract, just shapes and warmth. No one asked for it. It's ugly so far but it's mine.

Finished Nadia's painting yesterday. Took it to her apartment and she cried when she saw it — not because it was good, I think, but because she needed someone to do something soft for her and I was the one who did it. We sat on her empty floor surrounded by moving boxes and she told me things about her marriage I didn't know. How small she'd felt. How invisible.

I stayed longer than I planned. Made her tea with the one pot she'd unpacked. Told her about the residency I turned down two years ago and how I sometimes wonder if I made the right call coming back. She said at least you had a call to make. At least you have something that's yours.

That stayed with me on the drive home. I do have something that's mine. The studio, the clay, the color mixing — all of it. Even when I'm tired and resentful and covered in glaze at midnight, I'm still making things that didn't exist before I made them.

Treated myself to good coffee this morning. The expensive kind, with cardamom. Sat in the garden and drank it slow while the light came up. Small luxuries. I'm learning they're not small at all — they're how I keep going. Without the good coffee and the Dead Sea trips and the wine on the balcony, I'd hollow out. I need those things as much as my family needs the pharmacy runs. Maybe that's okay.
