entries.journal_entry: First full week of spring semester done. Managed to get through the group project meeting without making a fool of myself. The other three in my group are all in their mid-twenties. One of them, Priya, actually asked for my opinion on the policy brief outline. Small thing, but I didn't fumble the answer. Drove home to check on Mom after. She'd left the porch light on for me.

Professor Vasquez handed back the first reading response today. I got a B-minus. Fine. I expected that. But the comment in the margin said "push beyond summary -- what's YOUR position?" and I've been turning that over since 2pm. I summarized the readings accurately. I cited everything properly. The formatting was correct. I don't understand what she wants that I didn't give.

The younger students all seem comfortable throwing out half-formed opinions in class. This one guy, Marcus, will say "I think the entire framework is wrong" and then Professor Vasquez nods and says "interesting, say more." If I said something like that I'd need to be sure it was right first. You don't just declare something wrong without evidence. That's how I was taught. You do the reading, you report what it says, you don't pretend you're smarter than the author.

My ex-wife Linda used to say I was allergic to having opinions. That's not fair. I have plenty of opinions. I just don't see the point of broadcasting them when nobody asked. The paper asked me to respond to the readings. I responded. If "push beyond summary" means I'm supposed to disagree with published experts for the sake of classroom performance, then I don't know what I'm doing here.

Nudge: "What would happen if you did push beyond summary?"

Group meeting went sideways. The other three want to argue in the brief that the county should eliminate paper records entirely. I think that's reckless -- I've seen what happens when digital systems crash and there's no backup. But they all agreed so fast I just nodded. Sat in the parking ramp for twenty minutes after. Should have said something. Didn't.

Nudge: "You had thirty years of evidence. What stopped you?"

Mom had a fall this weekend. Nothing broken, thank God, but she bruised her hip badly and the visiting nurse said she needs to use a walker now. Spent all of Saturday rearranging her living room furniture so she has a clear path. She kept saying not to bother, that she was fine, that she'd been walking in that house for forty-three years without help. I moved the ottoman anyway.

My sister Karen called from Phoenix to "check in" which meant asking me if I'd looked into assisted living yet. I said no because Mom doesn't want that and I'm not going to force her. Karen said I was "enabling her stubbornness." I said okay and changed the subject. It's easy to have opinions about what Mom needs when you live two thousand miles away. I could have said that but I didn't. Not worth the phone call it would turn into.

Drove back to campus Sunday night. Missed the reading for Monday's class. I'll skim it before 9am and hope she doesn't cold-call me. I keep telling myself I can handle all of this -- school, Mom, the drive, the apartment -- but weeks like this I feel like I'm running on fumes. Not complaining, just noting it. Everybody has a lot going on.

Office hours today, first time I actually went. Sat outside Professor Vasquez's door for maybe ten minutes before I knocked. She was eating a sandwich, which made me feel bad for interrupting, but she waved me in. Asked about the reading response grade, what she meant by "your position." She said something about the difference between understanding an argument and engaging with it. That you can agree with a reading and still have something to add.

I nodded a lot. She asked me what I thought about the Ostrom reading specifically and I said I thought the commons framework made sense but I'd seen it fail in county-level resource sharing. She lit up at that, said that was exactly the kind of thing she wanted in my papers. Real-world pushback grounded in experience. I said I didn't think that counted as academic.

She laughed, not unkindly. Said "Gerald, your experience IS a source." I felt something shift, not sure what. Like being told a door I thought was locked was actually just stuck. Walked back to my apartment and reread my B-minus paper. She's right, it reads like a book report. I could have written about the three times our county tried shared digital archives and what actually happened. I knew all of that. I just didn't think it was my place to say it.

Group meeting again. We're finalizing the policy brief draft this week. Priya divided the sections and I got the implementation risks portion, which is fine because that's where my actual knowledge lives. Started writing it last night and it came out faster than anything I've written for this program so far. Three pages in two hours.

I included the thing about our county's failed digital archive migration. The one where the server crashed during a property tax audit and we had to pull paper backups from a warehouse in Proctor that hadn't been climate-controlled. Lost about six weeks of records. The kind of thing that doesn't show up in policy papers because the people who lived through it don't write policy papers.

Sent the draft to the group chat. Marcus replied with a thumbs-up emoji. Priya said "this is really good, Gerald." I stared at that message for longer than I should have. It shouldn't matter this much, a twenty-six-year-old telling me my writing is good. But the work I did at the county for three decades -- nobody ever said anything about it. You filed things right, nobody noticed. You filed things wrong, everybody noticed. Priya's two words did something the county never did.

Nudge: "Why does Priya's 'really good' land harder than thirty years of doing it right?"

Response: Because at the county, doing it right was just the minimum. Nobody thanks you for the minimum. Priya read what I wrote and responded to it like it mattered. Like what I thought was the point, not just whether the form was filled out correctly. I didn't know I needed that. Apparently I did.

Karen called again. She's been talking to Mom's neighbor, Barb, behind my back. Barb apparently told Karen that Mom left the stove burner on last Tuesday and didn't notice until the pot was dry. Karen said we need to "have a family conversation" about next steps. I said I'd handle it. She said I always say that and then nothing changes.

She's not wrong. But the way she frames it -- like I'm failing at this -- makes it impossible to hear. I know Mom needs more help. I've been the one driving back every other weekend, moving furniture, filling prescriptions, bringing groceries. Karen sends a check once a month and calls that "doing her part." I could say that. I won't. It would start something that can't be unsaid and then every holiday, every phone call would have that underneath it.

So I said I'd look into some options and we'd talk next weekend. I already know what will happen. She'll send me links to places in Arizona. I'll say Mom won't leave Duluth. She'll say I'm being unreasonable. I'll fold the conversation up and put it away and nothing will change. The whole thing makes me feel like a coward, which I know isn't the right word, but I can't find a better one. I just don't want to be the person who starts the fight.

Nudge: "What's the word if it's not coward?"

Response: Careful, maybe. Or just tired. I don't know. Coward implies I'm scared and it's not exactly that. It's more like I've calculated the cost of every version of this conversation and none of them end well. So I pick the one where at least nobody is yelling. Even if I'm the one absorbing it all. I've been doing that math my whole life.

Valentines Day was yesterday and I thought about Linda for the first time in a while. Not missing her exactly. Just remembering how she'd get frustrated when I wouldn't pick the restaurant. She'd say "just choose something, Gerald." I never could. Felt like if I picked wrong she'd be disappointed. Easier to let her decide and go along. She left. So maybe going along wasn't working either.

Policy brief presentation today. I was supposed to present the implementation risks section. Practiced in my apartment last night, had the whole thing mapped out, knew the material cold. Then I got to the classroom and Professor Vasquez asked who wanted to start and Marcus volunteered our group and suddenly I was standing at the front with three people half my age and a room full of strangers.

I got through it. Mostly read from my notes, which I know isn't great. My voice did that thing where it goes flat and monotone, the same voice I used to use in staff meetings at the county. Just get through it, say the correct things, don't draw attention. Priya jumped in during the Q&A and built on one of my points about legacy system failures. She cited my section like it was a published source. Professor Vasquez asked me a follow-up and I gave a real answer -- not a summary of what I'd read, an actual answer from what I know. It came out awkward but it came out.

Afterward Marcus said "dude, you killed it" and I think he was being generous but I'll take it. Something about surviving the thing you dread. Doesn't make you less afraid, just makes you slightly less certain you'll fail. Which I suppose is progress. Incremental, barely visible progress.

Long day. Drove to Duluth after class because Barb called and said Mom seemed confused about what day it was. Got there at 9pm. Mom was fine, watching Jeopardy, knew every answer in the history category. Made me a grilled cheese without me asking. I sat at the kitchen table and for a second it was like being twelve again.

Had the conversation. Told her Karen was worried, Barb was worried, I was worried. She said she was fine and that people should mind their own business. I said we might need to think about someone coming in during the day, just to help. She said "I don't need a babysitter, Gerald." I said okay. Drove back at midnight. The conversation Karen wanted me to have lasted about ninety seconds. I don't know if I tried hard enough or if trying harder would have just been cruel.

Nudge: "What would trying harder have looked like?"

Response: Sitting there longer, I guess. Listing reasons. Making her feel like she can't take care of herself. I don't want to do that to her. She's my mother. There's a difference between helping someone and cornering them.

Got the revised reading response back from Professor Vasquez. B-plus. She wrote "much better -- this is your voice" in the margin. I read that comment three times. Slid the paper into the folder where I keep things. Not sure why I'm keeping a paper folder of graded assignments like a kid bringing home a report card. But I am.

Group turned in the policy brief. Final version was solid. Priya formatted it, Marcus did the executive summary, I did the implementation risks, and our fourth member Aiden did the literature review. It reads like something a real policy shop would produce. I know that because I've read hundreds of those in my career. Nice to be on the writing end for once instead of the filing end.

Karen called. I told her about the conversation with Mom. She said ninety seconds wasn't enough and I needed to be firmer. I told her she was welcome to come to Duluth and be firm herself. There was a long pause. She said that was unfair. I said maybe. But I said it. First time in a long time I didn't just absorb what she threw and move on. Don't know if it changed anything. Probably made next month's phone call harder. But I was tired of calculating the cost of every sentence before I said it. Sometimes the math isn't worth it.

Nudge: "You stopped doing the math. What did you feel instead?"

Response: Lighter. Just for a second. Then immediately guilty, like I'd broken a rule I didn't know I was allowed to break. But the lighter part came first. That's new.

entries.journal_entry:
Spent the last two hours staring at a term sheet from a Lisbon-based startup that wants us to come in as operational partners. The numbers are solid enough but what actually caught me was the founder, this woman who walked away from a consulting gig to build a port logistics platform from scratch. She reminded me of how I felt fifteen years ago when I signed my first warehouse lease with money I did not really have.

The thing is, I told Ingrid I would slow down this year. We said that after the second exit. That was supposed to be the point where we had enough runway to just breathe for a while. And we do have the runway. But I keep circling back to this Lisbon deal like a dog that will not leave a bone alone.

I know what the smart move is. Park the capital, let the reserves do their work, stay disciplined. But I feel this pull and I cannot explain it rationally. It is not about the money. It is something about the feeling of standing at the edge of something that does not have a guaranteed outcome.

Nudge: "What does that edge feel like compared to the breathing room you mentioned?"

Call with the Lisbon founder again today, third one this week. She pitched the expansion roadmap and honestly it is ambitious but not reckless. I ran the downside scenarios on a napkin while she was talking, which is a habit Ingrid would recognize and roll her eyes at. Worst case we lose about what I spent on that sailboat restoration three years ago, so it is recoverable.

Ingrid asked over dinner if I had decided. I said not yet. That is technically true but I think she could tell from my tone that I have already half-committed in my head. She did not push back, just said "make sure you are not chasing the feeling again." I told her I did not know what she meant, which was a lie.

Valentine's dinner last night, the Italian place near Erasmusbrug. We had a good time, actually managed to not talk about business for most of it. Ingrid brought up taking a trip to Gotland this summer to visit her mother, maybe staying a few weeks. I said yes immediately, genuinely meant it.

But then on the walk home I caught myself thinking about the Lisbon timeline and whether a few weeks in Sweden would slow down onboarding if we signed the deal. I did not say that out loud. I wonder when I started splitting like this, one half of me wanting to just be present and the other half always running the next scenario.

Nudge: "When you say splitting, which half feels more like you?"

Response: That is the problem, they both do. The planning half is not some unwanted intruder, it is the part that built everything we have. But the part that wanted to just walk along the water with Ingrid without calculating anything, that felt real too. I think they used to be the same thing but somewhere along the way they started pulling in different directions.

Saturday morning, sitting with coffee in the kitchen. The house is quiet. Ingrid went to the market early which means she is buying too much cheese again and I will pretend to be surprised when she gets back.

I made a decision last night. I am going to pass on the Lisbon deal. Not because the numbers are wrong or because the founder is not impressive but because I realized I was looking for a feeling, not a business case. The feeling of the leap. That rush of committing to something uncertain and then having to make it work. I know that feeling well and it has served me, but I do not think chasing it right now is the responsible move.

We have the reserves in a good place. The studio has three portfolio companies that need attention. Ingrid and I are in a solid rhythm. I do not need to blow that up to feel alive. At least that is what I am telling myself this morning. Ask me again next week when I am bored.

One of our portfolio companies, the cold storage outfit in Antwerp, just lost their anchor client. Found out this morning from a panicked call from the founder who I could barely understand because he was speaking so fast. This is the company where we parked the biggest chunk of operational capital.

I spent the entire day on calls, restructuring scenarios, talking to our lawyer about contract terms. By evening I had a workable plan but the whole time there was this tight feeling in my chest. Not about the money exactly, we can absorb the hit if we need to. It is more that I had just gotten comfortable with the idea of things being stable and now the ground shifted.

What irritates me is that I know this is part of the game. I have been through worse. The first company almost went under twice before we found our footing. But back then the instability felt like fuel. Now it just feels like a threat to something I have built up carefully. I do not know when that switch happened.

Antwerp situation is stabilizing. We found a replacement client, not as big but enough to keep the operation running while we diversify the pipeline. I spent three days in Antwerp this week working on-site with the team and it reminded me that I actually enjoy the problem-solving part of this work more than the deal-making part.

There is something specific about walking into a warehouse that is half-empty and figuring out how to fill it. Not the adrenaline of a new deal but the satisfaction of fixing what is broken. Maybe that is the version of this work that does not require me to keep jumping off cliffs.

Had a long phone call with Ingrid tonight and told her about the replacement client. She sounded relieved, which I noticed because she had been careful not to sound worried all week. She said she is glad I was there in person rather than just sending emails. She is right. The founder needed to see someone calm in the room. I think I needed to be that person for my own reasons too.

Got an email from the Lisbon founder today. She closed the round without us, brought in a Madrid-based firm instead. Good for her. But I would be lying if I said it did not sting. Not because we lost the deal, I walked away from it deliberately. It stings because reading her update about the progress they have made in the last three weeks made me feel like I am standing still.

I know that is irrational. The studio is doing fine, the Antwerp crisis proved the model works, we are in good shape. But "good shape" feels like treading water sometimes. Like I am maintaining rather than building. And maintaining is not something I have ever been good at sitting with.

Ingrid would say this is exactly the pattern she warned me about. I get things to a good place, feel restless, then go looking for the next thing to keep myself occupied. She is probably right. But knowing it is a pattern does not make the restlessness go away. I keep checking the inbox for something that will make tomorrow feel different from today.

Nudge: "You said maintaining is not something you sit with. But you also chose it. What changed?"

Response: Nothing changed. I just had a slow day and read an email at the wrong time. It is fine. The Antwerp thing took a lot out of me and I probably just need a proper weekend off. Ingrid suggested we drive out to Kinderdijk, maybe I will take her up on that.

Did the Kinderdijk trip. Walked for hours, barely talked, ate pannenkoeken at that place near the windmills. It was exactly what I needed and I managed to leave my phone in the car for most of it.

Nudge: "What was it like having the phone in the car?"

Portfolio review meeting today with all three founders. The numbers are where they should be, maybe slightly ahead. The cold storage team in Antwerp has a real pipeline now and the other two are humming along. I should feel good about this and I suppose I do, in the way you feel good about a clean audit.

A guy I used to work with in Stockholm called afterward, wants to pitch me on a drone delivery concept for Nordic rural areas. My first instinct was interest. My second instinct was to check whether the first instinct was the problem. I told him to send the deck and I would look at it. Not committing to anything, just looking.

Stayed up too late reading the drone deck. It is actually well put together. The unit economics make sense for the regions they are targeting and the regulatory window is real. I caught myself building a financial model in a spreadsheet at one in the morning, which is exactly the kind of thing I do when something grabs me.

But I closed the laptop and went to bed. Ingrid was already asleep. I lay there thinking about the Antwerp scare and how it felt to have something we built nearly fall apart. I do not want to stretch us thin again. The reserves are there for a reason. Tomorrow I will look at the deck with fresh eyes and probably find three deal-breakers I missed because I was running on adrenaline.

Looked at the drone deck again over lunch. Found one real concern around the supply chain for replacement parts but the other issues I expected to find were not there. The founder has thought through most of the obvious failure modes.

Told Ingrid about it. She asked how much capital it would need and I gave her the number. She went quiet for a while and then said it was my call but that she wanted me to think about why the last time I felt this way about a deal I chose to walk away. I do not have a good answer for why this time feels different. Maybe it does not feel different. Maybe I am just further from the last scare.

Nudge: "Is the distance from the scare the reason, or the excuse?"

Response: Probably the excuse. I know that. Three weeks ago I was rattled by Antwerp and swore I would not overextend. Now I am sitting here telling myself this one is different because the deck looks solid. But every deck looks solid when you want it to. The honest answer is I miss the feeling of building something from nothing and I am trying to dress that up as a business decision.

Called my Stockholm friend back and told him I need two weeks to think. He said fine. I keep going back and forth and I hate that I cannot just pick a lane and stay in it.

Nudge: "What if both lanes are yours?"

Response: Then I would need to figure out a speed that works for both of them. Ingrid said something like that once, that maybe the problem is not what I want but how much of it I want at once. I did not really hear it at the time but it is starting to make sense. Maybe there is a version of this where I do not have to choose between building and protecting, I just have to stop pretending one of them is the whole answer.

entries.journal_entry:
Finished the last batch of grading for the semester and walked home the long way through campus. Snow was piling up on the quad and I could see my breath and I just felt good about it, the routine of it. Same walk, same coat, same apartment waiting with the soup I made this morning.

Nudge: "What is it about the same walk that does it for you?"

Response: I think it's that nothing is asking me to figure anything out. The route is automatic. I just get to exist in it without calculating.

My landlord mentioned he might sell the building in the spring. Just casually, at the mailbox, like it was nothing. I said something like "oh interesting" and went inside and sat on my bed for twenty minutes staring at the wall.

Nudge: "That sat with you, huh?"

Still thinking about what Doug said yesterday. I looked up what happens when a landlord sells a rental property in Ontario and the tenant protections are there on paper but I've heard enough stories from people in my program to know that "renoviction" is basically a verb now. My rent is $1,150 which is absurd for Toronto, everyone tells me that, and if I had to find a new place I'd be looking at $1,800 minimum for something comparable. That's an extra $650 a month I literally do not have.

I keep running the numbers. I could technically absorb it if I picked up more TA hours but then my thesis timeline stretches and my funding window is fixed. I mapped it out on a napkin at the kitchen table and none of the scenarios land well. One of my roommates from undergrad said I should "just deal with it when it happens" and I wanted to throw my phone across the room. Easy to say when your parents own a condo downtown.

The worst part is I chose this apartment specifically because it was cheap and close and I knew the landlord and it felt locked in. I turned down a nicer place in Kensington two years ago because this one felt more predictable. And now the thing I picked for being steady might be the thing that falls out from under me.

Got the renewal notice for my car insurance today. It went up $40 a month. I should call around and compare quotes but I've been putting it off because the semester starts next week and I don't have the bandwidth to sit on hold with insurance companies.

Told myself I'd do it over the break and now the break is basically gone. The payment comes out on the 15th so I'll probably just let it auto-renew and deal with it later. It's not a huge amount but it's another thing eroding the margin I built into my budget.

Nudge: "What usually happens when you push stuff like this down the road?"

Response: I mean it's forty bucks. It's not going to ruin me. I'll get to it in February when things calm down.

My advisor floated the idea of submitting our paper to a conference in Chicago in April. Which is great for the CV, sure, but that means registration fees, flights, hotel. He said the department might cover part of it but "might" is doing a lot of work in that sentence. I nodded and said sounds good and now I'm sitting here wondering why I didn't just ask what "part of it" means.

The thing is I know I should go. Everyone says conferences are how you build a network and it would be my first one. But dropping $800-1,200 on a maybe-partially-funded trip when my housing situation is already shaky feels like standing on a wobbly table and deciding to juggle. I told myself I'd look into it tomorrow but I know that means I'll look at the conference website, get stressed about the costs, and close the tab.

Nudge: "What would it take for you to actually ask him about the funding?"

Doug — the landlord — stopped me in the hallway again. He said he's "had some conversations" with a developer. Not a done deal, his words. But the fact that there are conversations at all means the clock is ticking on something I can't control. I asked if he had a timeline and he shrugged and said these things take months. Months is not a timeline. Months is a fog.

I spent the evening going through rental listings on Kijiji and Facebook Marketplace. Everything in my price range is either a room in a shared house with strangers or a studio above a convenience store forty minutes from campus by transit. The places that are actually livable are all $1,700 and up. I made a spreadsheet comparing options but halfway through I just stopped because none of them felt like real options. They felt like damage control.

My mom called and I didn't tell her about any of it. She worries and then she offers money she doesn't really have and then I feel worse. I said school was going fine and we talked about my cousin's wedding. Hung up and sat in the kitchen doing nothing for a while. I keep trying to plan my way through this but there's nothing to plan around yet. It's just this low hum of something coming and I can't pin it down enough to solve it.

Submitted my thesis chapter draft to my advisor today. Two months ahead of the internal deadline. He seemed surprised, said most students don't get chapter two in until March. I don't know, I just can't function when something is hanging over me unfinished so I blocked out time every morning since November and chipped away at it.

Felt good walking out of his office. Like at least this one thing is where it's supposed to be. The housing stuff is still a mess and the conference money is still a question mark but the thesis is the one part of my life right now where I set a plan and the plan actually held.

Nudge: "What does it feel like when a plan actually holds?"

Response: I mean it feels normal. That's kind of the whole point, it's not supposed to be remarkable that things go the way you set them up to go. I just wish more things worked like that.

My friend Priya asked if I wanted to move in with her and her partner when their lease comes up in May. They're looking at a two-bedroom near Bloor and Ossington and they need a third person for the den. Rent would be $1,300, which is more than I pay now but way less than anything I've been finding on my own. And I know Priya, I've known her since first year, she's consistent. She pays bills on time, she's quiet, she doesn't bring chaos into shared spaces.

I spent the evening going over my budget to see if $1,300 works. It's tight but it works if I keep the TA position next semester, which my advisor basically confirmed. I'd lose my parking spot but I barely drive in winter anyway. The neighborhood is farther from campus but it's on the subway line, door to door maybe thirty-five minutes. I wrote out the pros and cons and the cons are all minor inconveniences. The pros are that I'd know where I'm living in June and I could stop refreshing Kijiji at midnight.

I told Priya I'm in. She sent a thumbs up emoji and said she'd send me the lease details this weekend. It's not the same as having my own place, and it's not the cheap locked-in situation I had before. But I can see the shape of it. I can budget around it. And right now that's enough to let me actually sleep.

Nudge: "Is it actually enough, or is it just better than not knowing?"

entries.journal_entry:
Vikram got promoted to Staff Engineer today. Everyone's congratulating him on Slack, and yes, fine, he deserved it. But let's be honest—he's been here six months longer than me and his scope was smaller until Q2. I'm not bitter. I'm just aware of where I stand. My manager keeps telling me my time will come, but I've heard that before at my last company. You have to make your time come.

Called Appa tonight. He asked about work, and I told him everything was progressing well. No point worrying him with the details. He mentioned the temple renovation fund and I sent extra this month. These things matter. You take care of your family, you do what's expected—that's not a burden, that's just how it should be.

Nudge: "What would 'making your time come' actually look like?"

Response: Visibility. I need to be leading cross-functional initiatives, not just delivering my own sprint work. I've already started attending product roadmap meetings even when I'm not required. When the reorg happens in Q4, I want my name to come up naturally for the platform team lead role.
Priya asked when I'm taking her to meet my parents. I said after Diwali, maybe. We both know I'm stalling but she didn't push.

Three nights of on-call rotations. The junior devs can't seem to handle basic incident triage without escalating to me. I don't mind being the one they depend on, but I need sleep.

Nudge: "Is there a difference between being needed and being indispensable?"

Response: Indispensable means they can't function without you. Needed just means they prefer having you around. I'd rather be indispensable, even if it costs me sleep. Nobody promotes the guy who's easily replaced.

Quarterly review feedback came in. My manager said I have strong technical skills but need to demonstrate more "executive presence." I asked what that meant specifically. She said it's about how I communicate upward—being more concise in leadership meetings, framing problems in terms of business impact rather than technical detail.

I've always believed that doing excellent work should speak for itself. That's what my father taught me—results matter, not showmanship. But apparently that's not how it works here. The people who get ahead are the ones who know how to package themselves.

Spent the evening reading articles about executive communication. Downloaded a book on stakeholder management. I don't like that I have to play this game, but I've never failed at something I committed to learning. If this is what they want, I'll figure it out.

What frustrates me is that nobody told me the rules changed. At IIT, you studied hard, you got the marks, you got the placement. Clear metrics. Here it's all subjective—"presence," "influence," "visibility." How do you measure that? How do you know if you're winning?

Nudge: "What would it mean if excellent work alone was never enough?"

Response: Then everything I was taught about how success works would be wrong. My father worked for the same company for thirty years and retired as a senior manager—never played politics, just delivered results. But maybe that's why he stayed senior manager. Maybe there was a ceiling I didn't see. I don't want that ceiling.

Presented the Q3 roadmap to the leadership team today. Kept it to seven slides, used business metrics instead of technical specs. VP nodded twice. Small win.

Nudge: "How did it feel to communicate that way?"

Priya's friends are getting engaged left and right. She showed me photos from another mehendi last night and I could feel her watching for my reaction. I said the bride looked happy. That's not the response she wanted.

It's not that I don't want to marry her. I do. But there's an order to these things. My parents expect me to be settled—flat, savings, stable position—before I take that step. Amma specifically said not to repeat what her brother did, rushing into marriage without foundation and then struggling for years. I'm building the foundation. Priya should understand that this is for us, not against us.

My cousin Karthik called to announce he's getting married. He's two years younger than me, works at an MNC doing standard IT support, lives in a rented flat in Coimbatore. And yet he's the one Amma spent twenty minutes congratulating on the phone tonight. "Such good news, beta! When will we hear from you?"

I'm the one who sends money home. I'm the one who got into IIT, who moved to Bangalore for better opportunities, who's actually building something. Karthik posts cricket memes and calls it a day. But because he's checking the marriage box, suddenly he's the responsible one?

I'm not jealous of Karthik. His ceiling is that MNC job until retirement. I want more. But I can't help feeling like the family scoreboard doesn't track the things I'm actually achieving. It's all marriage, children, temple visits. The professional stuff—they're proud, sure, but they don't understand it. To them it's just "computer work."

What if I never make them understand? What if I keep climbing and the only thing they see is the boxes I haven't checked?

Nudge: "Whose approval matters more to you right now?"

The platform team lead role opened up officially. My manager submitted my name. I've been preparing all week—reviewing my contributions, documenting the initiatives I've led, practicing how to frame my scope expansion.

Interview is next Thursday. Five panelists including the CTO. I've never presented to him before. My manager said he values people who "think like owners"—whatever that means. I'm guessing it means speaking about company strategy as if you have personal stake in it. I can do that.

Called Priya to tell her. She was supportive but also asked if this meant more late nights. I said probably yes, at least initially. She went quiet. I told her once I get this role, things will stabilize. She said I said that about the senior engineer promotion too. She's not wrong, but what does she expect? This is how you build something. You sacrifice now for position later.

Appa would understand. He always said the early years are for investment, the later years for enjoying the returns. I'm still investing.

Interview went well, I think. CTO asked how I'd handle a direct report who consistently missed deadlines. I said I'd have a direct conversation about expectations and consequences. He seemed to like that.

Nudge: "What's making you unsure about how it went?"

Response: It's not uncertainty, it's just being realistic. You never know what other candidates said or what factors matter to them. I did my part. Now I wait.

Got the role. Platform Team Lead. Officially starts August 15th. Vikram sent me a congratulatory message that was probably through gritted teeth. Doesn't matter. I'm ahead now.

Manager said the CTO specifically mentioned my clarity about accountability. Direct conversations about expectations and consequences—turns out that's exactly his management philosophy. Lucky guess, or maybe I just think the way leaders do. Either way, I'm in.

Nudge: "What does being 'ahead' give you?"

Called home to share the news. Appa congratulated me, asked what the new title means in terms of responsibilities. Explained about headcount, budget ownership, technical direction. He said it sounds like a lot of work. Then asked if this means I'll be too busy to come for Pongal.

I said of course I'll come for Pongal. I always come for Pongal.

Amma asked about Priya. I said we're doing well. She said Karthik's wedding is in November and the family will be asking questions. I said I know. She said maybe Priya could come for the wedding, meet everyone. I said I'd think about it.

After the call, I sat with this feeling I couldn't name. I achieved exactly what I set out to achieve six months ago. Platform Team Lead before 32. Higher salary, direct reports, visibility to the C-suite. But the call home felt like two different conversations—me trying to share a professional milestone, them trying to pull me back to the family timeline.

Maybe both things can be true. Maybe I can lead the platform team and also bring Priya to Karthik's wedding and also send money home and also make Pongal. But it feels like I'm running two races on parallel tracks, and I'm not sure if either one knows the other exists.

Nudge: "What would it look like if both tracks knew about each other?"

Response: I'd have to tell my parents what the promotion actually cost—the late nights, the stress, why I haven't proposed yet. And I'd have to tell Priya that family expectations aren't just background noise to me, they're load-bearing. I don't know if anyone wants that level of honesty. Maybe it's easier to keep the tracks separate.
